Manchester has a **guaranteed transition in the middle of every bit**, giving excellent clock recovery — at the cost of **doubling the bandwidth**. *(1.5 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Drawing only *text* waveforms without clear levels and time axis loses 1–2 marks. Always mark the **time axis** and the **voltage levels** (e.g., +V, 0, −V).
> - Confusing **NRZ-L with NRZ-I** is the most common single mistake. NRZ-L is **level-based**; NRZ-I is **transition-based** — the polarity of the level does not matter in NRZ-I.
> - A common error in Bipolar AMI: students alternate based on the *previous level that was actually used*, not based on the *count of 1-bits*. With a long run of 0s, the alternation must **continue the pattern**, not reset. The examiner specifically tests this.
> - Forgetting the **conclusion line** at the end of a 7-mark sub-part costs 0.5 to 1 mark. Always close with a synthesis sentence.

---

### **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative choice**

**(a) Define the terms bit rate, baud rate, and bandwidth efficiency. A channel has a bandwidth of 4 kHz. Find the maximum bit rate using (i) Nyquist formula (no noise) with 8 signal levels, and (ii) Shannon formula if the S/N ratio is 30 dB. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions (1.5 marks):**

- **Bit rate ($R_b$):** Number of data bits transmitted per second (bps). *(0.5 mark)*
- **Baud rate ($S$):** Number of signal elements (symbols) transmitted per second. With $L$ levels, $R_b = S \log_2 L$. *(0.5 mark)*
- **Bandwidth efficiency ($r$):** Number of bits transmitted per second per Hz of bandwidth, $r = R_b / B$ (units bps/Hz). *(0.5 mark)*

**Given:** $B = 4$ kHz, $L = 8$, S/N = 30 dB.

**(i) Nyquist (noiseless) capacity:**

$$C = 2 B \log_2 L = 2 \times 4\,000 \times \log_2 8 = 2 \times 4\,000 \times 3 = 24\,000 \text{ bps}$$

$$\boxed{C = 24 \text{ kbps}} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**Converting S/N from dB to linear scale:**

$$\frac{S}{N} = 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1\,000$$

**(ii) Shannon (noisy) capacity:**

$$C = B \log_2 \!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) = 4\,000 \times \log_2 (1 + 1\,000)$$

$$C \approx 4\,000 \times \log_2 1\,001 \approx 4\,000 \times 9.967 \approx 39\,870 \text{ bps}$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 39.87 \text{ kbps}} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**Bandwidth efficiency comparison:**

- Nyquist: $r = 24\,000 / 4\,000 = 6$ bps/Hz
- Shannon: $r \approx 39\,870 / 4\,000 \approx 9.97$ bps/Hz (theoretical upper bound, requires Gaussian codebook).

**Conclusion:** Shannon's bound is the *theoretical maximum* under the given S/N; the achievable rate is lower because practical codes cannot reach this bound. *(1 mark for conclusion)*

---

**(b) Derive the relationship between bit rate $R_b$ and signal rate $S$ for an $L$-level digital signal. A digital signal uses 16 levels to send data at 64 kbps. Calculate the signal rate and the minimum theoretical bandwidth if NRZ encoding is used. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Step 1: With $L$ distinct signal levels, the number of bits that can be encoded into one signal element (symbol) is, by definition of binary information content:

$$n = \log_2 L \quad \text{bits per symbol}$$

Step 2: If $S$ signal elements are transmitted per second, the bits transmitted per second are:

$$R_b = S \times n = S \times \log_2 L$$

Step 3: Solving for the signal rate:

$$\boxed{S = \frac{R_b}{\log_2 L}}$$

Step 4: For binary NRZ, the minimum theoretical bandwidth equals half the signal rate (the fundamental frequency of the worst-case alternating pattern is $S/2$):

$$B_{min} = \frac{S}{2} = \frac{R_b}{2 \log_2 L}$$

**Numerical application (3 marks):**

Given $L = 16$, $R_b = 64$ kbps:

$$\log_2 16 = 4 \text{ bits per symbol}$$

$$S = \frac{64\,000}{4} = 16\,000 \text{ Baud}$$

$$B_{min} = \frac{S}{2} = \frac{16\,000}{2} = 8\,000 \text{ Hz} = 8 \text{ kHz}$$

**Bandwidth efficiency:**

$$r = \frac{R_b}{B_{min}} = \frac{64\,000}{8\,000} = 8 \text{ bps/Hz}$$

**Conclusion:** 16-level signaling compresses 4 bits into each symbol, cutting the required bandwidth to a quarter of the equivalent binary NRZ (which would need 32 kHz for 64 kbps). This is precisely why high-speed Ethernet (10BASE-T, 100BASE-TX, 1000BASE-T) adopted **MLT-3 and PAM-5** multilevel encodings. *(1 mark for conclusion)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist — print and memorize before the exam:**

- **Digital signal =** discrete-time waveform with a **finite** number of amplitude values (typically 2: HIGH and LOW).
- **Bit interval** $T_b$ and **bit rate** $R_b$ are reciprocal: $R_b = 1/T_b$. **Units:** bps for $R_b$, seconds for $T_b$.
- **Signal rate (Baud)** is the number of signal elements per second, **not** the number of bits per second.
- The master relation is $R_b = S \log_2 L$. Solve for $S$ by dividing by $\log_2 L$.
- **Minimum theoretical bandwidth** for binary NRZ: $B_{min} = R_b / 2$. For RZ or Manchester: $B_{min} = R_b$.
- **Nyquist capacity** (noiseless): $C = 2B \log_2 L$. **Shannon capacity** (noisy): $C = B \log_2 (1 + S/N)$.
- Convert S/N from **dB to linear** by $S/N = 10^{(\text{dB}/10)}$.
- **DC component** is the average voltage of the waveform. AC-coupled channels and transformers **block DC**, so codes with strong DC (Unipolar NRZ) are unsuitable for them.
- **Clock recovery** needs regular signal transitions. Long runs of identical bits (NRZ-L) break clock recovery. Manchester and AMI guarantee transitions.
- **Bipolar AMI** alternates the polarity of 1-bits; 0-bits are at zero. Alternation is **state-based**, not count-based.
- **NRZ-I** is *transition-based*: a transition at the start of the bit interval means a 1, no transition means a 0. Polarity is irrelevant.
- **Differential Manchester** inverts at the **start** of a bit interval for bit 1 and **does not invert** for bit 0; there is always a transition in the middle.
- **Bandwidth efficiency** $r = R_b / B$ — higher means more bits squeezed into each Hz.
- **Digital transmission wins** over analog for: noise immunity, regenerability, error detection/correction, encryption, integration with digital hardware. Always quote **at least 3 of these 5** in a 7-mark question.
- The Shannon limit is the **theoretical upper bound** — actual systems are 30%–50% below it with practical codes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final 30-Second Sanity Check Before the Exam:**
> 1. Did you remember to **convert S/N from dB** when using Shannon's formula?
> 2. Did you **draw the time axis and voltage levels** on every waveform?
> 3. Did you state **units** (bps, Baud, Hz) on every numerical answer?
> 4. Did you avoid confusing **NRZ-L (level)** with **NRZ-I (transition)**?
> 5. Did you write a **conclusion line** at the end of every 7-mark sub-part?

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Impairments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET521 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transmission Impairments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Transmission Impairments — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🔷 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transmission Impairments** are the undesirable phenomena that distort, weaken, or corrupt a signal as it propagates from the transmitter to the receiver through a physical communication medium (copper wire, optical fiber, wireless channel). They are the primary cause of **Bit Error Rate (BER)** at the receiver, and their analysis forms the foundation of *Channel Capacity Theory* and *Reliable Data Link Design*.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (Outcome-Based Education framework, NEP 2020 aligned), the term is mapped directly to **Course Outcome CO1**: *"Describe the fundamental concepts of data communication, signal encoding, and transmission media."* Impairments are evaluated under the cognitive levels **L1 (Remember)** and **L2 (Understand)** in Module 1.

The three canonical impairments prescribed by the KTU syllabus are:

| # | Impairment | Physical Root Cause |
|---|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Attenuation** | Energy absorption by the medium (resistive loss, scattering) |
| 2 | **Distortion** | Non-uniform frequency response of the medium (amplitude & delay) |
| 3 | **Noise** | Random unwanted energy from internal/external sources |

---

## 🔷 1.2 Real-World Analogy — "The Megaphone Across a Stadium"

Imagine you are shouting a message through a long, narrow, echoing stadium tunnel to a friend standing **1 kilometre** away.

- Your friend's voice would be **quieter** because the air "absorbed" the sound energy → this is **Attenuation**.
- The high-pitched hiss of a treble instrument would fade faster than the bass, so the song would sound **muffled or warped** → this is **Distortion**.
- Random crowd chatter, a distant helicopter, and crackling loudspeakers would mix into the signal → this is **Noise**.

The result? Your friend hears a **weaker, distorted, noisy version** of what you said — exactly what a receiver experiences at the end of a transmission medium.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Engineering:** A network engineer must *quantify* each impairment so they can design amplifiers, equalizers, and error-correcting codes. Without this, digital bits would arrive corrupted and retransmissions would flood the network.

---

## 🔷 1.3 Why Transmission Impairments Cannot Be Eliminated

Every physical medium (twisted pair, coaxial cable, optical fiber, free space) is governed by **Maxwell's equations** and **thermodynamic noise laws (Nyquist's Theorem)**. These create hard, irreducible limits on the **maximum data rate (channel capacity)** that no engineering ingenuity can violate — only approach.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Hook:** Whenever a question asks *"Why is Shannon's Channel Capacity considered an absolute upper bound?"* — the answer is *Transmission Impairments* (specifically noise). This is a **favourite KTU 2-mark question**.

---

## 🔷 1.4 The Three Impairments at a Glance

### (a) Attenuation
The progressive loss of signal power as it travels through a medium. Measured in **decibels (dB)**.

### (b) Distortion
Two sub-forms:
- **Attenuation Distortion (Amplitude Distortion)** — different frequency components are attenuated by different amounts.
- **Delay Distortion (Phase Distortion)** — different frequency components travel at different velocities, arriving at different times.

### (c) Noise
Four canonical sources:
- **Thermal Noise** (Johnson–Nyquist noise from electron agitation)
- **Intermodulation Noise** (non-linear mixing of frequencies)
- **Crosstalk** (coupling from adjacent channels)
- **Impulse Noise** (spikes from lightning, switching, ESD)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔷 2.1 Impairment #1 — Attenuation

### Physical Origin
When a signal propagates through a conductor (e.g., copper wire), the **resistance of the medium** dissipates part of the signal energy as heat. For optical fiber, **Rayleigh scattering** and **material absorption** dominate. For free space (radio), **inverse-square law path loss** is the main cause.

### Mathematical Definition
Attenuation is defined as the ratio of output power to input power, expressed in **decibels (dB)**:

$$A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \quad \text{(dB)}$$

where:
- $P_1$ = input (transmitted) power
- $P_2$ = output (received) power
- $A_{dB}$ = attenuation in decibels (negative value means loss)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Rule:** If $P_2 < P_1$, then $A_{dB}$ is **negative** (e.g., $-3$ dB means half the power).

### Engineering Solution
- **Amplifiers** (analog signals)
- **Repeaters** (digital signals — regenerate the bit pattern)
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** determines how many amplifiers can be cascaded.

---

## 🔷 2.2 Impairment #2 — Distortion

### (a) Attenuation Distortion (Amplitude Distortion)
Every transmission medium has a finite **bandwidth**. Within this bandwidth, attenuation is approximately constant; outside, it rises sharply. The medium therefore acts like a **band-pass filter**, attenuating some frequency components more than others.

> [!EXAMPLE]
> In a typical voice-grade telephone channel (300 Hz – 3400 Hz), frequencies outside this range are attenuated severely. This is why music played through a phone sounds "tinny".

### (b) Delay Distortion (Phase Distortion)
The **propagation velocity** of a signal through a medium varies with frequency. The **phase velocity** $v_p = \omega / \beta$ is frequency-dependent because $\beta(\omega)$ is non-linear.

Result: different frequency components of the same bit arrive at the receiver at **different times**, smearing adjacent bits into each other — a phenomenon called **Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)**.

### Engineering Solution
- **Equalizers** — adaptive filters that compensate for the non-uniform frequency response.
- **Pulse shaping** techniques (raised-cosine, Nyquist filtering).

---

## 🔷 2.3 Impairment #3 — Noise

### (a) Thermal Noise (White Noise)
Caused by the **thermal agitation of electrons** in a conductor. It is **uniformly distributed across all frequencies** (hence "white"). Its power is given by **Nyquist's Theorem**:

$$N_{thermal} = kTB \quad \text{(Watts)}$$

where:
- $k$ = **Boltzmann's constant** = $1.3803 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- $T$ = absolute temperature in Kelvin
- $B$ = bandwidth in Hertz

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal noise is the irreducible floor** — it cannot be eliminated, only minimized by lowering $T$ or $B$.

### (b) Intermodulation Noise
Arises from **non-linearities** in the transmitter, receiver, or amplifying elements. When two frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$ pass through a non-linear device, they produce sum and difference frequencies $mf_1 \pm nf_2$, which appear as noise.

### (c) Crosstalk
**Unwanted coupling** between adjacent signal-carrying channels. Analogous to overhearing someone else's phone conversation on your own line. Common in tightly bundled twisted-pair cables.

### (d) Impulse Noise
**Sudden, high-amplitude, short-duration spikes** caused by external electromagnetic disturbances: lightning, switching relays, power line surges, electrostatic discharge (ESD). Major source of errors in digital communication.

---

## 🔷 2.4 The Unified Metric — Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)

SNR is the **master figure of merit** for any communication channel:

$$SNR = \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} \quad \text{(dimensionless, linear scale)}$$

In decibels:

$$SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right) \quad \text{(dB)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** Always use **dB** for SNR unless a derivation explicitly demands the linear form.

---

## 🔷 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these six formulas — they cover ≈ 90% of Module 1 numerical questions in KTU exams.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Attenuation (dB) | $A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_2 / P_1)$ | dB | Negative value $=$ power loss |
| 2 | Power Gain (dB) | $G_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_2 / P_1)$ | dB | Positive value $=$ amplification |
| 3 | Thermal Noise | $N = kTB$ | Watts | $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K |
| 4 | SNR (linear) | $SNR = P_{signal} / P_{noise}$ | ratio | Always $\geq 1$ for usable channel |
| 5 | SNR (dB) | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(SNR)$ | dB | $10 \log$ (not $20 \log$ — power ratio) |
| 6 | Nyquist Bit Rate | $C = 2B \log_2 M$ | bps | $M$ $=$ no. of signal levels |
| 7 | Shannon–Hartley | $C = B \log_2(1 + SNR)$ | bps | Absolute upper bound on $C$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU:** **"A P N S N N S"** — **A**ttenuation, **P**ower gain, **N**yquist noise, **S**NR linear, **N**oise in dB, **N**yquist rate, **S**hannon. Write this on the first page of your answer booklet.

---

## 🔷 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecom operators** use SNR to plan cell tower placement and link budget.
- **Optical fiber engineers** use $A_{dB}$ to compute the maximum unrepeatered span distance.
- **Satellite communication** designers use Shannon's formula to choose modulation and coding schemes.
- **Data center cabling** standards (Cat6, Cat7) specify maximum attenuation per 100 m to guarantee 10 Gbps Ethernet.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🔷 3.1 Derivation: Attenuation in Decibels from First Principles

**Goal:** Show why a factor of 10 in power corresponds to exactly **10 dB**, and a factor of 2 corresponds to approximately **3 dB**.

### Step 1 — Define the Bel (Logarithmic Unit)
Alexander Graham Bell defined the **Bel** as the logarithm (base 10) of a power ratio:

$$B = \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \quad \text{(Bels)}$$

### Step 2 — Define the Decibel (one-tenth of a Bel)
A Bel is a large step. For finer granularity, the **decibel (dB)** was adopted:

$$A_{dB} = 10 \cdot B = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Prove the 10× = 10 dB Identity
Let $P_2 = 10 P_1$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{dB} &= 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{10 P_1}{P_1}\right) \\
       &= 10 \log_{10}(10) \\
       &= 10 \times 1 \\
       &= 10 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Prove the 2× ≈ 3 dB Identity
Let $P_2 = 2 P_1$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{dB} &= 10 \log_{10}(2) \\
       &= 10 \times 0.30103 \\
       &\approx 3.0103 \text{ dB} \\
       &\approx 3 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Expected Answer:** "A 3 dB loss corresponds to a factor of 2 in power (half-power point)." This is a **favourite 2-mark short answer**.

---

## 🔷 3.2 Derivation: Thermal Noise Power (Nyquist's Theorem)

**Goal:** Derive the noise power in a resistor at temperature $T$ over bandwidth $B$.

### Step 1 — Statistical Mechanics Foundation
Each degree of freedom of a system in thermal equilibrium carries an average energy $\frac{1}{2} kT$ (equipartition theorem). For a 1-D transmission line, the modes (resonators) are quantized.

### Step 2 — Energy per Mode
For a single mode at frequency $f$, the average energy is $kT$ (considering both kinetic and potential).

### Step 3 — Number of Modes in Bandwidth B
The number of independent modes in a bandwidth $B$ is proportional to $B$:

$$N_{modes} \propto B$$

### Step 4 — Total Noise Power
Total noise power = energy per mode × number of modes:

$$N = kTB \quad \text{(Watts)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant of proportionality is exactly 1 (in appropriate units), making this one of the cleanest results in physics.

---

## 🔷 3.3 Derivation: Shannon–Hartley Channel Capacity

**Goal:** Show why $C = B \log_2(1 + SNR)$ is the **absolute upper bound** on bit rate.

### Step 1 — Information-Theoretic Argument
Claude Shannon (1948) showed that for a **noisy channel of bandwidth $B$ (Hz)** and **signal power $S$** with **noise power $N$**, the maximum data rate (in bits per second) that can be transmitted with **arbitrarily low error probability** is:

$$C = B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{(bps)}$$

### Step 2 — Dimensions Check
- $B$ is in Hz
- $SNR = S/N$ is dimensionless
- $\log_2$ is dimensionless
- $\Rightarrow C$ has units of bits/second ✓

### Step 3 — The Two Boundary Cases

**Case A — Infinite Bandwidth:**
As $B \to \infty$, using $\ln(1+x) \approx x$ for small $x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &\to B \cdot \frac{S/N}{\ln 2} \cdot \frac{1}{1} \\
  &\approx 1.44 \cdot \frac{S}{N_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $N_0 = N/B$ is the **noise spectral density** (W/Hz). So $C$ saturates — **infinite bandwidth gives finite capacity** when noise is white.

**Case B — Infinite SNR:**
As $SNR \to \infty$, $C \to B \log_2(SNR) \to \infty$ — but practical amplifiers cannot provide infinite power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The Shannon bound is **unreachable in practice** — real systems achieve 60–80% of Shannon capacity. The gap is called the *SNR gap* or *coding gain deficit*.

---

## 🔷 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A communication channel has a bandwidth of $4$ kHz. The signal power at the receiver is $0.4$ mW and the noise power is $0.04$ μW. Compute:
> (a) The signal-to-noise ratio in dB.
> (b) The Shannon channel capacity.
> (c) Compare with the Nyquist bit rate for a binary signal ($M = 2$).

### Solution

**Part (a) — SNR in dB**

Given:
- $P_{signal} = 0.4$ mW $= 4 \times 10^{-4}$ W
- $P_{noise} = 0.04$ μW $= 4 \times 10^{-8}$ W

$$
\begin{aligned}
SNR_{linear} &= \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 10^{-8}} = 10^{4} \\
SNR_{dB} &= 10 \log_{10}(10^{4}) = 10 \times 4 = 40 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Shannon Capacity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{Shannon} &= B \log_2(1 + SNR) \\
            &= 4000 \times \log_2(1 + 10000) \\
            &= 4000 \times \log_2(10001) \\
            &= 4000 \times 13.287 \\
            &= 53{,}149 \text{ bps} \\
            &\approx 53.15 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (c) — Nyquist Bit Rate ($M = 2$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{Nyquist} &= 2B \log_2 M \\
            &= 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2(2) \\
            &= 2 \times 4000 \times 1 \\
            &= 8000 \text{ bps} = 8 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison:** Shannon's bound (53.15 kbps) is **6.6× higher** than the binary Nyquist rate (8 kbps). This is because Nyquist assumes a noiseless channel — the gap is the **price of noise**.

---

## 🔷 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Impairment vs Countermeasure

| Impairment | Symptom | Engineering Countermeasure | KTU Module 1 Reference |
|------------|---------|----------------------------|-------------------------|
| Attenuation | Reduced amplitude | Amplifier, Repeater | Section 1.3 |
| Attenuation Distortion | Loss of high/low freqs | Equalizer, Loading coils | Section 1.4 |
| Delay Distortion | Pulse smearing (ISI) | Adaptive equalizer | Section 1.4 |
| Thermal Noise | Constant hiss | Lower temperature, narrow BW | Section 1.5 |
| Intermodulation Noise | Sum/diff frequencies | Linear amplifiers, filters | Section 1.5 |
| Crosstalk | Adjacent channel interference | Twisted pairs, shielding | Section 1.5 |
| Impulse Noise | Sudden spikes | Error-correcting codes (FEC) | Section 1.5 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🔷 4.1 Block Diagram — Communication System & Impairments

```mermaid
graph LR
    TX[TRANSMITTER] --> ENC[ENCODER]
    ENC --> CHAN[TRANSMISSION MEDIUM]
    CHAN --> ATT[ATTENUATION]
    CHAN --> DIS[DISTORTION]
    CHAN --> NOI[NOISE ADDED]
    ATT --> SUM[CORRUPTED SIGNAL]
    DIS --> SUM
    NOI --> SUM
    SUM --> DEC[RECEIVER]
    DEC --> OUT[OUTPUT BITSTREAM]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **transmission medium** is the single point where *all three impairments* enter the system. A KTU question may ask: *"Where in the communication chain is noise added?"* — Answer: inside the medium.

---

## 🔷 4.2 Hierarchical Classification of Impairments

```mermaid
graph TD
    TI[TRANSMISSION IMPAIRMENTS] --> A[ATTENUATION]
    TI --> D[DISTORTION]
    TI --> N[NOISE]
    A --> A1[ENERGY LOSS IN MEDIUM]
    A --> A2[MEASURED IN DECIBELS]
    D --> D1[ATTENUATION DISTORTION]
    D --> D2[DELAY DISTORTION]
    D1 --> D1A[UNEQUAL FREQ LOSS]
    D2 --> D2A[INTER SYMBOL INTERFERENCE]
    N --> N1[THERMAL NOISE]
    N --> N2[INTERMODULATION NOISE]
    N --> N3[CROSSTALK]
    N --> N4[IMPULSE NOISE]
    N1 --> N1A[ELECTRON AGITATION]
    N2 --> N2A[NON LINEAR DEVICES]
    N3 --> N3A[ADJACENT CHANNEL COUPLING]
    N4 --> N4A[SHARP SPIKES]
```

---

## 🔷 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The SNR Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    SP[SIGNAL POWER GENERATED BY TX] --> SPR[RECEIVED SIGNAL POWER AT RX]
    SP --> ATTBLOCK[ATTENUATION BLOCK]
    ATTBLOCK --> SPR
    NP[NOISE POWER FROM MEDIUM] --> NT[THERMAL NOISE]
    NP --> NI[IMPULSE NOISE]
    NP --> NC[CROSSTALK]
    NT --> TOTALN[TOTAL NOISE POWER]
    NI --> TOTALN
    NC --> TOTALN
    SPR --> RATIO[SNR RATIO P SIGNAL OVER P NOISE]
    TOTALN --> RATIO
    RATIO --> SNRDB[SNR IN DB]
    RATIO --> CAP[CHANNEL CAPACITY FROM SHANNON]
    CAP --> BER[BIT ERROR RATE]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to use this diagram in a KTU answer:** When a question asks *"How does attenuation affect the BER?"*, trace the chain **SP → ATTBLOCK → SPR → RATIO → CAP → BER**. This shows you understand the *cascade effect*.

---

## 🔷 4.4 Signal Power vs Distance — Attenuation Profile

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[INPUT POWER P1] -->|TRAVELS DISTANCE D| P2[RECEIVED POWER P2]
    P2 -->|LOSS A DB| LOSS[ATTENUATION A DB]
    LOSS -->|MEASURED| AMP[AMPLIFIER OR REPEATER]
    AMP --> RESTORED[RESTORED POWER]
    RESTORED -->|CONTINUES| NEXT[NEXT HOP]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📘 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 🔷 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between attenuation and attenuation distortion.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Attenuation | Attenuation Distortion |
|---------|-------------|------------------------|
| **Definition** | Loss of signal power as it travels through the medium | Unequal attenuation of different frequency components |
| **Cause** | Resistance of the medium | Non-uniform frequency response of the medium |
| **Effect** | Overall reduction in amplitude | Selective loss of certain frequencies, distorting signal shape |
| **Remedy** | Amplifiers / Repeaters | Equalizers, filters |

> [!NOTE]
> Attenuation is a *level* problem; attenuation distortion is a *spectral* problem. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
**List the four types of noise that affect data communication and state one cause for each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Thermal Noise** — Thermal agitation of electrons in conductors.
2. **Intermodulation Noise** — Non-linear behaviour of amplifiers and multiplexers.
3. **Crosstalk** — Electromagnetic coupling between adjacent signal-carrying wires.
4. **Impulse Noise** — Sudden spikes from lightning, switching, or electrostatic discharge.

---

## 🔷 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### 📌 Question A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the three main transmission impairments in detail, with diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A signal of power $100$ mW is transmitted through a cable. The received power is $0.1$ mW. Find the attenuation in dB. If a repeater is placed midway that amplifies the signal by $20$ dB, what is the final received power? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q1(a) — Three Impairments

**1. Attenuation** (≈ 2 marks)
- Progressive loss of signal power as it propagates through the medium.
- Measured in decibels: $A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_2 / P_1)$.
- Overcome by amplifiers or repeaters.

**2. Distortion** (≈ 2.5 marks)
- Two sub-types:
  - **Attenuation Distortion** — non-uniform frequency response of the channel.
  - **Delay Distortion** — different frequency components propagate at different velocities, causing inter-symbol interference.
- Overcome by equalizers.

**3. Noise** (≈ 2.5 marks)
- Random unwanted energy added to the signal.
- Four types: thermal, intermodulation, crosstalk, impulse.
- Overcome by shielding, filtering, error-correcting codes, and FEC.

---

#### Solution to Q1(b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 100$ mW (transmitted power)
- $P_2 = 0.1$ mW (received power)
- Repeater gain = $20$ dB (placed midway)

**Step 1 — Compute attenuation over the full link**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{dB} &= 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \\
       &= 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{0.1}{100}\right) \\
       &= 10 \log_{10}(10^{-3}) \\
       &= 10 \times (-3) \\
       &= -30 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Attenuation over each half-link**

Since the link is uniform and the repeater is at the midpoint, each half has an attenuation of $-15$ dB.

**Step 3 — Power at the repeater input (midpoint)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{mid} &= P_1 \times 10^{(-15/10)} \\
        &= 100 \times 10^{-1.5} \\
        &= 100 \times 0.0316 \\
        &\approx 3.16 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Power after the repeater (gain = +20 dB)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{after\_amp} &= P_{mid} \times 10^{(20/10)} \\
               &= 3.16 \times 10^{2} \\
               &= 316 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Power at final receiver (after second half, $-15$ dB)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{final} &= 316 \times 10^{(-15/10)} \\
          &= 316 \times 10^{-1.5} \\
          &= 316 \times 0.0316 \\
          &\approx 10 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Result:** The repeater has restored the power from $0.1$ mW (un-amplified) to $\approx 10$ mW (amplified) — a **100× improvement**.

---

### 📌 Question B `[KTU University Exam — May 2022]` — **INTERNAL CHOICE**

**(a)** Derive the Shannon–Hartley theorem for channel capacity. State clearly the meaning of each term and the two boundary cases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A voice-grade channel has a bandwidth of $3$ kHz and SNR of $30$ dB. Compute:
(i) the maximum theoretical bit rate,
(ii) the Nyquist bit rate for a 4-level signal ($M = 4$),
(iii) the noise power if the signal power is $10$ mW. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q2(a) — Shannon–Hartley Derivation

**Statement (2 marks):** The Shannon–Hartley theorem states that the maximum data rate $C$ (bps) that can be transmitted over a channel of bandwidth $B$ (Hz) with signal power $S$ and noise power $N$ is:

$$C = B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \text{ bps}$$

**Term Definitions (2 marks):**
- $C$ = channel capacity (bps)
- $B$ = bandwidth (Hz)
- $S$ = signal power (W)
- $N$ = noise power (W)
- $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio (linear)

**Information-Theoretic Argument (1 mark):** Shannon proved that *if the data rate is less than $C$, then codes exist that allow transmission with arbitrarily low error probability*. If the rate exceeds $C$, no coding scheme can achieve reliable communication.

**Boundary Cases (2 marks):**
- **Case 1:** As $B \to \infty$, $C \to 1.44 \cdot S/N_0$ (finite) — so adding infinite bandwidth yields finite capacity when noise is white.
- **Case 2:** As $SNR \to \infty$, $C \to \infty$ — but infinite power is unphysical.

---

#### Solution to Q2(b) — Numerical Computations

**Given:**
- $B = 3$ kHz $= 3000$ Hz
- $SNR_{dB} = 30$ dB
- $M = 4$ for part (ii)
- $P_{signal} = 10$ mW for part (iii)

**Step (i) — Shannon Maximum Bit Rate**

First, convert $SNR_{dB}$ to linear:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SNR_{linear} &= 10^{30/10} = 10^{3} = 1000
\end{aligned}
$$

Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{Shannon} &= 3000 \times \log_2(1 + 1000) \\
            &= 3000 \times \log_2(1001) \\
            &= 3000 \times 9.967 \\
            &\approx 29{,}902 \text{ bps} \\
            &\approx 29.9 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii) — Nyquist Bit Rate for $M = 4$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{Nyquist} &= 2B \log_2 M \\
            &= 2 \times 3000 \times \log_2(4) \\
            &= 2 \times 3000 \times 2 \\
            &= 12{,}000 \text{ bps} = 12 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison:** Shannon's bound (29.9 kbps) is **2.49× higher** than the 4-level Nyquist rate. The Nyquist formula assumes a noiseless channel — the gap is the noise penalty.

**Step (iii) — Noise Power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SNR_{linear} &= \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} \\
1000 &= \frac{10 \text{ mW}}{P_{noise}} \\
P_{noise} &= \frac{10 \text{ mW}}{1000} = 0.01 \text{ mW} = 10 \text{ μW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Answers:** (i) **29.9 kbps**, (ii) **12 kbps**, (iii) **10 μW**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Using $20 \log$ instead of $10 \log$ for power ratios:** This is the **#1 mistake** in KTU exam scripts. For power, always use $10 \log$. ($20 \log$ is for voltage/current ratios.)
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign for attenuation:** A loss is a *negative* dB value. Students often write "$+30$ dB attenuation" which is meaningless.
> 3. **Mixing linear SNR with dB SNR in the same calculation:** Always be consistent — convert at the start and stick to that form.
> 4. **Stating Shannon's bound is *achievable*:** It is an *upper bound*; no real system can equal it exactly, but codes can approach it.
> 5. **Confusing $B$ (bandwidth) with $f$ (frequency):** In Shannon's formula, $B$ is the channel bandwidth in Hz, **not** the carrier frequency.
> 6. **Omitting units in numerical answers:** A 14-mark numerical problem without units will be docked **at least 1 mark** by the KTU examiner.

---

## 🔷 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Transmission Impairments: What You MUST Remember**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- [x] Three impairments: **A**ttenuation, **D**istortion, **N**oise.
- [x] Attenuation is measured in **dB** using $10 \log_{10}(P_2/P_1)$.
- [x] Distortion has two flavours: **amplitude** and **delay**.
- [x] Noise has four types: **thermal, intermodulation, crosstalk, impulse**.
- [x] Thermal noise power: $N = kTB$ (irreducible floor).
- [x] SNR linear = $P_S / P_N$; SNR dB = $10 \log_{10}(SNR_{linear})$.
- [x] Nyquist bit rate (noiseless): $C = 2B \log_2 M$.
- [x] Shannon capacity (noisy, absolute bound): $C = B \log_2(1 + SNR)$.

### ✅ Numerical Must-Knows
- [x] $10 \log_{10}(2) \approx 3$ dB.
- [x] $10 \log_{10}(10) = 10$ dB.
- [x] A $3$ dB loss = half the power; a $3$ dB gain = twice the power.
- [x] Boltzmann constant $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.

### ✅ Exam Pattern Triggers
- [x] "Define attenuation" → mention dB formula + amplifier.
- [x] "Compare Nyquist and Shannon" → noiseless vs noisy; absolute bound.
- [x] "Types of noise with examples" → 4 types, one cause each.
- [x] "Why is Shannon bound unattainable?" → noise is random; perfect coding is impossible.
- [x] "What is delay distortion?" → different velocities → ISI.

### ✅ Common KTU Pitfalls
- [x] Don't use $20 \log$ for power.
- [x] Don't forget the **negative sign** for attenuation.
- [x] Don't claim Shannon's bound is *achievable*.
- [x] Always carry **units** in numerical answers.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Tip:** When answering KTU Module 1 questions, *always* start with the formula, then substitute the values step-by-step. Examiners reward **methodology over final number** — even if your arithmetic slips, the structured approach earns full or near-full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Measures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET521 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Performance Measures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Performance Measures in Data Communication?

In data communication networks, **Performance Measures** are a standardized set of quantitative metrics used to evaluate, compare, and optimize the efficiency, reliability, and speed of a communication system. They form the benchmark for designing physical media, protocols, and routing algorithms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Performance measures are the parameters that quantify *how well* a communication channel or network performs under given load and signal conditions. They include **Channel Capacity, Bandwidth, Throughput, Latency, Bit Error Rate (BER), and Jitter**.

### 1.2 The Six Pillars of Network Performance

| Pillar | Metric | KTU Exam Focus |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Speed | Bandwidth, Data Rate, Throughput | High |
| Delay | Latency, Propagation Delay | Very High |
| Reliability | BER, SNR | High |
| Efficiency | Bandwidth Utilization, Protocol Overhead | Medium |
| Capacity | Nyquist, Shannon Theorems | Very High |
| Variability | Jitter | Medium |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Highway System

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Pump — Think of a Data Network as a Highway:**
> - **Bandwidth** → Number of lanes on the highway (maximum capacity of the road).
> - **Data Rate (Bit Rate)** → Number of cars passing a point per second.
> - **Throughput** → Number of cars that *actually* reach the destination per second (accounts for traffic jams, accidents).
> - **Latency (Delay)** → Time taken for one car to travel from city A to city B.
> - **Propagation Delay** → Fixed time based on the speed limit and distance (medium-dependent).
> - **Transmission Delay** → Time to push all cars of a convoy onto the highway (proportional to packet size).
> - **Jitter** → Variation in arrival times of cars in a convoy (uneven gaps).
> - **BER** → Cars that arrive damaged or in the wrong lane.
> - **SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio)** → Clarity of the highway signboards versus honking noise around you.

### 1.4 Why Performance Measures Matter in Engineering

- **Network Design**: Engineers use Shannon's and Nyquist's theorems to size links.
- **QoS (Quality of Service)**: VoIP, video streaming, and gaming demand strict latency and jitter bounds.
- **Capacity Planning**: ISPs use these metrics to upgrade infrastructure.
- **Protocol Tuning**: TCP window sizing depends on **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)**.
- **SLAs (Service Level Agreements)**: Contracts between ISPs and customers are written in terms of these exact metrics.

### 1.5 Important Standard Metrics (Memorize These)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Units Used in KTU Exams:**
> - Bandwidth ($B$) → **Hertz (Hz)**
> - Data Rate / Bit Rate ($R$ or $C$) → **bits per second (bps)**
> - Latency / Delay ($T$) → **seconds (s)**
> - Distance ($d$) → **meters (m)**
> - Propagation Speed ($s$) → **meters/second (m/s)**
> - SNR → dimensionless (in dB when $10 \log_{10} (S/N)$ is used)
> - BER → dimensionless ratio (errors/bits)
> - Speed of light in vacuum → $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
> - Speed of light in fiber/glass → $\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s
> - Speed of electrical signal in copper → $\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bandwidth ($B$)

**Definition:** Bandwidth is the range of frequencies that a medium can pass without significant attenuation. It is the *capacity of the analog channel*.

- **Why it matters:** A higher bandwidth allows more data to be transmitted per unit time.
- **Two contexts (often confused in exams):**
  1. *Analog bandwidth* → measured in **Hz** (frequency range).
  2. *Digital bandwidth* → measured in **bps** (data rate). Many textbooks use these interchangeably.

### 2.2 Data Rate / Bit Rate ($R$)

**Definition:** The number of bits transmitted per unit time, measured in **bits per second (bps)**.

- Depends on: bandwidth, signal levels, and noise.
- Higher data rate = faster communication but more susceptible to noise (inter-symbol interference).

### 2.3 Throughput

**Definition:** The *actual* rate of successful data delivery over a communication channel, after accounting for protocol overhead, errors, retransmissions, and queuing.

$$\text{Throughput} \leq \text{Bandwidth}$$

- **Why less than bandwidth?** Control frames, ACKs, collision back-offs, and header bytes consume channel capacity.

### 2.4 Latency / Delay — The Four Components

Total latency in a packet-switched network has **four sub-delays**:

$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{proc}} + T_{\text{queue}}$$

| Delay Type | Formula | Depends On |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Propagation Delay** $T_{\text{prop}}$ | $\dfrac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Propagation Speed}}$ | Medium, distance |
| **Transmission Delay** $T_{\text{trans}}$ | $\dfrac{\text{Packet Size}}{\text{Bandwidth}}$ | Packet size, link capacity |
| **Processing Delay** $T_{\text{proc}}$ | (Router-dependent) | Routers, error checks |
| **Queuing Delay** $T_{\text{queue}}$ | (Traffic-dependent) | Congestion, buffer size |

### 2.5 Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)

**Definition:** The amount of data that can be "in flight" (unacknowledged) on a link at any instant.

$$\text{BDP} = \text{Bandwidth} \times \text{Propagation Delay} = B \times T_{\text{prop}}$$

- **Why critical?** It dictates the **TCP window size** required to fully utilize the link. If the window is smaller than BDP, the sender is idle waiting for ACKs.

### 2.6 Nyquist Theorem (Noiseless Channel)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For a NOISELESS channel**, the maximum data rate is:
> $$C = 2B \log_2 M \quad \text{(bits/second)}$$
> where $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $M$ = number of discrete signal levels.

- Doubling $B$ doubles the capacity.
- Doubling $M$ increases capacity logarithmically.

### 2.7 Shannon's Theorem (Noisy Channel)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For a NOISY channel**, the maximum data rate (theoretical) is:
> $$C = B \log_2 (1 + \text{SNR}) \quad \text{(bits/second)}$$
> where SNR = $S/N$ (signal power / noise power).

- This is the **absolute upper limit** — no coding scheme can exceed it.
- SNR is often given in dB: $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(\text{SNR})$.

### 2.8 Bit Error Rate (BER)

$$\text{BER} = \frac{\text{Number of bit errors}}{\text{Total number of bits transmitted}}$$

- Lower BER = more reliable channel.
- For wired media, BER $\approx 10^{-9}$ to $10^{-12}$.
- For wireless, BER $\approx 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$.

### 2.9 Jitter

**Definition:** The variation in packet arrival times at the receiver.

- Critical for real-time applications (VoIP, video conferencing).
- Caused by queuing delays that vary with network load.

### 2.10 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Propagation Delay | $T_p = d / v$ | seconds | $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in cable |
| 2 | Transmission Delay | $T_t = L / R$ | seconds | $L$ = packet length (bits), $R$ = bandwidth (bps) |
| 3 | Total Latency | $T = T_p + T_t + T_{proc} + T_q$ | seconds | Sum of all 4 delays |
| 4 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $\text{BDP} = R \times T_p$ | bits | Min. bits in flight |
| 5 | Nyquist Capacity | $C = 2B \log_2 M$ | bps | Noiseless channel |
| 6 | Shannon Capacity | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | bps | Noisy channel (upper bound) |
| 7 | SNR in dB | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$ | dB | Always check unit |
| 8 | Throughput | $T_p \leq R$ | bps | Actual delivery rate |
| 9 | BER | $\text{BER} = \text{errors} / \text{bits}$ | dimensionless | Quality measure |
| 10 | Utilization | $U = T_t / (T_t + 2T_p)$ | ratio $\vert$ fraction | Stop-and-Wait protocol |

### 2.11 Engineering Utility

- **Internet Backbone Design:** Shannon's theorem sets the link capacity ceiling.
- **Satellite Communications:** Propagation delay is huge ($\approx 270$ ms one-way), so BDP is enormous.
- **5G/Optical Fiber:** Engineers push $B$ to terahertz ranges to maximize $C$.
- **Network Simulators (NS2/NS3):** All these metrics are the primary output parameters for validation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship between Bandwidth, Data Rate, and Signal Levels

**Starting Point:** A signal with bandwidth $B$ can be represented by at most $2B$ independent samples per second (Nyquist Sampling Theorem).

**Step 1:** Each sample can carry one of $M$ discrete amplitude levels.

**Step 2:** The information per sample is $\log_2 M$ bits.

**Step 3:** Therefore, bits per second = (samples per second) $\times$ (bits per sample):

$$C = (2B) \times (\log_2 M)$$

$$\boxed{C = 2B \log_2 M \quad \text{(Nyquist Bit Rate)}}$$

**Real-World Engineering Insight:** If we increase $M$ (signal levels), we need a higher SNR to distinguish them reliably — this is where Shannon's theorem comes in to cap $M$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Shannon's Channel Capacity

**Starting Premise:** A noisy channel of bandwidth $B$ (Hz) with additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN) can be modeled as a continuous channel.

**Step 1:** The channel can distinguish $2B$ independent signal levels per second (sampling theorem).

**Step 2:** With signal power $S$ and noise power $N$, the number of distinguishable levels per sample is $\sqrt{1 + S/N}$ (from the geometry of the noise sphere in signal space).

**Step 3:** Bits per sample = $\log_2(\sqrt{1 + S/N}) = \frac{1}{2} \log_2(1 + S/N)$.

**Step 4:** Multiply by samples per second $(2B)$:

$$C = 2B \times \frac{1}{2} \log_2(1 + S/N)$$

$$\boxed{C = B \log_2(1 + S/N) \quad \text{(Shannon-Hartley Theorem)}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: SNR in Decibels (Conversion Routine)

**Step 1:** SNR (linear) $= S / N$.

**Step 2:** Take base-10 logarithm: $\log_{10}(S/N)$.

**Step 3:** Multiply by 10 (for power ratios) to get decibels:

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Conversion back to linear:
$$\text{SNR} = 10^{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} / 10}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given SNR = 30 dB, find Shannon capacity for $B = 3$ kHz:

$$\text{SNR (linear)} = 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000$$

$$C = 3000 \times \log_2(1 + 1000) = 3000 \times \log_2(1001)$$

$$C \approx 3000 \times 9.967 \approx 29{,}900 \text{ bps}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Propagation Delay

**Starting Premise:** A signal travels through a medium at a speed $v$ (m/s), depending on the medium's refractive index.

**Step 1:** Time = Distance / Speed.

$$T_p = \frac{d}{v}$$

**Step 2:** For fiber optic cable, $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s (i.e., $\frac{2}{3}c$).

**Step 3:** For a 1000 km link:
$$T_p = \frac{1000 \times 10^3}{2 \times 10^8} = 5 \text{ ms}$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)

**Starting Premise:** BDP = (rate of filling the pipe) $\times$ (time to traverse the pipe).

**Step 1:** Rate of filling = Bandwidth $B$ (bits/second).

**Step 2:** Time to traverse = Propagation Delay $T_p$ (seconds).

**Step 3:** Multiply:
$$\text{BDP} = B \times T_p = B \times \frac{d}{v}$$

**Units Check:** (bits/second) $\times$ (seconds) = bits. ✓

---

### 3.6 Derivation: Stop-and-Wait Protocol Utilization

**Scenario:** Sender transmits one frame, waits for ACK before sending the next.

**Step 1:** Time to send one frame: $T_t = L / R$.

**Step 2:** Time for signal to reach receiver and ACK to return: $2 T_p$.

**Step 3:** Total cycle time:
$$T_{\text{cycle}} = T_t + 2 T_p$$

**Step 4:** Utilization ($U$) = fraction of time the link is busy with the actual frame:
$$U = \frac{T_t}{T_t + 2 T_p}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $T_t = L / R$ and $T_p = d / v$:

$$U = \frac{L/R}{L/R + 2d/v}$$

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Theorem?

| Scenario | Use Nyquist? | Use Shannon? | Why? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Noise-free channel | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Shannon requires noise term |
| Noisy channel, need absolute max | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Shannon gives upper bound |
| Designing real modem | ✅ Yes (practical) | ✅ Yes (theoretical cap) | Both — Nyquist for design, Shannon as limit |
| Given only $B$ and $M$ | ✅ Yes | ❌ Cannot (no SNR) | Insufficient data for Shannon |
| Given only $B$ and SNR | ❌ Cannot (no $M$) | ✅ Yes | Use log-log formula |
| Voice-grade telephone line (4 kHz, 30 dB SNR) | ❌ | ✅ Yes | SNR explicitly given |
| Coaxial cable with no specified noise | ✅ Yes | ❌ | Assumed noiseless |

### 3.8 Combined Worked Problem (Full KTU Style)

**Problem:** A channel has bandwidth 4 kHz and SNR of 30 dB. Find:
(a) Maximum data rate (Shannon).
(b) Minimum signal levels required if the data rate is to be 56 kbps (Nyquist).

**Solution:**

**(a) Shannon Capacity:**
$$C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$$
$$\text{SNR (linear)} = 10^{30/10} = 1000$$
$$C = 4000 \times \log_2(1001) \approx 4000 \times 9.967 = 39{,}868 \text{ bps}$$

**(b) Nyquist Signal Levels:**
$$R = 2B \log_2 M$$
$$56{,}000 = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2 M$$
$$\log_2 M = 7$$
$$M = 2^7 = 128 \text{ levels}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Shannon says max $\approx$ 39.9 kbps, but the problem demands 56 kbps. This is **impossible** in this channel — 56 kbps exceeds Shannon's limit. The KTU examiner wants you to state this contradiction for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Taxonomy of Performance Measures

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[Performance Measures]
    root --> A[Speed Metrics]
    root --> B[Delay Metrics]
    root --> C[Reliability Metrics]
    root --> D[Capacity Metrics]
    root --> E[Variability Metrics]
    root --> F[Efficiency Metrics]

    A --> A1[Bandwidth B in Hz]
    A --> A2[Data Rate R in bps]
    A --> A3[Throughput in bps]

    B --> B1[Propagation Delay Tp]
    B --> B2[Transmission Delay Tt]
    B --> B3[Processing Delay Tproc]
    B --> B4[Queuing Delay Tq]
    B --> B5[Total Latency Ttotal]

    C --> C1[Bit Error Rate BER]
    C --> C2[Signal to Noise Ratio SNR]

    D --> D1[Nyquist C equals 2B log2M]
    D --> D2[Shannon C equals B log2 1 plus SNR]

    E --> E1[Jitter variation in delay]

    F --> F1[Bandwidth Delay Product BDP]
    F --> F2[Utilization U]
    F --> F3[Efficiency ratio]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Latency Breakdown in a Router Path

```mermaid
graph LR
    src[Source Host] -->|Tt Transmit| wireA[Link 1]
    wireA -->|Tp Propagate| r1[Router 1]
    r1 -->|Tproc Process| r1
    r1 -->|Tqueue Queue| r1
    r1 -->|Tt Transmit| wireB[Link 2]
    wireB -->|Tp Propagate| r2[Router 2]
    r2 -->|Tproc Process| r2
    r2 -->|Tqueue Queue| r2
    r2 -->|Tt Transmit| wireC[Link 3]
    wireC -->|Tp Propagate| dst[Destination]

    wireA -.- noteA[Tp equals d over v]
    wireB -.- noteB[Tt equals L over R]
    r1 -.- noteC[Sum of Tproc plus Tqueue]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Shannon vs Nyquist Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Given Channel Parameters] --> q1{Is noise specified?}
    q1 -- No SNR given --> nyq[Use Nyquist C equals 2B log2M]
    q1 -- SNR given --> q2{Max capacity OR practical design?}
    q2 -- Max capacity --> shan[Use Shannon C equals B log2 1 plus SNR]
    q2 -- Practical design --> both[Check Nyquist then verify with Shannon]
    nyq --> end1[Final Answer]
    shan --> end1
    both --> end1
    end1 --> check{Nyquist result greater than Shannon?}
    check -- Yes --> warn[Mark channel as infeasible]
    check -- No --> ok[Feasible design]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Communication System

```mermaid
graph TD
    src[Source Data Bit Stream] --> enc[Source Encoder Compression]
    enc --> chenc[Channel Encoder Adds Redundancy]
    chenc --> mod[Modulator Maps Bits to Signal Levels M]
    mod --> ch[Physical Channel B Hz Additive Noise N]
    ch --> demod[Demodulator Receives Noisy Signal]
    demod --> chdec[Channel Decoder Corrects Errors]
    chdec --> srdec[Source Decoder Decompresses]
    srdec --> dst[Destination Reconstructed Data]

    ch -.- snr[Monitor SNR at Receiver]
    snr -.- shannon[Shannon Bound C equals B log2 1 plus SNR]
    mod -.- nyq[Nyquist C equals 2B log2M]
    chdec -.- ber[Compute BER for Reliability]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Bandwidth-Delay Product Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    time0[Time 0 Sender Starts] --> time1[Time Tp First Bit Arrives]
    time1 --> time2[Time Tp plus L over R Last Bit Arrives]
    time2 --> bdp[Bits In Flight equals BDP equals R times Tp]

    pipe[Conceptual Pipe of Length Tp and Width R] -.- bdp

    bdp --> tcp[Implies TCP Window greater than or equal to BDP]
    tcp --> util[Full Link Utilization]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define bandwidth, throughput, and latency. State one difference between bandwidth and throughput.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Bandwidth:** The maximum data-carrying capacity of a channel, measured in **Hz** (analog) or **bps** (digital). It is a *theoretical* property of the medium.
- **Throughput:** The *actual* rate of successful data delivery, measured in bps. It is always less than or equal to bandwidth.
- **Latency (Delay):** The time taken for a single bit or packet to travel from source to destination, measured in seconds.
- **Difference:** Bandwidth is the *capacity* of the link; throughput is the *realized* data rate. Bandwidth is fixed by the medium; throughput varies with load, errors, and protocol overhead.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**A noiseless channel has a bandwidth of 4 kHz. How many signal levels are required to send 48 kbps using Nyquist's theorem?**

**Model Answer:**

Using Nyquist's theorem:
$$C = 2B \log_2 M$$
$$48{,}000 = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2 M$$
$$\log_2 M = 6$$
$$M = 2^6 = \mathbf{64 \text{ signal levels}}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Define the following performance metrics and state their units:
    (i) Propagation delay     (ii) Transmission delay     (iii) Bandwidth-Delay Product     (iv) Bit Error Rate **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A satellite link at $3 \times 10^8$ m/s covers a distance of 36,000 km (one-way). A packet of 4000 bits is sent at 1 Mbps. Calculate the propagation delay, transmission delay, and the BDP. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definitions:**

(i) **Propagation Delay ($T_p$):** The time taken for a single bit to travel from one end of the link to the other. 
$$T_p = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Propagation Speed}} \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

(ii) **Transmission Delay ($T_t$):** The time required to push all the bits of a packet onto the transmission medium.
$$T_t = \frac{\text{Packet Size (bits)}}{\text{Bandwidth (bps)}} \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

(iii) **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP):** The number of bits that can fill the link's "pipe" simultaneously.
$$\text{BDP} = \text{Bandwidth} \times \text{Propagation Delay} \quad \text{(bits)}$$

(iv) **Bit Error Rate (BER):** The ratio of bits received in error to the total number of bits transmitted.
$$\text{BER} = \frac{\text{Number of bit errors}}{\text{Total bits sent}} \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- Distance $d = 36{,}000$ km $= 36 \times 10^6$ m
- Speed $v = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- Packet size $L = 4000$ bits
- Bandwidth $R = 1$ Mbps $= 10^6$ bps

**Step 1: Propagation Delay**
$$T_p = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{36 \times 10^6}{3 \times 10^8} = 0.12 \text{ s} = 120 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2: Transmission Delay**
$$T_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{4000}{10^6} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 4 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3: Bandwidth-Delay Product**
$$\text{BDP} = R \times T_p = 10^6 \times 0.12 = 120{,}000 \text{ bits} = 120 \text{ kbits}$$

**Result Summary:**
- $T_p = 120$ ms
- $T_t = 4$ ms
- $\text{BDP} = 120{,}000$ bits

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For a satellite link, $T_p$ dominates. This is why TCP window sizes for satellite must be at least 120 kbits to fully utilize the link.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Alternative]`

**(a)** State and prove Shannon's channel capacity theorem. What is the significance of the SNR in this theorem? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A channel has a bandwidth of 3 kHz and an SNR of 40 dB. Calculate:
    (i) The theoretical maximum data rate using Shannon's theorem.
    (ii) The number of signal levels required to achieve this data rate using Nyquist's theorem. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Shannon's Theorem Statement and Proof:**

**Statement:** For a noisy channel of bandwidth $B$ (Hz) and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$, the maximum achievable data rate (channel capacity) is:
$$C = B \log_2(1 + S/N) \quad \text{bits/second}$$

**Proof Sketch:**

1. The sampling theorem allows the channel to carry $2B$ independent signal levels per second.
2. With additive Gaussian noise of power $N$, the number of *distinguishable* levels per sample is $\sqrt{1 + S/N}$ (from the geometry of noise spheres in N-dimensional signal space).
3. The information per sample is therefore $\log_2(\sqrt{1 + S/N}) = \frac{1}{2} \log_2(1 + S/N)$ bits.
4. Total capacity:
$$C = 2B \times \frac{1}{2} \log_2(1 + S/N) = B \log_2(1 + S/N) \quad \text{(proved)}$$

**Significance of SNR:** The SNR determines how much information can be reliably carried per Hz of bandwidth. Higher SNR → higher capacity. If SNR $\to 0$, $C \to 0$. If SNR $\to \infty$, $C \to \infty$ logarithmically.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $B = 3$ kHz $= 3000$ Hz, SNR = 40 dB.

**Step 1: Convert SNR from dB to linear**
$$\text{SNR (linear)} = 10^{40/10} = 10^4 = 10{,}000$$

**Step 2: Shannon Capacity**
$$C = B \log_2(1 + S/N) = 3000 \times \log_2(10{,}001)$$
$$C = 3000 \times \log_2(10{,}001)$$
$$\log_2(10{,}001) = \frac{\log_{10}(10{,}001)}{\log_{10}(2)} \approx \frac{4.00004}{0.3010} \approx 13.288$$
$$C = 3000 \times 13.288 \approx \mathbf{39{,}864 \text{ bps} \approx 39.86 \text{ kbps}}$$

**Step 3: Signal Levels using Nyquist's Theorem**
$$C = 2B \log_2 M$$
$$39{,}864 = 2 \times 3000 \times \log_2 M$$
$$\log_2 M = \frac{39{,}864}{6000} = 6.644$$
$$M = 2^{6.644} \approx \mathbf{100 \text{ levels}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** The result $M \approx 100$ is consistent with the SNR value — to distinguish 100 levels, the noise must be small compared to the spacing, which is true for SNR = 40 dB.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Mixing up Nyquist and Shannon.** Nyquist is for *noiseless* channels ($S/N$ ratio irrelevant). Shannon is for *noisy* channels and gives an *upper bound*.
> 2. **Unit errors:** $B$ in kHz and $C$ in Mbps — always convert units first. KTU examiners specifically deduct for inconsistent units.
> 3. **Confusing bandwidth (Hz) with data rate (bps):** They are related but not the same. $B$ is analog; $R$ is digital.
> 4. **Propagation speed in fiber:** Use $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s, NOT $3 \times 10^8$ m/s. Many students lose 1 mark here.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert dB to linear** before substituting into Shannon's formula. If SNR = 30 dB, you MUST compute $S/N = 1000$ first.
> 6. **Ignoring the "no solution" case:** If Nyquist demands $M$ levels but SNR cannot support them, state explicitly that the rate is unattainable.
> 7. **Forgetting SNR in Shannon:** If a question gives only $B$ and $M$, you CANNOT use Shannon — only Nyquist.
> 8. **Wrong TCP window:** For BDP questions, the answer is the window size in *bits*, not bytes (unless specified).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Performance Measures**

- **Bandwidth ($B$):** Frequency range of the channel; measured in **Hz**.
- **Data Rate ($R$ or $C$):** Bits per second transmitted; measured in **bps**.
- **Throughput:** *Actual* successful delivery rate; always $\leq R$.
- **Latency = $T_p + T_t + T_{proc} + T_q$.**
- **Propagation Delay $T_p = d / v$:** Distance-dependent; medium-dependent.
- **Transmission Delay $T_t = L / R$:** Packet-size-dependent; bandwidth-dependent.
- **BDP $= R \times T_p$:** Bits in flight; sets TCP window size.
- **Nyquist:** $C = 2B \log_2 M$ — *noiseless* channel.
- **Shannon:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — *noisy* channel; absolute upper bound.
- **SNR (dB) $= 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$;** always convert dB → linear first.
- **BER** = errors / bits; lower is better.
- **Jitter** = variation in latency; critical for VoIP and video.
- **If Nyquist result > Shannon bound → infeasible channel.**
- **Speed in copper/fiber** $\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s; in vacuum $= 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- **BDP units are bits**, not bytes (convert carefully).
- **Memorize the conversion:** $10 \log_{10}(1000) = 30$ dB; $10 \log_{10}(10000) = 40$ dB.
- **For long-distance/satellite links, propagation delay dominates.**
- **For local networks, transmission delay dominates.**
- **Throughput $\neq$ Bandwidth** — always distinguish in definitions.

---

**End of Module 1 — Performance Measures Notes**
*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET521 - DATA COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Transmission Modes ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Transmission Modes** (also called **Communication Modes** or **Data Flow Directions**) define the direction and manner in which data signals propagate between two communicating devices (a **Source/DTx** and a **Destination/DRx**) across a physical communication channel or link. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Data Communication, transmission modes are classified primarily based on **two orthogonal parameters**: 1. **Direction of Signal Flow** (Simplex, Half-Duplex, Full-Duplex) 2. **Number of Bits Transmitted Per Clock Cycle** (Serial, Parallel) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 1, the focus is on **Directional Modes** (Simplex, Half-Duplex, Full-Duplex). Serial vs Parallel is generally treated under Module 2/3, but the directional classification is the foundational gateway concept for understanding protocols like **UART, SPI, I²C, Ethernet, and TCP**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **narrow one-lane wooden bridge** connecting two villages, Village A and Village B: - **Simplex Mode:** The bridge is permanently barricaded on one end. Traffic can ONLY flow from Village A to Village B. A villager can shout news from A, but B cannot reply. Think of a **public TV broadcast** or a **radio station** — the TV station transmits, your TV only receives. - **Half-Duplex Mode (HDX):** The bridge is one-lane but two-way. A traffic light on both ends ensures only one village's traffic enters the bridge at a time. If A is crossing, B must wait. Think of a **walkie-talkie** or **CB radio** — you say *"Over"*, then the other person speaks. - **Full-Duplex Mode (FDX):** The bridge is replaced by a **two-lane highway**, or a **single-lane bridge with two separate tubes**. Village A and Village B can send traffic to each other *simultaneously* without collision. Think of a **mobile phone call** — both people can talk and listen at the same time. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in engineering:** The choice of transmission mode directly dictates the **bandwidth utilization**, **hardware cost** (number of wires/channels), **protocol overhead** (collision handling), and **latency** of any communication system. Selecting the wrong mode can halve the effective throughput of a network link. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Channel Capacity (C)** is measured in **bits per second (bps)** or **Hertz (Hz)** for analog. - **Propagation Delay ($\tau$)** is measured in **seconds (s)**. - **Standard Duplex Notation:** `10BASE-T` Ethernet defaults to **Full-Duplex**, while legacy **10BASE5** (Thicknet) coaxial was **Half-Duplex** with CSMA/CD. - **Spectral Efficiency:** Measured in **bits/second/Hz (bps/Hz)**. - **Minimum Wire Count:** Simplex = **1**, Half-Duplex = **1 or 2** (shared), Full-Duplex = **2 or 4** (separate channels). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Directional Transmission Modes — Structured Logic Breakdown #### **2.1.1 Simplex Mode (SX)** - **Direction:** Strictly **unidirectional** (one-way only). - **Hardware:** Requires **one communication channel** and **one dedicated transmitter** at the source, **one dedicated receiver** at the destination. - **Bandwidth Utilization:** The entire channel bandwidth $B$ is allocated to the source. The destination cannot echo or acknowledge via the same channel. - **Why it exists:** Maximizes channel capacity for one-way bulk data (e.g., telemetry from a satellite, sensor-to-base-station IoT uplink, television broadcast). - **How it works:** Source modulates a carrier with baseband data $\rightarrow$ channel $\rightarrow$ destination demodulates. The reverse physical path is **absent**. #### **2.1.2 Half-Duplex Mode (HDX)** - **Direction:** **Bi-directional**, but **not simultaneous**. - **Hardware:** Requires **one shared channel** (or a pair of wires) capable of being switched between transmit and receive modes. Both stations need both a transmitter and a receiver, but they share the medium. - **Bandwidth Utilization:** At any instant $t$, the full bandwidth $B$ is used by **only one** direction. Effective bidirectional throughput is therefore *time-shared*. - **Why it exists:** Cost-effective compromise when full-duplex hardware (separate channels) is too expensive, but two-way communication is mandatory (e.g., walkie-talkies, legacy Ethernet with CSMA/CD). - **How it works:** A **Media Access Control (MAC)** protocol (e.g., CSMA/CD, token passing) arbitrates turn-taking. A **Turn-Around Time ($T_{TA}$)** penalty is incurred each time the direction switches. #### **2.1.3 Full-Duplex Mode (FDX)** - **Direction:** **Bi-directional and simultaneous**. - **Hardware:** Requires **two physically separate channels** (e.g., two wire pairs, or two frequency bands via FDD, or time slots via TDD). - **Bandwidth Utilization:** The full bandwidth $B$ is used for **both directions concurrently**. Theoretical aggregate throughput can be **doubled** compared to half-duplex. - **Why it exists:** High-performance, low-latency bidirectional links (e.g., modern switched Ethernet, mobile telephony, fiber optic links). - **How it works:** Implemented via: - **Frequency Division Duplex (FDD):** Separate upstream and downstream frequency bands. - **Time Division Duplex (TDD):** Alternating time slots, but slots are interleaved finely enough to *appear* simultaneous. - **Separate Physical Pairs (e.g., 4-wire Ethernet).** ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula / Definition** | **Unit** | **Applicability** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Channel Capacity (Shannon) | $C$ | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bps | All modes | | Nyquist Bit Rate | $R$ | $R = 2B \log_2(M)$ | bps | Noiseless channel | | Simplex Throughput | $S_{SX}$ | $S_{SX} = C$ (one direction only) | bps | Unidirectional | | Half-Duplex Throughput | $S_{HDX}$ | $S_{HDX} = \dfrac{C \cdot T_{\text{data}}}{T_{\text{data}} + T_{TA} + T_{\text{col}}}$ | bps | Bi-directional, time-shared | | Full-Duplex Throughput | $S_{FDX}$ | $S_{FDX} = C_{\text{up}} + C_{\text{down}} = 2C$ (ideal) | bps | Bidirectional, simultaneous | | Turn-Around Time | $T_{TA}$ | Time to flip Tx/Rx switch | seconds (s) | Half-Duplex penalty | | Effective Utilization | $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{\text{data}}}{T_{\text{data}} + T_{TA}}$ | dimensionless (ratio) | HDX efficiency | | Total Round-Trip Delay | $T_{\text{RTT}}$ | $T_{\text{RTT}} = 2\tau + T_{\text{proc}}$ | s | Acknowledgment systems | | FDD Bandwidth Split | $B_{FDD}$ | $B_{FDD} = B_{\text{up}} + B_{\text{down}}$ | Hz | Full-Duplex | | TDD Frame Duration | $T_{\text{frame}}$ | $T_{\text{frame}} = T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}} + T_{\text{gp}}$ | s | Full-Duplex (TDD) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** In **5G NR (New Radio)**, the choice between TDD and FDD directly depends on traffic asymmetry. TDD is favored for **TDD-LTE** and **5G TDD** (sub-6 GHz) because uplink (sensors, IoT) and downlink (video, web) ratios are dynamic. FDD is preserved for voice-centric 4G LTE bands (e.g., Band 3, Band 7) where symmetry is required. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Simplex:** Satellite TV downlink (DTH), FM radio broadcast, GPS signal (space-to-earth), keyboard-to-computer input (without LEDs), telemetry from remote weather stations. - **Half-Duplex:** Walkie-talkies, citizen band (CB) radio, legacy 10BASE5/10BASE2 Ethernet with CSMA/CD, some industrial RS-485 multidrop networks, half-duplex UART. - **Full-Duplex:** Modern full-duplex Ethernet (100BASE-TX, 1000BASE-T), mobile phones (4G/5G), fiber optic links, USB 3.0, SPI (4-wire mode), TCP connections, telephone landlines. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Simplex vs Half-Duplex vs Full-Duplex | **Property** | **Simplex (SX)** | **Half-Duplex (HDX)** | **Full-Duplex (FDX)** | |---|---|---|---| | Direction of Data Flow | One-way only (A $\rightarrow$ B) | Two-way, alternating (A $\leftrightarrow$ B) | Two-way, simultaneous (A $\leftrightarrow$ B) | | Simultaneity | Not possible | Not possible | **Yes** | | Minimum Physical Channels | **1** | **1** (shared) or 2 (switched) | **2** (separate) | | Channel Bandwidth $B$ Allocation | 100% to one direction | 100% to active direction (time-shared) | 50% up, 50% down (or split by FDD/TDD) | | Peak Throughput per Direction | $C = B \log_2(1+\text{SNR})$ | $\frac{C}{2}$ (average, due to sharing) | $C$ per direction, $2C$ aggregate | | Hardware Cost | **Lowest** (1 Tx, 1 Rx) | **Medium** (1 Tx+Rx pair per station) | **Highest** (2 Tx, 2 Rx, or echo cancellers) | | Turn-Around Penalty | None | **Yes** — $T_{TA}$ incurred per switch | None | | Collision Possibility | **None** | **Yes** (if both transmit at once) | **Theoretically None** (with proper isolation) | | Latency for Ack | Cannot acknowledge | High (must wait for turn) | Low (immediate) | | Example Protocol | DVB-S, GPS, Keyboard UART | Walkie-Talkie, CSMA/CD Ethernet | TCP/IP, Mobile 4G/5G, Full-Duplex Ethernet | | Echo / Self-Interference | None | Low (shared medium helps) | **High** (requires echo cancellation) | ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Half-Duplex Efficiency with Turn-Around Time **Problem Setup:** A half-duplex link has a raw channel capacity of $C$ bps. A station transmits a data frame of $L$ bits, then waits for an Acknowledgment (ACK) of $L_{ack}$ bits from the other station. The Turn-Around Time per direction switch is $T_{TA}$ seconds, and the one-way propagation delay is $\tau$ seconds. **Goal:** Derive the **Effective Throughput** $S_{HDX}$ (in bps). --- **Step 1 — Frame Transmission Time** The time required to push the data frame of $L$ bits onto the wire at bit rate $C$ is: $$T_{\text{frame}} = \frac{L}{C}$$ **Step 2 — Turn-Around Delay** After the frame is fully transmitted, the sending station must **stop transmitting** and switch its hardware to receive mode. The receiving station must do the reverse. This is the **Turn-Around Time**: $$T_{TA}$$ **Step 3 — Propagation Delay** The signal (or the ACK) takes time $\tau$ to physically traverse the medium. After turn-around, the ACK travels back, incurring another $\tau$: $$T_{\text{prop\_total}} = 2\tau$$ **Step 4 — ACK Transmission Time** The ACK frame is $L_{ack}$ bits. Its transmission time is: $$T_{\text{ack}} = \frac{L_{ack}}{C}$$ *(In many simplified KTU problems, $L_{ack} \ll L$ and $T_{\text{ack}}$ is approximated as negligible, or absorbed into $T_{TA}$.)* **Step 5 — Total Cycle Time** The total time for one full data-exchange cycle is the sum of all the above components: $$T_{\text{cycle}} = T_{\text{frame}} + T_{TA} + T_{\text{prop\_total}} + T_{\text{ack}}$$ Substituting the expressions: $$T_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{L}{C} + T_{TA} + 2\tau + \frac{L_{ack}}{C}$$ **Step 6 — Effective Throughput Definition** Effective throughput is the number of *useful data bits* ($L$) delivered per *total cycle time*: $$S_{HDX} = \frac{L}{T_{\text{cycle}}}$$ Substituting $T_{\text{cycle}}$: $$S_{HDX} = \frac{L}{\frac{L}{C} + T_{TA} + 2\tau + \frac{L_{ack}}{C}}$$ **Step 7 — Simplified Form (Neglecting ACK and Combining Constants)** For KTU exam simplicity, define $T_{\text{idle}} = T_{TA} + 2\tau$ (the total *overhead time* per cycle where no useful data flows). Then: $$S_{HDX} = \frac{L}{\frac{L}{C} + T_{\text{idle}} + \frac{L_{ack}}{C}}$$ **Step 8 — Utilization Ratio (Efficiency $\eta$)** The channel utilization $\eta$ is the fraction of total cycle time spent actually transmitting useful payload: $$\eta = \frac{T_{\text{frame}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}} = \frac{\frac{L}{C}}{\frac{L}{C} + T_{\text{idle}} + \frac{L_{ack}}{C}}$$ This shows that as $L \rightarrow \infty$ (very large frames), $T_{\text{idle}}$ becomes negligible and $\eta \rightarrow 1$ (100% efficient). But for short frames, half-duplex suffers badly. --- ### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Full-Duplex Throughput Aggregation **Setup:** A full-duplex link uses **Frequency Division Duplex (FDD)**. The total allocated spectrum is $B_{total}$ Hz, split into an upstream band $B_{\text{up}}$ and a downstream band $B_{\text{down}}$. **Step 1 — Shannon Capacity per Direction** For an Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN) channel with Signal-to-Noise Ratio $\text{SNR}_{\text{up}}$ on the upstream band: $$C_{\text{up}} = B_{\text{up}} \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}_{\text{up}})$$ Similarly, for the downstream band: $$C_{\text{down}} = B_{\text{down}} \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}_{\text{down}})$$ **Step 2 — Aggregate Throughput** Since the two bands operate **simultaneously and independently**, the aggregate link throughput is the arithmetic sum: $$S_{FDX} = C_{\text{up}} + C_{\text{down}}$$ **Step 3 — Symmetric Case (Ideal KTU Exam Simplification)** In the standard symmetric assumption $B_{\text{up}} = B_{\text{down}} = \frac{B_{total}}{2}$ and $\text{SNR}_{\text{up}} = \text{SNR}_{\text{down}} = \text{SNR}$: $$S_{FDX} = 2 \cdot \frac{B_{total}}{2} \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$$ Simplifying: $$S_{FDX} = B_{total} \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$$ This is **exactly equal** to the Shannon capacity of the entire band used simplex-style. The key insight: **full-duplex does not double the Shannon capacity; it merely enables bidirectional symmetry without sacrificing per-direction throughput.** It removes the turn-around penalty, not the bandwidth limit. **Step 4 — Comparison** $$S_{SX} = B_{total} \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$$ $$S_{HDX, \text{per direction}} = \frac{B_{total}}{2} \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \cdot \eta$$ $$S_{FDX, \text{per direction}} = \frac{B_{total}}{2} \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$$ This proves the **full-duplex advantage**: each direction gets the *full* Shannon capacity of half the band, whereas half-duplex time-shares the whole band and additionally pays the $T_{TA}$ penalty. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Three Transmission Modes ```mermaid graph LR nodeA[Device A] -- Unidirectional Signal Only --> nodeB[Device B] nodeA -. No Return Path Exists .-> nodeB classDef simplex fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class nodeA,nodeB simplex ``` > **Figure 4.1 — Simplex Mode:** Solid arrow denotes the *only* allowed signal direction. The dotted reverse arrow is intentionally marked as "No Return Path" to emphasize unidirectionality. --- ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Half-Duplex with Turn-Around Arbitration ```mermaid graph TD devA[Station A
Has Tx and Rx] -- Direction 1 Active --> channel[Shared Channel] channel -- Direction 1 Active --> devB[Station B
Has Tx and Rx] devB -. Direction 2 After Turn Around .-> channel channel -. Direction 2 After Turn Around .-> devA arbiter[MAC Protocol
CSMA CD or Token] --- channel classDef device fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef medium fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class devA,devB device class channel,arbiter medium ``` > **Figure 4.2 — Half-Duplex Mode:** Solid arrows show the currently active direction; dashed arrows show the *other* permitted direction that becomes active only after a turn-around. The MAC arbiter governs the switching. --- ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Full-Duplex with Independent Simultaneous Channels ```mermaid graph LR subA[Station A Tx] -- Upstream Channel --> recvB[Station B Rx] subC[Station B Tx] -- Downstream Channel --> recvD[Station A Rx] subA --- recvD subC --- recvB classDef transmitter fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef receiver fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class subA,subC transmitter class recvB,recvD receiver ``` > **Figure 4.3 — Full-Duplex Mode:** Two physically isolated channels (Upstream and Downstream) operate in parallel. Each station has an independent transmitter and receiver chain, allowing truly simultaneous bidirectional communication. --- ### 4.4 Mermaid Decision Flowchart: Choosing the Right Transmission Mode ```mermaid flowchart TD start[New Communication Link Design] --> q1{Is two way data exchange required} q1 -- No --> simplex[Use Simplex Mode
Lowest cost, highest one way throughput] q1 -- Yes --> q2{Is simultaneous bidirectional traffic required} q2 -- No --> hdx[Use Half Duplex Mode
Single channel with MAC arbitration] q2 -- Yes --> fdx[Use Full Duplex Mode
Dual channels via FDD TDD or separate pairs] classDef decision fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B7500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef result fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class q1,q2 decision class simplex,hdx,fdx result ``` > **Figure 4.4 — Engineering Decision Tree:** A systematic flowchart for selecting the correct transmission mode based on traffic symmetry and bidirectional requirements. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Define Simplex, Half-Duplex, and Full-Duplex transmission modes. Give one real-world example for each.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Simplex Mode:** Communication is **unidirectional**. Data flows from the transmitter to the receiver only; the receiver cannot send data back over the same channel. *Example:* Television broadcasting (TV transmitter $\rightarrow$ TV set) or GPS satellite signals received by a smartphone. 2. **Half-Duplex Mode:** Communication is **bi-directional but not simultaneous**. Both stations can transmit and receive, but only one station can transmit at a time, with the other in receive mode. A turn-around time is required to switch directions. *Example:* Walkie-talkie communication, where the user says "over" to indicate the end of transmission. 3. **Full-Duplex Mode:** Communication is **bi-directional and simultaneous**. Both stations can transmit and receive at the same time using two separate physical or logical channels. *Example:* A mobile phone conversation over a 4G/5G cellular network, or full-duplex Ethernet. --- #### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **List the advantages and disadvantages of Half-Duplex over Full-Duplex transmission.** **Model Answer:** | **Advantages of Half-Duplex** | **Disadvantages of Half-Duplex** | |---|---| | **Lower cost:** Only one channel and one antenna/wire pair is needed, reducing hardware expenditure. | **Lower throughput:** Channel bandwidth is shared in time, halving the effective per-direction data rate. | | **Simpler design:** No need for echo cancellation circuits or FDD filters. | **Turn-around penalty:** $T_{TA}$ latency is incurred every time the direction switches, degrading performance for short frames. | | **Lower power consumption:** Single transmit/receive chain per station. | **Collision risk:** If both stations transmit simultaneously, packets collide and must be retransmitted (CSMA/CD). | | **Adequate for asymmetric, interactive traffic** (e.g., HTTP request/response). | **Not suitable for high-speed symmetric traffic** (e.g., voice, video calls). | --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **(a)** Explain the three transmission modes with neat block diagrams. Compare their channel utilization and give two real-world examples for each. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A half-duplex link has a channel capacity of $C = 1$ Mbps. The turn-around time is $T_{TA} = 50 \text{ \mu s}$, the one-way propagation delay is $\tau = 10 \text{ \mu s}$, and the ACK is negligible.
