# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example: Bit Stuffing in HDLC

> **Problem:** The sender needs to transmit the following bitstream (the contents of the Information field) over an HDLC link:
> 
> $$\text{Data} = 011011111110111110$$

Apply HDLC's bit-stuffing rule (insert a `0` after every five consecutive `1`s) and write out the **stuffed bitstream** that is actually placed on the wire.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Define the rule.**
Scan the bitstream from left to right. Maintain a counter $c$ of consecutive `1`s.
When $c$ reaches 5, append a `0` to the output and reset $c = 0$ (because the inserted `0` breaks the run).

**Step 2 — Process each input bit.**

| Step | Input Bit | Counter $c$ | Action | Output |
|------|-----------|-------------|--------|--------|
| 1 | `0` | 0 | Emit `0` | `0` |
| 2 | `1` | 1 | Emit `1` | `01` |
| 3 | `1` | 2 | Emit `1` | `011` |
| 4 | `0` | 0 | Emit `0` | `0110` |
| 5 | `1` | 1 | Emit `1` | `01101` |
| 6 | `1` | 2 | Emit `1` | `011011` |
| 7 | `1` | 3 | Emit `1` | `0110111` |
| 8 | `1` | 4 | Emit `1` | `01101111` |
| 9 | `1` | 5 | Emit `1`, then **insert `0`** | `0110111110` |
| 10 | `1` | 1 (reset by inserted 0) | Emit `1` | `01101111101` |
| 11 | `1` | 2 | Emit `1` | `011011111011` |
| 12 | `0` | 0 | Emit `0` | `0110111110110` |
| 13 | `1` | 1 | Emit `1` | `01101111101101` |
| 14 | `1` | 2 | Emit `1` | `011011111011011` |
| 15 | `1` | 3 | Emit `1` | `0110111110110111` |
| 16 | `1` | 4 | Emit `1` | `01101111101101111` |
| 17 | `1` | 5 | Emit `1`, then **insert `0`** | `0110111110110111110` |
| 18 | `0` | 0 | Emit `0` | `01101111101101111100` |

**Step 3 — Write the final stuffed bitstream.**

$$\boxed{\text{Stuffed} = 0110111110\mathbf{1}0110111110\mathbf{0}0}$$

**Step 4 — Verify uniqueness of the flag.**
Confirm that the pattern `01111110` does not appear anywhere in the stuffed stream. (The maximum run of `1`s is exactly 5, terminated by an inserted `0`.)

**Step 5 — Reverse process (Unstuffing) at the receiver.**
The receiver scans for the sequence `11111`. If the next bit is `0`, it is **discarded**; if the next bit is `1`, it is the start of the closing flag `01111110`. Applying this rule to the stuffed stream above recovers the original `011011111110111110`. ✓

## 3.2 Worked Example: Window and Sequence Number Logic

> **Problem:** HDLC uses a 3-bit sequence number. Two stations A and B are exchanging I-frames. Station A sends frames with N(S) = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Compute the next sequence number it can use, and the maximum sliding-window size.

### Solution

The sequence numbers are taken **modulo 8** (since 3 bits give $2^3 = 8$ states).

**Step 1 — Compute the next N(S).**
After N(S) = 5, the next number is:
$$N(S)_{\text{next}} = (5 + 1) \bmod 8 = 6$$

**Step 2 — Maximum window size.**
The maximum number of unacknowledged I-frames a station may send is:
$$W_{\max} = 2^3 - 1 = 7$$
(This is the standard "modulo-$2^k$" window limit to avoid ambiguity at the receiver.)

**Step 3 — General formula (extended HDLC).**
If 7-bit sequence numbers are used (extended control field), then:
$$W_{\max} = 2^7 - 1 = 127$$
$$N(S)_{\text{next}} = (N(S)_{\text{current}} + 1) \bmod 128$$

## 3.3 Worked Example: Decoding a Control Field

> **Problem:** An HDLC frame's control field is received as the bitstring `10010110`. Identify the frame type and interpret all fields.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify frame type from the leading bit.**
The first bit is `1`. This is **not** an I-frame (which starts with `0`).
The second bit is `0`. Therefore, this is an **S-frame** (Supervisory).

**Step 2 — Break down the bits.**

$$\underbrace{1}_{P} \; \underbrace{0}_{\text{type}} \; \underbrace{01}_{S_1 S_2} \; \underbrace{011}_{N(R)} \; \underbrace{0}_{P/F}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret each segment.**
- $S_1 S_2 = 01$ → **RNR (Receive Not Ready)**
- $N(R) = 011_2 = 3$ → Acknowledges receipt of frames up to and including N(S) = 2
- $P/F = 0$ → Not a poll or final bit

**Step 4 — Full English meaning.**
> *"I (the secondary) am temporarily not ready to accept further I-frames. I have successfully received all I-frames up to sequence number 2. The next I-frame I expect has N(S) = 3. This is **not** a final response."*

## 3.4 Derivation: Why is HDLC's Sliding Window $2^k - 1$ and not $2^k$?

Consider a 3-bit sequence number ($k=3$, range $0$ to $7$). Suppose the window were $W=8$ (i.e., the full range).

- Station A sends frames with N(S) = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7.
- All are lost due to channel error. A's timer expires.
- A retransmits the same 8 frames.
- The receiver, having received no correct frame, cannot distinguish between the **originals and the retransmissions** using the N(S) alone.

By restricting $W \le 2^k - 1$, the receiver can always tell a new frame from a duplicate by checking whether its N(S) lies within the expected window. Mathematically, the **ambiguity-free range** is:

$$W_{\max} = 2^k - 1 = 2^{n_{\text{seq}}} - 1$$

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HDLC Frame Structure (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1["Flag 01111110"] --> A["Address Field 8 bits"]
    A --> C["Control Field 8 or 16 bits"]
    C --> I["Information Field Variable n x 8 bits"]
    I --> FCS["FCS 16 or 32 bits CRC"]
    FCS --> F2["Flag 01111110"]
    F1 -.shared closing/opening.-> F2
```

**Interpretation:** The frame is a linear chain. The closing flag of one frame is the opening flag of the next (back-to-back transmission), saving 8 bits per frame pair in continuous streams.

## 4.2 Control Field Format (All Three Frame Types)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IF["I FRAME Information"]
        I0["0 bit"]
        I1["N S 3 bits send sequence"]
        I2["N R 3 bits receive sequence"]
        I3["P F 1 bit poll or final"]
        I0 --> I1 --> I2 --> I3
    end
    subgraph SF["S FRAME Supervisory"]
        S0["1 0"]
        S1["S1 S2 type code"]
        S2["N R 3 bits receive sequence"]
        S3["P F 1 bit"]
        S0 --> S1 --> S2 --> S3
    end
    subgraph UF["U FRAME Unnumbered"]
        U0["1 1"]
        U1["M1 M2 M3 M4 M5 modifier bits"]
        U2["P F 1 bit"]
        U0 --> U1 --> U2
    end
```

## 4.3 Bit Stuffing Flowchart (Sender Side)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Read next bit B from payload"] --> B{"Is B = 1"}
    B -- No --> C["Emit B and reset counter C to 0"] --> A
    B -- Yes --> D["Emit B and increment C"]
    D --> E{"C equals 5"}
    E -- No --> A
    E -- Yes --> F["Emit stuffed 0 bit on wire"] --> G["Reset C to 0"] --> A
    H["End of payload: add FCS and Flag"] --> Z["Done"]
    A -.all bits done.-> H
```

## 4.4 Frame Type Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start["HDLC Frame received"] --> Check1{"First bit of Control"}
    Check1 -- 0 --> Info["I Frame Information"]
    Check1 -- 1 --> Check2{"Second bit of Control"}
    Check2 -- 0 --> Sup["S Frame Supervisory"]
    Check2 -- 1 --> Un["U Frame Unnumbered"]
    Sup --> SupT{"S1 S2 bits"}
    SupT -- 00 --> RR["RR Receive Ready"]
    SupT -- 01 --> RNR["RNR Receive Not Ready"]
    SupT -- 10 --> REJ["REJ Reject Go Back N"]
    SupT -- 11 --> SREJ["SREJ Selective Reject"]
    Un --> UnT{"Modifier M1 M2 M3 M4 M5"}
    UnT --> Ulist["SABM SNRM DISC UA FRMR DM SABME etc"]
```

## 4.5 HDLC Mode Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Unbalanced["Unbalanced Configuration"]
        P["Primary Station"] --command--> S1["Secondary"]
        S1 --response--> P
    end
    subgraph Balanced["Balanced Configuration"]
        C1["Combined Station A"] <--> C2["Combined Station B"]
    end
    Unbalanced --NRM or ARM--> M1["Normal Response Mode or Async Response Mode"]
    Balanced --ABM--> M2["Asynchronous Balanced Mode"]
```

## 4.6 Data Communication Block Flow (Logical Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    APP["Application Layer Data"] --> ENC["HDLC Framing Module"]
    ENC --> STUFF["Bit Stufing Unit"]
    STUFF --> PHY["Physical Layer Encoder"]
    PHY --> LINE["Communication Channel"]
    LINE --> PHY2["Receiver Physical Layer"]
    PHY2 --> UNSTUFF["Bit Unstuffing Unit"]
    UNSTUFF --> FCSCHK["FCS Verification"]
    FCSCHK --OK--> DEC["HDLC De-Framing Module"]
    FCSCHK --Error--> DROP["Drop Frame and Request Retransmit"]
    DEC --> APP2["Application Layer Data at Receiver"]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Define HDLC. Mention any four features of HDLC.**

**Model Answer:**
**HDLC (High-Level Data Link Control)** is a **bit-oriented data link layer protocol** standardized by ISO (ISO 3309 and ISO 4335), derived from IBM's SDLC, used for reliable data transfer over point-to-point and multipoint links.

**Four Features:**
1. **Bit-oriented framing** — Operates on a continuous bitstream rather than fixed-size characters.
2. **Single flag pattern** (`01111110`) for both opening and closing delimiters, with **bit stuffing** for transparency.
3. **Full-duplex operation** is supported, along with both **point-to-point** and **multipoint** configurations.
4. **Built-in flow and error control** using sequence numbers, acknowledgements, and a sliding window mechanism. (3 marks)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**What is the purpose of bit stuffing in HDLC? Why is it required?**

**Model Answer:**
**Purpose:** Bit stuffing ensures that the **flag pattern `01111110`** (used as a frame delimiter) does **not accidentally appear** inside the Address, Control, Information, or FCS fields of a frame.

**How:** After every **five consecutive `1` bits** in the payload, the sender **inserts an extra `0` bit**. The receiver detects this stuffed `0` and **removes it** to recover the original data.

**Why required:** Without bit stuffing, the receiver could mistake a random bit pattern inside the data for a frame's closing flag, causing the frame to be incorrectly truncated. Bit stuffing thus provides **data transparency**. (3 marks)

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Choose ONE Option

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

#### (a) Explain the HDLC frame structure with a neat diagram. List the function of each field. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The HDLC frame consists of **six fields**, transmitted serially:

| Field | Size | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Flag** | 8 bits (`01111110`) | Marks the beginning and end of the frame. The closing flag may be shared with the opening flag of the next frame. |
| **Address** | 8+ bits (extendable) | Identifies the secondary station involved in the transmission. |
| **Control** | 8 or 16 bits | Specifies the frame type (I, S, or U), contains sequence numbers N(S) and N(R), and the P/F bit. |
| **Information** | Variable, multiple of 8 bits | Carries the user's payload. Present only in I-frames and certain U-frames. |
| **FCS** | 16 or 32 bits | A CRC checksum computed over A, C, and I fields. Used by the receiver to detect errors. |
| **Flag** | 8 bits | Closes the frame. |

**Diagram:**

```
+--------+--------+--------+----------+--------+--------+
|  Flag  |  Addr  |  Ctrl  |   Info   |  FCS   |  Flag  |
|01111110|  8 b   |  8/16b |  N x 8b  | 16/32b |01111110|
+--------+--------+--------+----------+--------+--------+
   F1        A        C          I         FCS      F2
```

**Key Points:**
- The control field is 8 bits in basic mode and 16 bits in extended mode (LSB = 0 indicates 8-bit format, LSB = 1 indicates extended).
- A single shared flag between back-to-back frames reduces overhead by 8 bits per frame pair.
- FCS is generated using CRC-CCITT ($x^{16} + x^{12} + x^5 + 1$) or CRC-32.

#### (b) Explain bit stuffing in HDLC with a suitable example. Given the data bitstream `1011111011111110110`, show the stuffed output and explain the unstuffing process at the receiver. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Bit Stuffing:**
To prevent the flag pattern `01111110` from appearing inside the frame, HDLC mandates that **after every five consecutive `1`s in the payload, a `0` bit is automatically inserted** by the sender. The receiver removes any `0` that follows exactly five `1`s.

**Worked Example — Sender Side:**

Input data: `1 0 1 1 1 1 1 0 1 1 1 1 1 1 1 0 1 1 0`

| Bit | Count of `1`s | Output (Stuffed) |
|-----|---------------|-------------------|
| 1 | 1 | `1` |
| 0 | 0 | `10` |
| 1 | 1 | `101` |
| 1 | 2 | `1011` |
| 1 | 3 | `10111` |
| 1 | 4 | `101111` |
| 1 | 5 | `1011111` **+ insert `0`** |
| 0 | 0 | `10111110` |
| 1 | 1 | `101111101` |
| 1 | 2 | `1011111011` |
| 1 | 3 | `10111110111` |
| 1 | 4 | `101111101111` |
| 1 | 5 | `1011111011111` **+ insert `0`** |
| 1 | 1 (after reset) | `101111101111101` |
| 1 | 2 | `1011111011111011` |
| 0 | 0 | `10111110111110110` |
| 1 | 1 | `101111101111101101` |
| 1 | 2 | `1011111011111011011` |
| 0 | 0 | `10111110111110110110` |

**Stuffed Output:**
$$\boxed{10111110\mathbf{0}111110\mathbf{0}110110}$$

(Note the inserted `0`s in bold; the original two 0s are unchanged.)

**Unstuffing Process at Receiver:**
1. Scan the incoming bitstream.
2. Count consecutive `1`s.
3. If the count reaches 5, inspect the **next** bit:
   - If it is `0` → it is a **stuffed bit**; **remove it** and reset the counter.
   - If it is `1` → the sequence `111111` indicates the **end of the flag** — proceed to frame processing.

**Verification:** Applying unstuffing to the above output recovers the original `1011111011111110110`. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Do not stuff inside the flag itself** — only Address/Control/Information/FCS are stuffed.
> 2. **Reset the counter after inserting the 0** — students often forget this and incorrectly count the inserted 0 as data.
> 3. **The receiver removes a `0` only if it directly follows five `1`s** — never a `1`.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### (a) Explain the three types of HDLC frames with their control field formats. Discuss the role of the P/F bit. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The **control field** identifies the frame type. The first bit(s) classify the frame:

**(i) Information Frame (I-frame):** Carries user data piggybacked with control information.
- Format: `0 | N(S) | N(R) | P/F`
- N(S) = 3-bit send sequence number
- N(R) = 3-bit receive sequence number (acknowledgement)
- Example: `0 101 011 1` = I-frame, N(S)=5, N(R)=3, P/F=1

**(ii) Supervisory Frame (S-frame):** Performs flow and error control functions; contains **no information field**.
- Format: `1 0 | S1 S2 | N(R) | P/F`
- Four types:
  - `00` — **RR** (Receive Ready): positive ACK; ready to receive next.
  - `01` — **RNR** (Receive Not Ready): busy; cannot accept more.
  - `10` — **REJ** (Reject): Go-Back-N — retransmit from N(R).
  - `11` — **SREJ** (Selective Reject): retransmit only N(R).

**(iii) Unnumbered Frame (U-frame):** Used for link management (no sequence numbers).
- Format: `1 1 | M1 M2 M3 M4 M5 | P/F`
- Examples: SABM (Set ABM), DISC (Disconnect), UA (Unnumbered Acknowledgement), FRMR (Frame Reject), DM (Disconnect Mode).

**Role of the P/F (Poll/Final) Bit:**
- When set by a **primary** station, it is a **Poll** — commanding the secondary to respond immediately.
- When set by a **secondary** station, it is a **Final** — indicating this is the last (or only) response frame to the poll.
- The meaning is **direction-dependent**; the same bit is interpreted differently based on which station sent the frame.

#### (b) Describe the three data transfer modes of HDLC and the two link configurations. How does the choice of mode affect the polling mechanism? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Two Link Configurations:**

1. **Unbalanced Configuration:** A single primary station connected to one or more secondary stations. Used with NRM and ARM.
2. **Balanced Configuration:** Two combined stations, each with equal control. Used with ABM.

**Three Data Transfer Modes:**

| Mode | Configuration | Polling Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **NRM (Normal Response Mode)** | Unbalanced | Secondary may transmit **only** after being polled (explicit permission). Suited for multipoint links with one primary and many secondaries (e.g., terminal-to-host). |
| **ARM (Asynchronous Response Mode)** | Unbalanced | Secondary may initiate transmission **at any time** without permission. The primary still polls, but the secondary need not wait. Used when the secondary has urgent data. |
| **ABM (Asynchronous Balanced Mode)** | Balanced | **Either** combined station may initiate transmission at any time. No polling required. Used in point-to-point full-duplex links (e.g., X.25, PPP). |

**Effect on Polling:**
- In **NRM**, polling is **mandatory and frequent** — the primary controls all channel access.
- In **ARM**, polling is **still used** (for synchronization) but secondaries are not blocked by it.
- In **ABM**, **polling is unnecessary** because both ends have equal transmission rights; the link is essentially contention-free in software.

**Real-World Use:**
- **NRM** — IBM's SNA terminal networks.
- **ABM** — Modern point-to-point leased lines, PPP, X.25 LAPB.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Part B):**
> 1. **S-frames do not carry an Information field** — a common error.
> 2. **P/F is the same bit but interpreted differently** based on whether the sender is primary or secondary.
> 3. **ABM is the most-used mode in modern networks** — examiners expect you to know this.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for HDLC (KTU Module 2)**

- ✅ **HDLC = ISO bit-oriented protocol**, derived from **SDLC**; standards **ISO 3309** (frame) and **ISO 4335** (procedures).
- ✅ **Six fields per frame:** Flag, Address, Control, Information, FCS, Flag.
- ✅ **Flag = `01111110` (0x7E)** — used at start and end; can be shared between back-to-back frames.
- ✅ **Bit stuffing rule:** Insert `0` after **five consecutive `1`s** in the payload; receiver removes the same.
- ✅ **Three station types:** Primary, Secondary, Combined.
- ✅ **Two configurations:** Unbalanced (P–S) and Balanced (C–C).
- ✅ **Three modes:** NRM (poll-driven), ARM (secondary-initiated), ABM (both can initiate, used in PPP/LAPB).
- ✅ **Three frame types:** I (data + ack), S (control only, no info field), U (link management).
- ✅ **S-frame types:** RR, RNR, REJ, SREJ (encoded in `S1 S2` bits).
- ✅ **P/F bit** is direction-dependent (Poll from primary, Final from secondary).
- ✅ **FCS uses CRC-16** ($x^{16}+x^{12}+x^5+1$) or **CRC-32**; computed over A, C, I fields only.
- ✅ **3-bit sequence numbers** → range $0$ to $7$ → **window size = $2^3 - 1 = 7$**.
- ✅ **HDLC variants in real life:** **PPP** (Internet dial-up), **LAPB** (X.25), **LAPD** (ISDN), **LAPF** (Frame Relay), and even **LTE MAC**.
- ✅ **Key advantage over BSC:** Bit-oriented, transparent to any data, full-duplex, efficient.
- ✅ **Receiver detects frame end** either by the closing flag or by an idle line (`>= 7` consecutive `1`s in some implementations).
- ✅ **A single shared flag** between two consecutive frames saves **8 bits of overhead** per frame pair.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Random and Controlled Access
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET521 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Random and Controlled Access

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Data Link Layer (DLL)** of the OSI/TCP-IP model, the **Medium Access Control (MAC) sublayer** governs how multiple devices (nodes/stations) share a common broadcast communication medium (such as a coaxial cable, radio frequency, or optical fiber) without destructive interference. When the medium is **shared** (multi-point/broadcast topology), deterministic arbitration is required to decide **"who transmits, and when."**

**Random and Controlled Access** are the two principal families of **Multiple Access Protocols (MAC Protocols)** that resolve this contention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> **Random Access Protocols** (also called *Contention-Based Protocols*) allow any station to transmit at any time, with no prior coordination. Collisions are permitted and are resolved by retransmission algorithms (e.g., ALOHA, Slotted ALOHA, CSMA, CSMA/CD, CSMA/CA).
>
> **Controlled Access Protocols** (also called *Deterministic Protocols*) ensure that only one station transmits at a time by using a strict coordination mechanism — typically a control token, polling, or a reservation scheme (e.g., Reservation, Polling, Token Passing).

## 1.2 Intuition / Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **single one-lane bridge** that many villagers need to cross simultaneously:

- **Random Access (ALOHA)** → Villagers simply walk onto the bridge whenever they wish. Sometimes two villagers collide mid-bridge; they stop, retreat, and try again after a random wait.
- **Slotted ALOHA** → A traffic light at the bridge entrance forces villagers to start crossing only when the light turns green (fixed time slots). This halves the chance of head-on collisions.
- **CSMA** → Before stepping onto the bridge, villagers *listen* to see if anyone is already crossing ("carrier sense"). If clear, they walk in.
- **CSMA/CD** → While crossing, they keep listening; if they hear another villager, they scream *"collision!"*, abort, and back off.
- **CSMA/CA** → They agree beforehand to *avoid* collisions by sending a small "ready" signal (RTS/CTS) and waiting for acknowledgment, much like two people coordinating with a polite *"after you."*
- **Controlled Access (Token Passing)** → A single **wooden baton (token)** is held by one villager at a time. Only the holder may cross. After crossing, the baton is passed to the next villager in a strict, round-robin sequence.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standardized Performance Metrics used by KTU Examiners:**
> - **T<sub>prop</sub>** → Propagation time (sec) = **Distance / Propagation Speed**
> - **T<sub>trans</sub>** or **T<sub>fr</sub>** → Frame transmission time (sec) = **Frame Length / Data Rate**
> - **a (Normalized Propagation Delay)** = **T<sub>prop</sub> / T<sub>trans</sub>** → The most critical dimensionless KTU parameter.
> - **Pure ALOHA Throughput (S)** = **G × e<sup>−2G</sup>** → Maximum S<sub>max</sub> = **0.184 (≈ 18.4 %)** at **G = 0.5**.
> - **Slotted ALOHA Throughput (S)** = **G × e<sup>−G</sup>** → Maximum S<sub>max</sub> = **0.368 (≈ 36.8 %)** at **G = 1.0**.
> - **Speed of signal in copper coax/twisted pair** ≈ **2 × 10<sup>8</sup> m/s** (≈ 2/3 c).
> - **Speed of signal in optical fiber** ≈ **2 × 10<sup>8</sup> m/s** (approx., limited by refractive index).
> - **Ethernet minimum frame size** = **64 bytes** (512 bits) — required for CSMA/CD collision detection.
> - **Ethernet maximum frame size** = **1518 bytes** (excluding preamble).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Overview

The Data Link Layer's MAC sublayer divides channel access protocols into three broad families (as per **Forouzan / KTU 2024 syllabus**):

| Family | Philosophy | Representative Protocols |
|---|---|---|
| **Random Access** | No coordination; collisions allowed & resolved | Pure ALOHA, Slotted ALOHA, CSMA, CSMA/CD, CSMA/CA |
| **Controlled Access** | Strict coordination by a controller/token | Reservation, Polling, Token Passing |
| **Channelization** | Dedicated bandwidth/time/code slice | FDMA, TDMA, CDMA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Random and Controlled access are the **focus of KTU Module 2**. Channelization (FDMA/TDMA/CDMA) belongs to Module 3.

---

## 2.2 RANDOM ACCESS PROTOCOLS — Detailed Logic

### 2.2.1 PURE (UNSLOTTED) ALOHA

- **Invented** at the University of Hawaii in the early 1970s for radio packet networks.
- **Logic**: A station transmits a frame the *moment* it has data. There are **no time slots** and **no carrier sensing**.
- **Collision Condition**: A frame of duration `T_fr` collides if *any other* station transmits during the **vulnerable period = 2 × T_fr**.
- **How it works (bullet logic)**:
  1. Station generates a frame.
  2. Transmit immediately over the shared medium.
  3. The station (and the receiver) waits for an **ACK** within a timeout.
  4. If **no ACK** → assume **collision** occurred.
  5. Wait a **random backoff time** (multiple of `T_fr` or `T_prop`).
  6. Retransmit. Repeat up to a maximum retry count.
- **Why random backoff?** → If every collided station retried at the same instant, they would collide *again* forever (a phenomenon called **capture effect / synchronization**).
- **Real-world utility**: Original ALOHAnet for inter-island Hawaiian radio communication; precursor to Ethernet.

### 2.2.2 SLOTTED ALOHA

- **Modification**: Time on the channel is divided into **discrete time slots** of duration `T_fr` each. A clock (or a sync beacon) synchronizes all stations.
- **Logic**: A station may only transmit at the **start of a slot**. If it misses the slot start, it must wait for the next one.
- **Vulnerable period** reduces from `2 T_fr` to exactly `T_fr` (only frames in the *same slot* collide).
- **Why throughput doubles**: Doubling the vulnerable time removes the cross-slot collision probability.

### 2.2.3 CSMA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access)

- **Improvement over ALOHA**: Stations *listen to the medium first* (carrier sensing / "Listen Before Talk").
- **Variants based on persistence**:
  - **1-persistent CSMA**: If medium is idle, transmit *immediately* with probability 1. If busy, keep sensing until idle, then transmit. **Highest collision risk** of all CSMA variants.
  - **Non-persistent CSMA**: If medium is busy, *wait a random time* before sensing again. Reduces collisions but adds delay.
  - **p-persistent CSMA**: Used in **slotted channels**. If idle, transmit with probability `p`; defer to next slot with probability `(1-p)`. Balances throughput and delay.
- **Limitation**: CSMA still suffers collisions due to **propagation delay** — by the time a station senses idle, the leading bit of a distant station's frame may already be in transit.

### 2.2.4 CSMA/CD (Collision Detection)

- **Where used**: IEEE 802.3 Ethernet (classic bus & star topologies with hubs).
- **Logic**:
  1. Sense carrier; if idle, start transmitting.
  2. **While transmitting, continue to monitor the medium** for signal energy (collision detection).
  3. If collision detected (signal level > expected), **transmit a 32-bit Jam signal** to ensure all stations detect the collision.
  4. Stop transmission, run the **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)** algorithm:
     - For the *i*-th collision, choose `k` uniformly at random from `{0, 1, ..., 2^i − 1}`.
     - Wait `k × 51.2 μs` (the **slot time** for 10 Mbps Ethernet).
     - Maximum backoff: 1023 slots (after 16 collisions the frame is dropped).
  5. Retry.
- **Why a minimum frame size of 64 bytes?** → The sender must still be transmitting when the *farthest* collision signal returns. This guarantees the sender *detects* the collision before finishing the frame. The condition is:
  
  $$T_{trans} \geq 2 \times T_{prop} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad a \leq 0.5$$
  
  (where `a = T_prop / T_trans`).

### 2.2.5 CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance)

- **Where used**: IEEE 802.11 Wi-Fi (wireless LANs).
- **Why not CSMA/CD?** → In wireless, a station **cannot hear its own transmission** while transmitting (the near-far problem), so collision *detection* is impractical. Additionally, hidden/exposed terminal problems exist.
- **Logic**:
  1. **DIFS (Distributed Inter-Frame Space)** wait → sense medium.
  2. If idle for DIFS, start a **backoff counter** randomly drawn from `[0, CW]`.
  3. Decrement counter while medium is idle; freeze counter when medium is busy.
  4. When counter reaches 0, transmit.
  5. Receiver waits **SIFS (Short Inter-Frame Space)** and sends **ACK**.
  6. On collision (no ACK), sender doubles the **Contention Window (CW)** up to a maximum.
- **Optional enhancement**: **RTS/CTS handshake** (Request-To-Send / Clear-To-Send) to mitigate hidden-terminal collisions.

---

## 2.3 CONTROLLED ACCESS PROTOCOLS — Detailed Logic

### 2.3.1 RESERVATION

- **Logic**: Time is divided into **reservation intervals** and **data intervals**. Stations first *reserve* a slot for the upcoming data interval.
- **Typical structure (e.g., Roberts Reservation)**:
  - A frame is divided into `N` mini-slots (one per station).
  - In the reservation sub-frame, a station asserts its mini-slot if it has data to send.
  - In the data sub-frame, stations transmit **in the order they reserved**, with no contention.
- **Why use it?** → Completely eliminates collisions once reservation is done. Suitable for **satellite / long-delay** channels.

### 2.3.2 POLLING

- A **primary device (controller)** polls secondary devices one at a time.
- **Two types**:
  - **Roll-Call Polling**: Controller asks each station in *predefined order* (1, 2, ..., N, 1, 2, ...): *"Station X, do you have data to send?"* Station responds with data or "nothing."
  - **Hub Polling (Token-style)**: A *poll frame* is passed station-to-station; only the polled station transmits and then passes the poll to the next.
- **Drawback**: Single point of failure (the controller); polling overhead can be large when most stations are idle.

### 2.3.3 TOKEN PASSING

- A small control frame called the **Token** (3-byte frame in IEEE 802.5) circulates among stations in a **logical ring**.
- **Rules**:
  - A station **may transmit only when it holds the token**.
  - After transmitting (or if it has nothing to send), the station passes the token to its **logical successor**.
  - **Token Holding Time (THT)** limits how long a station may hold the token (e.g., 10 ms in Token Ring).
- **Failure handling**: A **Active Monitor** station detects a lost token (no token seen for a full **Token Rotation Time**) and issues a new one.
- **Standards**: IEEE 802.4 (Token Bus), IEEE 802.5 (Token Ring), FDDI (fiber).
- **Real-world utility**: Historically used in IBM Token Ring networks, factory automation networks (ARCNET, Profibus).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **definitive formula table** every KTU 2024 student must memorize for this module. **Vertical pipes in absolute-value expressions use `\vert` to protect Markdown table syntax.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Propagation Time | $T_{prop} = D / v$ | $D$ = cable length, $v$ ≈ **2 × 10<sup>8</sup> m/s** in copper |
| 2 | Transmission Time | $T_{trans} = L / R$ | $L$ = frame length (bits), $R$ = data rate (bps) |
| 3 | Normalized Delay | $a = T_{prop} / T_{trans}$ | If $a \rightarrow 0$: LAN; if $a \rightarrow 1$: satellite |
| 4 | Pure ALOHA Throughput | $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$ | Max $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184$ at $G = 0.5$ |
| 5 | Slotted ALOHA Throughput | $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$ | Max $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$ at $G = 1.0$ |
| 6 | Vulnerable Period (Pure ALOHA) | $V = 2 \cdot T_{fr}$ | Any transmission starting within $\pm T_{fr}$ collides |
| 7 | Vulnerable Period (Slotted ALOHA) | $V = T_{fr}$ | Only same-slot collisions |
| 8 | CSMA/CD efficiency | $E = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.4 \cdot a}$ | Approx. for 10 Mbps Ethernet with 51.2 μs slot |
| 9 | Binary Exp. Backoff (BEB) | $k \in \left[0,\ 2^{i} - 1\right]$ | $i$ = collision attempt number (capped at 10 → 1023) |
| 10 | Token Rotation Time (TRT) | $TRT = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (T_{trans,i} + \tau_i)$ | Sum of all station transmit times + propagation |
| 11 | CSMA/CD condition | $T_{trans} \geq 2 \cdot T_{prop}$ | Forces minimum frame size for collision detection |
| 12 | Polling efficiency | $E = \dfrac{N \cdot T_{data}}{N \cdot (T_{poll} + T_{prop}) + N \cdot T_{data}}$ | $T_{poll}$ = poll frame time |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where these protocols are deployed in industry today:**
> - **Slotted ALOHA variants** → RFID anti-collision readers, satellite uplink channels (VSAT systems).
> - **CSMA/CD** → Legacy 10/100 Mbps Ethernet hubs & coaxial bus networks (rare today, but conceptually taught everywhere).
> - **CSMA/CA** → Every Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11) router, smartphone hotspot, and IoT device.
> - **Token Passing** → ARCNET, IBM Token Ring (legacy), Profibus (industrial automation), FDDI (fiber backbone in the 1990s).
> - **Polling/Reservation** → Satellite MAC (DVB-RCS2), USB host polling, Bluetooth master-slave polling, LTE resource reservation in the MAC scheduler.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION 1 — Pure ALOHA Throughput

We model the system as a **Poisson process**:
- The channel is shared by an **infinite population** of independent users.
- New frames (new + retransmitted) arrive at a **mean rate of `G` frames per frame-time**.
- Transmission is unslotted (continuous time).

The probability that exactly `k` frames are generated in a time interval of duration `t` is:

$$
P(k) = \dfrac{(G t)^{k} \cdot e^{-G t}}{k!}
$$

A particular frame is transmitted over a duration of `T_fr` (which we normalize to 1 frame-time for analysis). For this frame to be **successfully received**, **no other frame** may overlap with it.

The vulnerable period for a pure ALOHA frame is `2 · T_fr = 2` frame-times (one frame-time before, one frame-time after, because a colliding frame could start anywhere in that window).

The probability of **zero arrivals** in a window of length 2 is given by substituting `t = 2` and `k = 0` into the Poisson equation:

$$
P(0) = \dfrac{(G \cdot 2)^{0} \cdot e^{-G \cdot 2}}{0!}
$$

Performing the arithmetic step-by-step:

$$
P(0) = \dfrac{1 \cdot e^{-2G}}{1}
$$

$$
P(0) = e^{-2G}
$$

**Throughput `S`** is defined as the average number of *successful* frames per frame-time, which equals the load `G` multiplied by the probability of success:

$$
S = G \cdot e^{-2G}
$$

This is the **Pure ALOHA throughput equation**.

### Maximization of S

To find the maximum, take the derivative with respect to `G` and set it to zero:

$$
\dfrac{dS}{dG} = e^{-2G} + G \cdot (-2) \cdot e^{-2G} = 0
$$

Factor out `e^{-2G}` (which is never zero):

$$
e^{-2G} \cdot (1 - 2G) = 0
$$

Since $e^{-2G} \neq 0$, we must have:

$$
1 - 2G = 0
$$

$$
G = 0.5
$$

Substituting $G = 0.5$ back into the throughput equation:

$$
S_{max} = 0.5 \cdot e^{-2 \cdot 0.5}
$$

$$
S_{max} = 0.5 \cdot e^{-1}
$$

$$
S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2e}
$$

$$
S_{max} \approx \dfrac{1}{5.4366} \approx 0.1839
$$

$$
S_{max} \approx 18.4\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The **maximum throughput of Pure ALOHA is approximately 18.4 %** of the raw channel capacity, achieved at a load of $G = 0.5$ frames per frame-time.

---

## 3.2 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION 2 — Slotted ALOHA Throughput

In Slotted ALOHA, time is **discretized** into slots of length `T_fr`. A frame may only be transmitted at the **start of a slot**.

The vulnerable period shrinks to a **single slot** `T_fr = 1` frame-time because:
- Two frames in the *same* slot will collide.
- Frames in *adjacent* slots do **not** overlap (one finishes at the end of slot $n$ before the other begins at the start of slot $n+1$).

The probability of zero other arrivals in a window of length 1 (i.e., in the same slot) is:

$$
P(0) = e^{-G}
$$

Therefore, throughput is:

$$
S = G \cdot e^{-G}
$$

### Maximization of S

$$
\dfrac{dS}{dG} = e^{-G} - G \cdot e^{-G} = 0
$$

$$
e^{-G} \cdot (1 - G) = 0
$$

$$
G = 1
$$

Substituting $G = 1$:

$$
S_{max} = 1 \cdot e^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{e}
$$

$$
S_{max} \approx 0.3679
$$

$$
S_{max} \approx 36.8\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Slotted ALOHA achieves exactly **double** the maximum throughput of Pure ALOHA. This is the single most asked numerical problem in KTU examinations.

---

## 3.3 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION 3 — CSMA/CD Minimum Frame Size Constraint

Consider the **worst case** for collision detection in a CSMA/CD network:
- Two stations, **A** and **B**, are at the **two opposite ends** of the cable.
- Cable length = $D$ meters; signal propagation speed = $v$ m/s.
- Just before station A begins to transmit, station B starts transmitting (A does not yet know B is active — A senses idle).

**Step 1:** Compute the propagation time across the entire cable:

$$
T_{prop} = \dfrac{D}{v}
$$

**Step 2:** The first bit from B must travel the full distance $D$ to reach A:

$$
\text{Time for B's first bit to reach A} = T_{prop} = \dfrac{D}{v}
$$

**Step 3:** For A to **detect the collision**, A must still be transmitting when B's signal arrives. Hence the frame transmission time at A must be at least as long as the round-trip propagation time:

$$
T_{trans} \geq 2 \cdot T_{prop}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $T_{trans} = L / R$ and $T_{prop} = D / v$:

$$
\dfrac{L}{R} \geq 2 \cdot \dfrac{D}{v}
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the minimum frame length $L_{min}$:

$$
L_{min} = \dfrac{2 \cdot D \cdot R}{v}
$$

**Numerical Example (a classic KTU problem):**

> A CSMA/CD network uses a 10 Mbps data rate over a 1 km coaxial cable with $v = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s. Find the minimum frame size.

$$
T_{prop} = \dfrac{1000}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 5\ \mu s
$$

$$
L_{min} = 2 \cdot T_{prop} \cdot R = 2 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 10 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
L_{min} = 100\ \text{bits} = 12.5\ \text{bytes}
$$

The IEEE 802.3 standard **mandates** a minimum of **64 bytes (512 bits)** to be safe even for longer cables (max 2500 m with 4 repeaters, 25.6 μs round-trip), explaining the slot time of 51.2 μs (= 512 bits / 10 Mbps).

---

## 3.4 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION 4 — Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)

The BEB algorithm is required for CSMA/CD. After the *i*-th collision, the station:

**Step 1:** Sets the backoff counter `k` as a uniformly distributed random integer in the range:

$$
k \in \left[0,\ 2^{i} - 1\right]
$$

**Step 2:** Waits for a duration of $k \times T_{slot}$ where $T_{slot} = 51.2\ \mu s$ in 10 Mbps Ethernet.

**Step 3:** Caps the exponent at $i = 10$ (so the counter ranges from 0 to 1023).

**Step 4:** After 16 consecutive collisions, the frame is **abandoned** and an error is reported to the upper layer.

**Worked Example (KTU-style):**

> A station on a 10 Mbps Ethernet network experiences its 3rd, 4th, and 5th collisions. List all possible values of the backoff counter $k$ for each, and find the maximum possible wait time in μs for the 5th collision.

For 3rd collision: $k \in [0, 2^3 - 1] = [0, 7]$, so 8 possible values.

For 4th collision: $k \in [0, 2^4 - 1] = [0, 15]$, so 16 possible values.

For 5th collision: $k \in [0, 2^5 - 1] = [0, 31]$, so 32 possible values.

Maximum wait for 5th collision:

$$
T_{wait, max} = 31 \times 51.2\ \mu s = 1587.2\ \mu s \approx 1.59\ \text{ms}
$$

---

## 3.5 Comparative Throughput Diagram (Key KTU Plot)

The following table summarizes the peak throughputs:

| Protocol | Peak Throughput S<sub>max</sub> | Optimum Load G | Vulnerable Period |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pure ALOHA | 0.184 (≈ 18.4 %) | 0.5 | 2 T<sub>fr</sub> |
| Slotted ALOHA | 0.368 (≈ 36.8 %) | 1.0 | T<sub>fr</sub> |
| 1-persistent CSMA | ≈ 0.529 | depends on `a` | 2 T<sub>prop</sub> |
| Non-persistent CSMA | ≈ 0.818 | depends on `a` | T<sub>prop</sub> |
| CSMA/CD (Ethernet) | ≈ 0.90+ for very small `a` | 1.0 | 2 T<sub>prop</sub> |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Random vs. Controlled Access Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Layer PDU arrives at MAC Sublayer] --> B{Is a Controller or Token Present?}
    
    B -- Yes --> C[Controlled Access Path]
    B -- No --> D[Random Access Path]
    
    C --> C1{Access Method?}
    C1 -- Token Held --> C2[Transmit Frame]
    C1 -- Token Not Held --> C3[Wait for Token]
    C3 --> C2
    C2 --> C4[Release Token to Successor]
    
    D --> D1{Sense Carrier First?}
    D1 -- Yes CSMA family --> D2{Collision Detection Possible?}
    D1 -- No ALOHA family --> D5[Transmit Immediately]
    
    D2 -- Yes Wired Ethernet --> D3[CSMA CD with BEB Backoff]
    D2 -- No Wireless --> D4[CSMA CA with RTS CTS and DIFS SIFS]
    
    D5 --> D6{ACK Received?}
    D5 --> D3
    D3 --> D7{Collision Detected?}
    D7 -- Yes --> D8[Send Jam Signal and Backoff]
    D8 --> D3
    D7 -- No --> D9[Success]
    
    D4 --> D10{DIFS Wait Clear and Backoff Zero?}
    D10 -- No --> D11[Decrement or Freeze Backoff]
    D11 --> D10
    D10 -- Yes --> D12[RTS to Receiver]
    D12 --> D13[Wait for CTS]
    D13 -- Received --> D14[Transmit Frame]
    D14 --> D15[Wait for ACK]
    D15 -- Received --> D9
    D15 -- Timeout --> D8
    D13 -- Timeout --> D8
    
    D6 -- Yes --> D9
    D6 -- No --> D8
```

## 4.2 Token Ring Logical Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Station 1\nToken Holder] -->|Frame then Token| S2[Station 2\nLogical Successor]
    S2 -->|Token Pass| S3[Station 3]
    S3 -->|Token Pass| S4[Station 4]
    S4 -->|Token Pass| S5[Station N]
    S5 -->|Token Pass back| S1
    
    S1 -.->|Monitors Token| AM[Active Monitor\nDetects Lost Token and Reissues]
    AM -.->|Recovery Action| S1
```

## 4.3 Slotted ALOHA Slot Map (Visual Time Axis)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Slotted ALOHA Slot Contention Timeline
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    
    section Slot 1
    Station A transmits :a1, 0, 1
    Collision with C    :crit, c1, 0, 1
    
    section Slot 2
    Idle                 :done, idle2, 1, 1
    
    section Slot 3
    Station B transmits  :b1, 2, 1
    Success              :milestone, m1, 2, 1
    
    section Slot 4
    Station D transmits  :d1, 3, 1
    Success              :milestone, m2, 3, 1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read the diagram carefully:** Frames from `A` and `C` in `Slot 1` *collide* (both start at the slot boundary). `Slot 2` is *idle*. `Slot 3` carries a *successful* `B` frame. `Slot 4` carries a *successful* `D` frame. This is exactly how Slotted ALOHA behaves on a shared medium.

## 4.4 CSMA/CD Collision Detection Timeline

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Station A
    participant B as Station B
    participant M as Medium
    
    A->>M: Sense Carrier (Idle)
    B->>M: Sense Carrier (Idle, slightly later)
    A->>M: Begin Transmit (Frame)
    B->>M: Begin Transmit (Frame) - Collision begins
    M-->>A: B's signal arrives after T_prop
    Note over A: Collision Detected!
    A->>M: Transmit Jam Signal
    B->>M: Transmit Jam Signal
    A->>A: Run Binary Exp. Backoff
    B->>B: Run Binary Exp. Backoff
    A->>M: Retransmit after Random Wait
    B->>M: Retransmit after Random Wait
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define the term "vulnerable period" for Pure ALOHA and Slotted ALOHA. Why is it different?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The *vulnerable period* is the time interval during which any other transmission by another station will cause a **collision** with a frame that has just started transmitting.

- **Pure ALOHA:** Vulnerable period = **2 × T<sub>fr</sub>**, because a colliding frame may start **at any time** during a window spanning one full frame-time *before* and one full frame-time *after* the given frame's start. Hence the collision window is symmetric and equals **2 T<sub>fr</sub>**.
- **Slotted ALOHA:** Vulnerable period = **T<sub>fr</sub>**, because all stations are forced to start their transmission only at slot boundaries. Two frames collide **only** if they are transmitted in the **same slot**, eliminating the cross-slot overlap entirely.

This is why Slotted ALOHA achieves **double** the maximum throughput (36.8 %) compared to Pure ALOHA (18.4 %).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between CSMA/CD and CSMA/CA. State one application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | CSMA/CD | CSMA/CA |
|---|---|---|
| Collision handling | **Detects** collisions after they occur | **Avoids** collisions before they occur |
| Energy monitoring during TX | Yes (collision → Jam) | No |
| Backoff method | Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB) | Contention Window (CW) doubling |
| Acknowledgment | Not mandatory | **Mandatory** ACK after SIFS |
| Topology suitability | **Wired** (full-duplex collision possible) | **Wireless** (hidden/exposed terminals) |
| Application | IEEE 802.3 Ethernet (legacy) | IEEE 802.11 Wi-Fi, 802.15.4 ZigBee |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the operation of **Slotted ALOHA**. Derive its throughput expression and find the maximum throughput.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Pure ALOHA network operates at 9600 bps. Each frame carries **800 bits**. Find:
  (i) Frame transmission time `T<sub>fr</sub>`.
  (ii) Vulnerable period.
  (iii) Maximum throughput in frames/second and in percentage.
  (iv) If the system is converted to **Slotted ALOHA**, what is the new maximum throughput in frames/second?

---

### Question A — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Slotted ALOHA (7 marks)

**Operation (diagram + 4 marks):**

```
|<------ Slot 1 ------>|<------ Slot 2 ------>|<------ Slot 3 ------>|<-Slot 4->|...
   [A]      [B]              [C]         [D]                          [E]
   ✓ success ✓ success       ✗ collision          ✓ success
```

- Time is divided into **discrete slots of length T<sub>fr</sub>**.
- A station that has a frame ready **waits for the start of the next slot** to begin transmission.
- If **only one station** transmits in a slot → **success**.
- If **two or more** transmit in the same slot → **collision** → all involved stations wait a random number of slots and retry.
- Throughput `S` = average number of successful frames per slot.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Assume infinite users, Poisson arrival model with mean rate `G` frames per frame-time. Probability of `k` frames generated in a single slot of unit length:

$$
P(k) = \dfrac{G^{k} \cdot e^{-G}}{k!}
$$

For a frame to succeed, no other frame may be generated in the **same slot** (vulnerable period = 1 slot). Probability of zero other frames:

$$
P(0) = e^{-G}
$$

Therefore:

$$
S = G \cdot e^{-G}
$$

Differentiating and setting to zero:

$$
\dfrac{dS}{dG} = e^{-G} - G \cdot e^{-G} = e^{-G} \cdot (1 - G) = 0
$$

$$
\Longrightarrow G = 1
$$

Substituting:

$$
S_{max} = 1 \cdot e^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{e} \approx 0.368
$$

**Maximum throughput of Slotted ALOHA = 36.8 % at G = 1.0.**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** Data rate $R = 9600$ bps; frame length $L = 800$ bits.

**(i) Frame transmission time:**

$$
T_{fr} = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{800}{9600} = \dfrac{1}{12} \text{ s} \approx 0.0833\ \text{s} = 83.33\ \text{ms}
$$

**(ii) Vulnerable period (Pure ALOHA):**

$$
V_{pure} = 2 \cdot T_{fr} = 2 \times 0.0833 = 0.1667\ \text{ s} = 166.67\ \text{ms}
$$

**(iii) Maximum throughput in frames per second:**

Channel capacity in frames per second = $1 / T_{fr} = 12$ frames/s.

Maximum successful throughput (Pure ALOHA) = $S_{max} \times$ channel capacity:

$$
\lambda_{max,pure} = 0.184 \times 12 = 2.21\ \text{frames/second}
$$

Percentage = **18.4 %**.

**(iv) Slotted ALOHA new maximum throughput:**

$$
\lambda_{max,slotted} = 0.368 \times 12 = 4.42\ \text{frames/second}
$$

**Thus Slotted ALOHA exactly doubles the maximum throughput — from 2.21 to 4.42 frames/second.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **CSMA/CD** protocol with a flow diagram. Show that the **minimum frame length** in CSMA/CD must satisfy $L_{min} = \dfrac{2 \cdot D \cdot R}{v}$ where $D$ is the maximum cable length, $R$ is the data rate, and $v$ is the propagation speed.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 10 Mbps CSMA/CD network uses a cable of maximum length 2500 m. Propagation speed is $2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s. Compute:
  (i) Round-trip propagation delay.
  (ii) Minimum frame length in bits.
  (iii) If the cable is replaced by a 1 Gbps link, what is the new minimum frame length in bits?
  (iv) Comment on the practical implication of (iii).

---

### Question B — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — CSMA/CD Explanation (7 marks)

**Operation (4 marks):**

1. **Carrier sense** — station checks whether the medium is idle.
2. If idle, **begin transmission**; simultaneously, the station **continues to monitor** the medium's signal level.
3. If a **collision** is detected (signal energy exceeds expected), the station:
   - **Stops transmission immediately**.
   - **Sends a 32-bit Jam signal** to ensure all other stations also detect the collision.
   - Runs the **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)** algorithm to wait a random time, then retries.
4. The station repeats the above up to 16 times. If all 16 attempts fail, the frame is **abandoned**.

**Derivation of minimum frame length (3 marks):**

Worst case: stations at *both ends* of the cable. Just before station A starts transmitting, station B also starts. A senses idle and begins. A does not know of B's transmission until B's signal has propagated the full length $D$ of the cable.

**Time for B's first bit to reach A:**

$$
t_1 = T_{prop} = \dfrac{D}{v}
$$

For A to **detect the collision**, A must still be transmitting at $t = t_1$, which means the frame transmission time $T_{trans} \geq t_1$:

$$
T_{trans} \geq T_{prop} = \dfrac{D}{v}
$$

However, A also needs to send its own signal back to B to *register* a complete collision. The total round-trip is $2 T_{prop}$. To be safe (so that the *first bit* of the frame has not finished transmitting before the collision is detected):

$$
T_{trans} \geq 2 \cdot T_{prop}
$$

Substituting $T_{trans} = L_{min}/R$ and $T_{prop} = D/v$:

$$
\dfrac{L_{min}}{R} \geq 2 \cdot \dfrac{D}{v}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{min} = \dfrac{2 \cdot D \cdot R}{v}}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $R_1 = 10$ Mbps = $10^7$ bps; $D = 2500$ m; $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**(i) Round-trip propagation delay:**

$$
T_{prop,one-way} = \dfrac{D}{v} = \dfrac{2500}{2 \times 10^8} = 1.25 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{s} = 12.5\ \mu s
$$

$$
T_{round-trip} = 2 \cdot T_{prop,one-way} = 25\ \mu s
$$

**(ii) Minimum frame length at 10 Mbps:**

$$
L_{min} = 2 \cdot D \cdot R_1 / v = 2 \times 2500 \times 10^7 / (2 \times 10^8)
$$

$$
L_{min} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{10}}{2 \times 10^8} = 250\ \text{bits} \approx 31.25\ \text{bytes}
$$

The IEEE 802.3 standard pads the minimum to **64 bytes (512 bits)** for safety (to account for repeater delays and the 5-segment / 4-repeater rule).

**(iii) New minimum frame length at 1 Gbps:**

$$
L_{min}' = 2 \cdot D \cdot R_2 / v = 2 \times 2500 \times 10^9 / (2 \times 10^8)
$$

$$
L_{min}' = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{12}}{2 \times 10^8} = 25{,}000\ \text{bits} = 3125\ \text{bytes}
$$

**(iv) Practical implication:**

At 1 Gbps, the *theoretical* minimum frame length for a 2500 m cable is **3125 bytes**, which is unacceptably large and would waste bandwidth for short frames. This is precisely why **full-duplex switched Ethernet** (point-to-point links with switches) is used at gigabit speeds: with no shared medium, **collisions are impossible** and CSMA/CD is disabled. IEEE 802.3 specifications for 1 Gbps and above (10G, 40G, 100G) **abandon CSMA/CD entirely** and operate in **full-duplex mode** only.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic — KTU Valuation Key Patterns:**
> 1. **Pure ALOHA throughput formula confusion:** Students often write $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$ for Pure ALOHA (this is the **Slotted** formula!). The correct Pure ALOHA equation is $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$. **Examiner docks 1 full mark for this swap.**
> 2. **Vulnerable period mix-up:** Writing $T_{fr}$ for Pure ALOHA and $2 T_{fr}$ for Slotted — it is the **reverse**.
> 3. **CSMA/CD formula substitution error:** When asked for $L_{min}$, students frequently forget to multiply by **2** for the round-trip — they get the propagation factor wrong. Always draw the timeline.
> 4. **BEB indexing:** Off-by-one errors in $2^i - 1$ (collision $i$ vs. attempt $i+1$). Use the *i-th collision* wording from the question.
> 5. **Token Ring failure recovery:** Many students forget to mention the **Active Monitor** and **lost token detection** (a full Token Rotation Time without seeing the token) — required for a complete answer.
> 6. **Polling types:** Confusing **Roll-Call** (controller-driven) with **Hub Polling** (token-like, station-driven). KTU examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 7. **Diagrams mandatory:** For a 7-mark sub-question, an unlabeled diagram fetches only partial marks. Always label **time axis, slot boundaries, stations, and outcomes (success/collision/ack)**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Pure ALOHA:** $S = G e^{-2G}$, $S_{max} = 0.184$ at $G = 0.5$, vulnerable = $2 T_{fr}$.
- [ ] **Slotted ALOHA:** $S = G e^{-G}$, $S_{max} = 0.368$ at $G = 1$, vulnerable = $T_{fr}$. **Throughput doubles** vs. Pure ALOHA.
- [ ] **CSMA:** Listen Before Talk (LBT). Variants = 1-persistent, non-persistent, p-persistent.
- [ ] **CSMA/CD:** Wired Ethernet. Listens *during* transmission. Uses **Jam signal** + **BEB**. Minimum frame = 64 bytes; slot time = 51.2 μs.
- [ ] **CSMA/CA:** Wireless (Wi-Fi). Cannot detect collisions → uses **DIFS/SIFS**, **contention window**, **RTS/CTS**, and **mandatory ACK**.
- [ ] **Minimum frame length constraint:** $L_{min} = 2 D R / v$. Higher bit rate → larger minimum frame (impractical at ≥ 1 Gbps → Ethernet abandons CSMA/CD and goes full-duplex).
- [ ] **BEB range:** $k \in [0, 2^i - 1]$ where $i$ = collision number. Cap at $i = 10$ (range 0–1023). After 16 collisions → frame dropped.
- [ ] **Reservation:** Mini-slots in a reservation sub-frame reserve data slots in the data sub-frame.
- [ ] **Polling:** Two types — **Roll-Call** (controller-asks) vs. **Hub Polling** (token-asks). Single point of failure = controller.
- [ ] **Token Passing:** Logical ring, **Token Holding Time (THT)** limits each station. **Active Monitor** detects and regenerates a lost token.
- [ ] **Standards mapping:** ALOHA = ALOHAnet, Slotted ALOHA = RFID/VSAT, CSMA/CD = IEEE 802.3, CSMA/CA = IEEE 802.11, Token Ring = IEEE 802.5, Token Bus = IEEE 802.4.
- [ ] **Channel parameter `a` = T_prop / T_fr:** Small `a` → LAN (good for CSMA/CD); large `a` → satellite (better for ALOHA / reservation).
- [ ] **Always draw timelines** in CSMA/CD, BEB, and ALOHA collision questions. **Diagrams are mandatory for full marks** at KTU.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IEEE Standards for Wired and Wireless LAN
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# IEEE Standards for Wired and Wireless LAN

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET521 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IEEE Standards for Wired and Wireless LAN

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **IEEE 802 Standard Family** is a set of networking standards developed by the **Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE) 802 LAN/MAN Standards Committee (LMSC)**. These standards define the **Physical Layer (Layer 1)** and a portion of the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** of the **OSI Reference Model** for **Local Area Networks (LAN)**, **Metropolitan Area Networks (MAN)**, and certain **Wireless Personal Area Networks (WPAN)**.

The IEEE 802 family uses a layered approach where the Data Link Layer is split into two sublayers:
- **Logical Link Control (LLC)** — defined by **IEEE 802.2**
- **Media Access Control (MAC)** — defined by the specific technology standard (e.g., 802.3, 802.11)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The IEEE 802 family is the foundation of modern LANs. Standards like **IEEE 802.3 (Ethernet)** dominate wired networks, while **IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi)** dominates wireless networks. KTU questions frequently test the comparative frame formats, MAC protocols, and CSMA/CD vs CSMA/CA mechanisms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — IEEE:**
> IEEE (Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers) is the world's largest technical professional organization, headquartered in the United States, responsible for publishing standards that govern modern communication technologies. The "802" prefix originates from the year and month the project was initiated — **February (2) of 1980 (80)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **huge office building** with many floors and rooms (this represents a network of computers). To ensure everyone can talk to each other smoothly, the office management creates a **"Rulebook"** for conversations:

- **Which room can be entered and how?** (This is the **Physical Layer** — the cables or radio waves.)
- **Who can speak first when two people want to talk at the same time?** (This is the **MAC Sublayer** — the access rules.)
- **What language is being spoken? How are sentences formed?** (This is the **LLC Sublayer** — logical data packaging.)

The **IEEE 802 Standard Family** is this universal "Rulebook" that every networking device (routers, switches, Wi-Fi cards, Bluetooth chips) follows so that equipment from different manufacturers can work together — a property called **interoperability**.

### 1.3 The IEEE 802 Reference Model

| OSI Layer | IEEE 802 Layer | Function |
|-----------|---------------|----------|
| Layer 2 (Data Link) | **LLC (802.2)** | Flow control, error handling, multiplexing |
| Layer 2 (Data Link) | **MAC (802.3, 802.11, etc.)** | Frame assembly, addressing, medium access |
| Layer 1 (Physical) | **PHY (Physical)** | Bit transmission over medium |

### 1.4 Why IEEE Standards Matter in Engineering

- **Interoperability** — A Dell laptop can connect to a Cisco switch using Ethernet without compatibility issues.
- **Cost reduction** — Standardized chipsets lead to mass production.
- **Global adoption** — Wi-Fi (802.11) and Ethernet (802.3) are universally deployed.
- **Engineering design** — Network engineers use these standards to design campus LANs, enterprise networks, and wireless infrastructure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The IEEE 802 Family — Master Chart

The IEEE 802 committee is divided into several working groups, each addressing a specific technology. Below is the high-yield table for KTU 2024:

| Standard | Full Name | Primary Use | Medium |
|----------|-----------|-------------|--------|
| **IEEE 802.1** | Internetworking | LAN/MAN bridging, VLANs (802.1Q) | Architecture |
| **IEEE 802.2** | Logical Link Control (LLC) | Common Data Link sublayer | All LANs |
| **IEEE 802.3** | Ethernet (CSMA/CD) | Wired LAN | Twisted pair, fiber, coax |
| **IEEE 802.4** | Token Bus | Industrial LANs (legacy) | Coaxial cable |
| **IEEE 802.5** | Token Ring | LANs (legacy, IBM) | Twisted pair |
| **IEEE 802.6** | Metropolitan Area Network (DQDB) | MAN | Fiber/distributed queues |
| **IEEE 802.7** | Broadband LAN | Coax broadband | Coaxial cable |
| **IEEE 802.8** | Fiber Optic LAN | Fiber-optic networks | Optical fiber |
| **IEEE 802.9** | Integrated Services (IsoEthernet) | Voice + Data | Twisted pair |
| **IEEE 802.10** | LAN Security | Security & encryption | All LANs |
| **IEEE 802.11** | Wireless LAN (Wi-Fi) | Wireless LAN | Radio waves (2.4/5/6 GHz) |
| **IEEE 802.12** | Demand Priority (100BASE-VG) | 100 Mbps LAN | Twisted pair |
| **IEEE 802.15** | Wireless PAN (Bluetooth, ZigBee) | Short-range wireless | Radio waves |
| **IEEE 802.16** | Wireless MAN (WiMAX) | Broadband wireless | Radio waves (up to 50 km) |
| **IEEE 802.20** | Mobile Broadband Wireless Access | Mobile networks | Radio waves |
| **IEEE 802.22** | Wireless Regional Area Network (WRAN) | Cognitive radio | TV whitespace |

### 2.2 Deep Dive: IEEE 802.2 — Logical Link Control (LLC)

The **LLC sublayer** sits above all MAC sublayers, providing a uniform interface to the Network Layer (typically IP). It performs:
- **Multiplexing** of upper-layer protocols (e.g., IPv4, IPv6, ARP)
- **Error control** and **flow control**
- **Service Access Point (SAP)** addressing

> [!NOTE]
> **Three LLC Services:**
> 1. **Type 1 — Unacknowledged Connectionless Service** (most common, used in Ethernet)
> 2. **Type 2 — Connection-Oriented Service** (rarely used)
> 3. **Type 3 — Acknowledged Connectionless Service** (used in some legacy systems)

### 2.3 Deep Dive: IEEE 802.3 — Ethernet (Wired LAN)

Ethernet is the **dominant wired LAN standard**, originally developed by **Robert Metcalfe and David Boggs at Xerox PARC in 1973**, and standardized as **IEEE 802.3 in 1985**.

#### 2.3.1 Ethernet MAC Sublayer — CSMA/CD

Ethernet uses **Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection (CSMA/CD)**:

1. **Carrier Sense** — Listen to the medium. If idle, transmit.
2. **If busy** — Wait until the medium becomes idle, then transmit.
3. **Collision Detection** — Monitor the medium during transmission. If a collision is detected:
   - Stop transmission immediately.
   - Send a **jam signal** (32-bit pattern) to ensure all stations detect the collision.
   - Wait for a **random backoff time** (Binary Exponential Backoff).

#### 2.3.2 Binary Exponential Backoff Algorithm

After the $n$-th collision for a frame, the station randomly selects a slot number $k$ from:

$$k \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 2^m - 1\}$$

where:
$$m = \min(n, 10)$$

The station then waits:
$$T_{\text{wait}} = k \times T_{\text{slot}}$$

The maximum backoff after 16 consecutive collisions results in frame discard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The slot time for **10BASE-T Ethernet** is $\mathbf{51.2 \ \mu s}$ (transmission time for 64 bytes at 10 Mbps). This corresponds to the minimum frame size.

#### 2.3.3 Minimum Frame Size & Maximum Network Length

For CSMA/CD to work, the frame must be long enough that collision detection completes before frame transmission ends:

$$T_{\text{frame}} \geq 2 \times T_{\text{propagation}}$$

This is why Ethernet has:
- **Minimum frame size: 64 bytes** (header + payload + FCS)
- **Maximum 10BASE-T segment length: 2500 meters** (with repeaters)
- **Maximum 10BASE5 segment length: 500 meters**

#### 2.3.4 Ethernet Frame Format (IEEE 802.3)

| Field | Size | Function |
|-------|------|----------|
| Preamble | 7 bytes | Synchronization pattern (10101010...) |
| SFD (Start Frame Delimiter) | 1 byte | Marks start of frame (10101011) |
| Destination MAC Address | 6 bytes | Receiver address |
| Source MAC Address | 6 bytes | Sender address |
| Length/Type | 2 bytes | Length (802.3) or EtherType (Ethernet II) |
| Payload (Data) | 46–1500 bytes | Upper-layer data |
| FCS (Frame Check Sequence) | 4 bytes | CRC-32 error detection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Total frame size: 64 bytes (minimum) to 1518 bytes (maximum).** If payload < 46 bytes, **padding** is added.

#### 2.3.5 Ethernet Evolution (Speed Standards)

| Standard | Speed | Medium | Year |
|----------|-------|--------|------|
| 10BASE-5 | 10 Mbps | Thick coax | 1980 |
| 10BASE-2 | 10 Mbps | Thin coax | 1985 |
| 10BASE-T | 10 Mbps | Twisted pair | 1990 |
| 100BASE-TX (Fast Ethernet) | 100 Mbps | Cat5 UTP | 1995 |
| 1000BASE-T (Gigabit Ethernet) | 1 Gbps | Cat5e/6 | 1999 |
| 10GBASE-T | 10 Gbps | Cat6a/7 | 2006 |
| 40GBASE-T / 100GBASE-T | 40/100 Gbps | Fiber/Backbone | 2010+ |

### 2.4 Deep Dive: IEEE 802.5 — Token Ring

Token Ring was developed by **IBM** and standardized as **IEEE 802.5**.

- Uses a **token-passing** mechanism. A special 3-byte **token** circulates around the logical ring.
- Only the station holding the token can transmit.
- **No collisions** occur because access is deterministic.
- Supports **priority levels** and **token holding time (THT)**.
- **Physical topology:** Star (using **MAU** — Multistation Access Unit)
- **Logical topology:** Ring

### 2.5 Deep Dive: IEEE 802.4 — Token Bus

- Combines features of Ethernet (bus) and Token Ring (token-passing).
- Stations form a **logical ring** on a physical bus.
- Token passes from one station to the next in sequence.
- Used in **industrial automation** (e.g., MAP — Manufacturing Automation Protocol).
- Largely obsolete today.

### 2.6 Deep Dive: IEEE 802.11 — Wireless LAN (Wi-Fi)

This is the **most important wireless standard** for KTU. Wi-Fi operates using **CSMA/CA** (Collision Avoidance) instead of CSMA/CD because wireless stations **cannot detect collisions** while transmitting (the "**hidden node problem**" and reduced receiving sensitivity during transmission).

#### 2.6.1 Wi-Fi Network Components

- **STA (Station):** Wireless client device
- **AP (Access Point):** Central hub connecting STAs to the wired network
- **BSS (Basic Service Set):** Group of stations under one AP
- **ESS (Extended Service Set):** Multiple BSSs interconnected via a **DS (Distribution System)**
- **SSID (Service Set Identifier):** Name of the Wi-Fi network

#### 2.6.2 CSMA/CA Mechanism

1. **DIFS (DCF Inter-Frame Space)** — Station waits for DIFS after medium becomes idle.
2. **Backoff Counter** — Station picks a random slot and decrements the counter only when the medium is idle.
3. **RTS/CTS Handshake (optional but recommended):**
   - Sender sends **RTS (Request to Send)**.
   - Receiver replies with **CTS (Clear to Send)** after **SIFS (Short Inter-Frame Space)**.
   - All other stations defer transmission (NAV — Network Allocation Vector).
4. **Data transmission** followed by **ACK** (acknowledgment) from receiver.

$$T_{\text{ACK timeout}} = \text{SIFS} + \text{ACK transmission time}$$

#### 2.6.3 Inter-Frame Spacing Hierarchy

| IFS | Priority | Used For |
|-----|----------|----------|
| **SIFS** | Highest | ACK, CTS, fragment |
| **PIFS** | High | PCF (Point Coordination Function) |
| **DIFS** | Normal | DCF (Distributed Coordination Function) |
| **EIFS** | Lowest | Error recovery |

#### 2.6.4 IEEE 802.11 Frame Format

| Field | Size (bytes) | Function |
|-------|--------------|----------|
| Frame Control | 2 | Type, subtype, flags |
| Duration/ID | 2 | NAV duration |
| Address 1 | 6 | Receiver MAC |
| Address 2 | 6 | Transmitter MAC |
| Address 3 | 6 | Source/Destination (depends on type) |
| Sequence Control | 2 | Fragment/sequence number |
| Address 4 | 6 | Optional (WDS frames only) |
| Payload | 0–2312 | Data |
| FCS | 4 | CRC-32 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Four address fields exist because 802.11 supports four types of frame transfer: from station to station (BSS), from AP to station, from station to AP, and between APs (WDS — Wireless Distribution System).

#### 2.6.5 IEEE 802.11 Evolution (Wi-Fi Generations)

| Standard | Generation | Frequency | Max Speed | Year |
|----------|------------|-----------|-----------|------|
| 802.11a | Wi-Fi 1 | 5 GHz | 54 Mbps | 1999 |
| 802.11b | Wi-Fi 2 | 2.4 GHz | 11 Mbps | 1999 |
| 802.11g | Wi-Fi 3 | 2.4 GHz | 54 Mbps | 2003 |
| 802.11n (Wi-Fi 4) | Wi-Fi 4 | 2.4/5 GHz | 600 Mbps | 2009 |
| 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5) | Wi-Fi 5 | 5 GHz | 6.93 Gbps | 2014 |
| 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6/6E) | Wi-Fi 6 | 2.4/5/6 GHz | 9.6 Gbps | 2019/2021 |
| 802.11be (Wi-Fi 7) | Wi-Fi 7 | 2.4/5/6 GHz | 46 Gbps | 2024 |

#### 2.6.6 Hidden Node Problem and RTS/CTS

When stations A and C **cannot sense each other** (e.g., due to a wall) but both can communicate with B, simultaneous transmission at A and C causes a collision at B. **RTS/CTS** solves this by having A and C exchange control frames with B before sending data — all nearby stations hearing CTS defer transmission.

### 2.7 Deep Dive: IEEE 802.15 — Wireless Personal Area Network (WPAN)

- **802.15.1 — Bluetooth** (now maintained by Bluetooth SIG)
  - Range: ~10 m
  - Speed: 1–3 Mbps (Classic), up to 24 Mbps (BLE 5.x)
  - Uses **FHSS (Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum)** at 2.4 GHz
  - Master-slave topology (Piconet: 1 master + up to 7 active slaves)

- **802.15.4 — ZigBee, 6LoWPAN**
  - Range: ~100 m
  - Low power, low data rate (250 kbps)
  - Used in IoT, smart home, industrial sensors

### 2.8 Deep Dive: IEEE 802.16 — WiMAX (Wireless MAN)

- Provides broadband wireless up to **50 km** range.
- Data rates up to **75 Mbps**.
- Supports **OFDM (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing)** and **MIMO (Multiple Input Multiple Output)**.
- Designed as a last-mile broadband alternative to DSL/cable.
- Largely replaced by LTE/5G for mobile broadband.

### 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Value | Notes |
|---------|-----------------|-------|
| CSMA/CD minimum frame | $T_{\text{frame}} \geq 2 \times T_{\text{propagation}}$ | Collision detection before end of frame |
| Ethernet min frame | **64 bytes** | Includes header + payload + FCS |
| Ethernet max frame | **1518 bytes** | Excludes preamble + SFD |
| Slot time (10BASE-T) | **51.2 μs** | Equals 64-byte transmission at 10 Mbps |
| Backoff range | $k \in [0, 2^m - 1]$, $m = \min(n, 10)$ | Binary Exponential Backoff |
| Wi-Fi DIFS | $DIFS = SIFS + 2 \times SlotTime$ | IEEE 802.11 DCF |
| Wi-Fi SIFS | **10 μs** (802.11b), **16 μs** (802.11a) | Short IFS |
| Wi-Fi Max Frame Payload | **2312 bytes** | Can be fragmented |
| Wi-Fi MAC Address Length | **48 bits (6 bytes)** | Same as Ethernet |
| Token Holding Time (802.5) | **10 ms** (default) | Limits long transmissions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always express the backoff formula correctly. The $n$ represents the **number of collisions for the current frame**, not the total collisions on the network.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Ethernet Minimum Frame Size and Maximum Cable Length

The most important mathematical relationship in wired Ethernet (CSMA/CD) is that the **transmission time of the minimum frame** must be at least **twice the maximum propagation delay** of the network. This ensures that any collision is detected before the sender finishes transmitting the frame.

**Given:**
- Network bit rate: $R = 10$ Mbps (10BASE-T)
- Propagation speed in cable: $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s (typical for copper)
- Maximum cable length: $L = 500$ m (worst-case collision domain)
- Number of repeaters: typically 4 repeaters + 5 segments in 10BASE-T
- Total round-trip distance: $D_{\text{RT}} = 2 \times 2500 = 5000$ m

**Step 1: Compute one-way propagation delay**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{prop}} &= \frac{L}{v} \\
&= \frac{500}{2 \times 10^8} \\
&= 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \\
&= 2.5 \ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute round-trip propagation delay (worst case)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{RT}} &= 2 \times T_{\text{prop}} \\
&= 2 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 5 \ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

Including repeaters and end-to-end collision domain (2500 m), the worst-case round-trip is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{RT (max)}} &= \frac{2 \times 2500}{2 \times 10^8} \\
&= 25 \ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute minimum frame transmission time**

For collision detection, the frame transmission time must be at least $2 \times T_{\text{prop}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{tx (min)}} &= 2 \times T_{\text{prop (max)}} \\
&= 2 \times 25 \ \mu s \\
&= 51.2 \ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute minimum frame size in bits**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{min (bits)}} &= R \times T_{\text{tx (min)}} \\
&= 10 \times 10^6 \times 51.2 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 512 \text{ bits} \\
&= 64 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The **minimum Ethernet frame size of 64 bytes** is mathematically derived from the requirement that CSMA/CD must be able to detect collisions. This is why any payload smaller than 46 bytes (64 minus 18 bytes of header/FCS) is **padded**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Binary Exponential Backoff Probability

**Scenario:** Two stations collide. They each independently choose a backoff slot $k$.

**Step 1: After 1st collision**

Each station picks $k$ from $\{0, 1\}$ — 2 choices.
The probability both pick the **same** $k$ (another collision):
$$P_{\text{collision}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

**Step 2: After 2nd collision**

Each station picks $k$ from $\{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ — 4 choices.
$$P_{\text{collision}} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

**Step 3: Generalizing**

After $n$ collisions, each station picks $k$ from $\{0, 1, \ldots, 2^{m-1}\}$ where $m = \min(n, 10)$:

$$P_{\text{collision after } n \text{ attempts}} = \frac{1}{2^m} = \frac{1}{2^{\min(n, 10)}}$$

**Step 4: Maximum backoff**

After 10 collisions, the window freezes at 1024 slots. After **16 collisions**, the frame is **discarded** and the upper layer is notified.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Major IEEE 802 Standards (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | 802.3 Ethernet | 802.5 Token Ring | 802.11 Wi-Fi | 802.15.1 Bluetooth |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Access Method** | CSMA/CD | Token Passing | CSMA/CA | Master-slave polling |
| **Topology** | Star/Bus | Star (logical Ring) | Star (AP-centered) | Piconet (master + slaves) |
| **Medium** | Copper/Fiber | Copper | Radio (RF) | Radio (2.4 GHz FHSS) |
| **Speed** | 10 Mbps – 100 Gbps | 4 / 16 Mbps | 11 Mbps – 46 Gbps | 1–24 Mbps |
| **Range** | ~100 m (UTP) | ~100 m | ~100 m indoor | ~10 m |
| **Collision Possible?** | Yes (CD) | No (deterministic) | Possible (avoided via CA) | No (master controls) |
| **Frame Header** | 14 bytes | 21 bytes (typical) | 24–34 bytes | Variable |
| **Min Frame** | 64 bytes | — | — | — |
| **Addressing** | 48-bit MAC | 48-bit MAC | 48-bit MAC | 48-bit MAC |
| **Full Duplex?** | Yes (modern) | No | Yes (802.11n+) | No |
| **Wired/Wireless** | Wired | Wired | Wireless | Wireless |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Wi-Fi Throughput Under RTS/CTS

**Given:**
- Frame payload: $L$ bytes
- Bit rate: $R$ Mbps
- RTS, CTS, ACK sizes (in time): $t_{\text{RTS}}, t_{\text{CTS}}, t_{\text{ACK}}$
- SIFS, DIFS, slot time: known constants

**Step 1: Total time for one successful frame transmission**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= t_{\text{RTS}} + \text{SIFS} + t_{\text{CTS}} + \text{SIFS} + t_{\text{Data}} + \text{SIFS} + t_{\text{ACK}} + \text{DIFS} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Data transmission time**

$$
t_{\text{Data}} = \frac{L \times 8}{R}
$$

**Step 3: Effective throughput**

$$
S = \frac{L \times 8}{T_{\text{total}}} \quad \text{(bits per second)}
$$

**Step 4: Efficiency**

$$
\eta = \frac{t_{\text{Data}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** For small frames, RTS/CTS overhead reduces throughput significantly. For large frames, the overhead becomes negligible. The RTS threshold is typically set to **500–2347 bytes** — below it, RTS/CTS is disabled to save bandwidth.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Token Ring Token Holding Time

**Given:**
- Ring has 50 stations
- Ring latency (1-bit delay per station) = 50 × 1 = 50 bit-times
- Cable adds 250 m of propagation, speed $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s, rate $R = 16$ Mbps

**Step 1: Cable propagation delay**

$$
t_{\text{cable}} = \frac{250}{2 \times 10^8} = 1.25 \ \mu s
$$

**Step 2: Convert to bit-times**

$$
t_{\text{cable (bit-times)}} = 1.25 \times 10^{-6} \times 16 \times 10^6 = 20 \text{ bit-times}
$$

**Step 3: Total ring latency**

$$
T_{\text{ring}} = 50 + 20 = 70 \text{ bit-times}
$$

**Step 4: Maximum Token Holding Time (THT)**

IEEE 802.5 default THT = **10 ms**. In 16 Mbps:

$$
L_{\text{THT}} = 10 \times 10^{-3} \times 16 \times 10^6 = 160{,}000 \text{ bits} = 20{,}000 \text{ bytes}
$$

So a station can transmit up to 20,000 bytes per token acquisition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Use:** Token Ring THT and ring latency directly determine the **maximum effective throughput** of the network. Network designers size the ring to keep latency low while allowing sufficient THT for data frames.

---

### 3.6 Worked Example: Wi-Fi Backoff Calculation

**Given:** A station has experienced 4 collisions. Compute the possible backoff range.

**Step 1: Determine m**

$$
m = \min(n, 10) = \min(4, 10) = 4
$$

**Step 2: Backoff window size**

$$
W = 2^m = 2^4 = 16
$$

**Step 3: Backoff range**

$$
k \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 15\}
$$

**Step 4: Maximum wait time (slot = 9 μs for 802.11a/g)**

$$
T_{\text{max wait}} = 15 \times 9 = 135 \ \mu s
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** The window doubles up to 1024 (10 collisions). If a student uses $2^n$ (i.e., $2^4 = 16$ in number) but writes the **range** as $0$ to $2^n - 1$ correctly, full marks are awarded.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the IEEE 802.2 Logical Link Control (LLC) sublayer. List its three types of services.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Logical Link Control (LLC)** sublayer, defined by **IEEE 802.2**, is the upper sublayer of the Data Link Layer in the IEEE 802 reference model. It provides a **uniform interface** to the upper network layer regardless of which underlying MAC technology (Ethernet, Wi-Fi, Token Ring) is in use. Its primary functions include **multiplexing/demultiplexing of upper-layer protocols** using **Service Access Points (SAPs)**, **flow control**, and **error recovery**.

**Three LLC services are:**

1. **Type 1 — Unacknowledged Connectionless Service:** Connectionless operation with no acknowledgment. Used in **Ethernet (802.3)** and most LANs. Operates as best-effort delivery.

2. **Type 2 — Connection-Oriented Service:** Establishes a logical connection with acknowledgments, flow control, and error recovery. Rarely implemented in modern LANs.

3. **Type 3 — Acknowledged Connectionless Service:** Connectionless operation but with acknowledgment for each frame. Used in legacy systems and some industrial networks.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition 1M, Three services 1.5M, Example 0.5M = **Total 3 marks**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between CSMA/CD and CSMA/CA. Why is CSMA/CA preferred over CSMA/CD in wireless networks?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | CSMA/CD | CSMA/CA |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| **Full Form** | Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision **Detection** | Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision **Avoidance** |
| **Used In** | IEEE 802.3 Ethernet (wired) | IEEE 802.11 Wi-Fi (wireless) |
| **Action on Collision** | Detect & retransmit after backoff | Avoid via backoff + RTS/CTS |
| **Collision Mechanism** | Detected via voltage change on cable | Cannot be detected; only avoided |
| **Efficiency** | Lower for high load (collisions) | Higher for wireless media |

**Why CSMA/CA is preferred in wireless:**

In wireless networks, a station **cannot simultaneously transmit and listen** for collisions because its own transmission signal is **much stronger** than any incoming signal — the receiver is "blinded." This is called the **near-far problem**. Additionally, the **hidden node problem** prevents some stations from sensing each other, making collision detection unreliable. Therefore, **collisions are avoided** using randomized backoff, IFS hierarchy, and the **RTS/CTS handshake** before actual data transmission.

**Marks Distribution:** Comparison table 2M, Justification 1M = **Total 3 marks**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]**

**(a) Explain the IEEE 802.3 Ethernet frame format with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of each field. (7 marks)**
