**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the CSMA/CD protocol. A network using CSMA/CD has a maximum cable length of 2.5 km and operates at 10 Mbps. If the propagation speed is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s, calculate the minimum frame size required. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q1(a):

The **IEEE 802.3 Ethernet frame** consists of the following fields (sent left to right, MSB first):

| Field | Size (Bytes) | Description |
|-------|--------------|-------------|
| **Preamble** | 7 | Alternating pattern `10101010...` used for **clock synchronization** between sender and receiver. |
| **SFD** | 1 | `10101011` — marks the **start of the frame**; final two bits (`11`) signal the start. |
| **Destination MAC** | 6 | Hardware address of the **intended receiver**. Can be unicast, multicast, or broadcast (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF). |
| **Source MAC** | 6 | Hardware address of the **sender**. |
| **Length/Type** | 2 | In IEEE 802.3, this is the **length of the payload**. In Ethernet II, it is the **EtherType** (e.g., 0x0800 for IPv4). |
| **Payload (Data)** | 46–1500 | **Upper-layer data** (e.g., IP packet). If data < 46 bytes, padding is added. |
| **FCS (Frame Check Sequence)** | 4 | **CRC-32** checksum for **error detection** over the entire frame except preamble/SFD. |

**Frame Structure Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Preamble 7B] --> B[SFD 1B]
    B --> C[DA 6B]
    C --> D[SA 6B]
    D --> E[Length 2B]
    E --> F[Data 46 to 1500B]
    F --> G[FCS 4B]
```

**Key Points:**
- **Minimum frame = 64 bytes** (header 14B + data 46B + FCS 4B).
- **Maximum frame = 1518 bytes** (header 14B + data 1500B + FCS 4B).
- The preamble and SFD are not counted in the 64-byte minimum.

---

#### Model Solution to Q1(b):

**CSMA/CD Protocol Flow:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Station has data] --> B{Medium Idle?}
    B -->|No| C[Wait]
    C --> B
    B -->|Yes| D[Start Transmission]
    D --> E{Collision?}
    E -->|No| F[Success]
    E -->|Yes| G[Send Jam Signal]
    G --> H[Backoff]
    H --> B
```

**Step-by-step Operation:**

1. The station **senses the carrier** (medium) before transmitting. If the medium is **idle**, it begins transmission.
2. If the medium is **busy**, the station waits and retries later.
3. During transmission, the station **monitors for collisions** (a sudden voltage rise on the cable).
4. On collision, the station **stops transmitting immediately** and sends a **32-bit jam signal** to ensure all stations are notified.
5. The station then waits for a **random backoff time** using the **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)** algorithm before retrying.
6. After **16 collisions**, the frame is **discarded** and the upper layer is notified.

**Numerical Problem Solution:**

**Given:**
- Cable length: $L = 2.5$ km $= 2500$ m
- Bit rate: $R = 10$ Mbps
- Propagation speed: $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s

**Step 1: One-way propagation delay**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{prop}} &= \frac{L}{v} \\
&= \frac{2500}{2 \times 10^8} \\
&= 12.5 \ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Worst-case round-trip propagation delay (2 × one-way)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{RT}} &= 2 \times 12.5 \ \mu s \\
&= 25 \ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Minimum frame transmission time**

For collision detection to work, the frame must be transmitted for at least the round-trip time:

$$
T_{\text{frame(min)}} = T_{\text{RT}} = 25 \ \mu s
$$

**Step 4: Minimum frame size in bits**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{min}} &= R \times T_{\text{frame(min)}} \\
&= 10 \times 10^6 \times 25 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 250 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Convert to bytes**

$$
L_{\text{min}} = \frac{250}{8} = 31.25 \text{ bytes} \approx 32 \text{ bytes}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The minimum frame size required is approximately **32 bytes**. However, the IEEE 802.3 standard mandates a **64-byte minimum** to account for repeaters, additional propagation, and safety margins. This is why Ethernet pads frames shorter than 64 bytes.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q2]**

**(a) Explain the architecture of IEEE 802.11 Wireless LAN with a BSS and ESS. List the physical and MAC layer specifications. (7 marks)**

**(b) With a diagram, explain the RTS/CTS mechanism in IEEE 802.11. How does it solve the hidden node problem? A station experiences 3 collisions in a row. Calculate the backoff range and the maximum waiting time (slot time = 9 μs). (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q2(a):

**IEEE 802.11 Architecture:**

The IEEE 802.11 WLAN architecture consists of multiple components, organized into Basic Service Sets (BSS) and Extended Service Sets (ESS).

**Components:**

1. **Station (STA):** A wireless-equipped device (laptop, phone, IoT sensor).
2. **Access Point (AP):** A wireless base station that acts as a **bridge** between wireless STAs and the wired network.
3. **Basic Service Set (BSS):** The set of all stations that communicate with each other through a **single AP**. Identified by a **BSSID** (the AP's MAC address).
4. **Distribution System (DS):** A wired (or wireless) backbone that connects multiple APs. Typically a switched Ethernet.
5. **Extended Service Set (ESS):** Multiple BSSs interconnected via a DS. Identified by a network name (**SSID**).
6. **Portal:** Provides integration with non-802.11 networks (e.g., Internet gateway).

**Architecture Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ESS] --> B[DS Distribution System]
    B --> C[AP 1]
    B --> D[AP 2]
    C --> E[BSS 1]
    D --> F[BSS 2]
    E --> E1[STA A]
    E --> E2[STA B]
    F --> F1[STA C]
    F --> F2[STA D]
```

**Physical Layer Specifications:**

| Standard | Frequency | Modulation | Max Speed |
|----------|-----------|------------|-----------|
| 802.11b | 2.4 GHz | DSSS/CCK | 11 Mbps |
| 802.11a | 5 GHz | OFDM | 54 Mbps |
| 802.11g | 2.4 GHz | OFDM | 54 Mbps |
| 802.11n | 2.4/5 GHz | MIMO-OFDM | 600 Mbps |
| 802.11ac | 5 GHz | MU-MIMO/OFDM | 6.93 Gbps |
| 802.11ax | 2.4/5/6 GHz | OFDMA | 9.6 Gbps |

**MAC Layer Specifications:**

- **CSMA/CA** with **Binary Exponential Backoff**
- **DCF (Distributed Coordination Function)** — basic CSMA/CA
- **PCF (Point Coordination Function)** — optional, contention-free
- **RTS/CTS handshake** for hidden node avoidance
- **MAC frame format** with up to 4 address fields
- **Maximum MSDU size: 2304 bytes** (larger than Ethernet's 1500)

---

#### Model Solution to Q2(b):

**RTS/CTS Mechanism in IEEE 802.11:**

The **Request to Send / Clear to Send** (RTS/CTS) handshake is a four-way exchange used to reserve the wireless medium before transmitting data. It is an optional but **highly recommended** mechanism in 802.11.

**Step-by-step Operation:**

1. The sender waits for **DIFS** (DCF Inter-Frame Space) and a backoff counter.
2. After the medium is idle, the sender transmits a short **RTS (Request to Send)** frame containing the **duration of the upcoming transmission**.
3. The receiver waits for **SIFS** (Short Inter-Frame Space) and replies with a **CTS (Clear to Send)** frame echoing the duration.
4. All other stations that hear either the RTS or CTS set their **NAV (Network Allocation Vector)** to the indicated duration and **defer transmission**.
5. The sender waits for **SIFS** and transmits the **data frame**.
6. The receiver waits for **SIFS** and sends an **ACK (Acknowledgment)**.
7. If the sender does not receive CTS within a timeout, it **doubles the contention window** and retries.

**Sequence Diagram:**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Sender
    participant R as Receiver
    participant X as Other Station
    S->>R: RTS (duration T)
    R-->>S: CTS (duration T-d)
    Note over X: X hears CTS, sets NAV
    S->>R: Data Frame
    R-->>S: ACK
```

**How RTS/CTS Solves the Hidden Node Problem:**

Consider three stations: **A**, **B (AP)**, and **C**. A and C **cannot sense each other** (they are hidden from each other due to a physical obstacle) but both can communicate with B. Without RTS/CTS, A and C may transmit simultaneously, causing a collision at B.

**With RTS/CTS:**
- A sends RTS to B → B replies with CTS → C hears the CTS (since C is in B's range).
- C sets its NAV and defers transmission until A's exchange completes.
- Hence, even if C cannot sense A's transmission, it **respects A's reservation** based on B's CTS.

**Backoff Calculation:**

**Given:** $n = 3$ collisions, slot time = $9 \ \mu s$

**Step 1: Determine m**

$$
m = \min(n, 10) = \min(3, 10) = 3
$$

**Step 2: Backoff window**

$$
W = 2^m = 2^3 = 8
$$

**Step 3: Backoff range**

$$
k \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7\}
$$

**Step 4: Maximum waiting time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{max wait}} &= (2^m - 1) \times T_{\text{slot}} \\
&= 7 \times 9 \ \mu s \\
&= 63 \ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** The backoff range is **0 to 7 slots**, and the maximum waiting time is **63 μs**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in IEEE 802 Standards Questions:**
>
> 1. **CSMA/CD vs CSMA/CA Confusion:** Students often write "collision detection" for Wi-Fi. **Wi-Fi uses Collision AVOIDANCE because wireless stations cannot detect collisions during transmission.** The examiner deducts 1 mark for this fundamental error.
>
> 2. **Backoff Formula Error:** Writing $k \in [0, 2^n]$ instead of $k \in [0, 2^n - 1]$. Always remember the range is **0 to $2^m - 1$** where $m = \min(n, 10)$.
>
> 3. **Frame Format Confusion:** Mixing IEEE 802.3 frame format (with **Length** field) and Ethernet II frame format (with **EtherType** field). KTU questions explicitly ask for 802.3 — use the **Length** field, not EtherType.
>
> 4. **Wi-Fi Frame Address Fields:** Students often assume only 2 addresses (like Ethernet). 802.11 has **up to 4 address fields** for different frame types (BSS, WDS). Forgetting Address 4 loses marks.
>
> 5. **Slot Time vs SIFS/DIFS:** SIFS, PIFS, DIFS are **NOT equal** to slot time. SIFS < PIFS < DIFS. SIFS = 10 μs (802.11b), 16 μs (802.11a/g). DIFS = SIFS + 2 × SlotTime.
>
> 6. **Slot Time:** Students confuse the 51.2 μs slot time of 10BASE-T Ethernet with the 9 μs slot time of 802.11a/g. They are different standards — **always use the standard-specific value**.
>
> 7. **Minimum Frame Size Calculation:** Students forget to multiply by 2 for round-trip. The correct formula is $T_{\text{frame}} \geq 2 \times T_{\text{prop}}$.
>
> 8. **Token Ring:** Writing "Token Bus" instead of "Token Ring" for 802.5. They are **different standards** (802.4 vs 802.5).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick Revision Checklist for IEEE Standards for Wired and Wireless LAN:**

- ✅ **IEEE 802 family** governs Layers 1 and 2 of the OSI model for LANs and MANs.
- ✅ **IEEE 802.2 (LLC)** is common to all 802 standards and provides a uniform upper interface.
- ✅ **IEEE 802.3 (Ethernet)** uses **CSMA/CD**, has a **64-byte minimum frame**, **1518-byte maximum frame**, **48-bit MAC addresses**, and slot time of **51.2 μs** at 10 Mbps.
- ✅ **CSMA/CD Formula:** $T_{\text{frame(min)}} \geq 2 \times T_{\text{prop}}$; min frame = 64 bytes is derived from collision detection requirement.
- ✅ **Binary Exponential Backoff:** After $n$ collisions, $k \in [0, 2^{\min(n,10)} - 1]$; frame discarded after **16** collisions.
- ✅ **Ethernet Frame Fields:** Preamble (7B) + SFD (1B) + DA (6B) + SA (6B) + Length (2B) + Data (46–1500B) + FCS (4B).
- ✅ **IEEE 802.5 (Token Ring)** uses **token passing** — no collisions; physical star, logical ring; default THT = 10 ms.
- ✅ **IEEE 802.4 (Token Bus)** is for legacy industrial LANs — logical ring on physical bus.
- ✅ **IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi)** uses **CSMA/CA**, **RTS/CTS** to avoid hidden node problem, with up to **4 MAC address fields** and **DIFS/SIFS/PIFS/EIFS** spacing hierarchy.
- ✅ **Wi-Fi Frame:** Max payload 2312 bytes (can be fragmented); 48-bit MAC; FCS = CRC-32.
- ✅ **Wi-Fi Generations:** 802.11a/b/g/n/ac/ax/be (Wi-Fi 1 to 7) — speeds 11 Mbps to 46 Gbps.
- ✅ **Hidden Node Problem** solved by RTS/CTS — stations set **NAV** based on duration in CTS.
- ✅ **IEEE 802.15 (WPAN):** 802.15.1 = Bluetooth (10 m, FHSS, master-slave, 1–24 Mbps); 802.15.4 = ZigBee (IoT, 250 kbps).
- ✅ **IEEE 802.16 (WiMAX):** Long-range broadband wireless up to 50 km, up to 75 Mbps.
- ✅ **Backoff Window Size:** doubles each collision up to 1024 (after 10 collisions).
- ✅ **Collision Detection** works in **wired** because voltage change is detectable. **Collision Avoidance** is needed in **wireless** because stations cannot listen while transmitting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SCTP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET521 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SCTP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**SCTP (Stream Control Transmission Protocol)** is a reliable, message-oriented, connection-oriented transport layer protocol defined by the IETF in **RFC 4960**. It operates at the same layer as TCP and UDP but provides enhanced capabilities by combining the best features of both, while introducing novel features tailored primarily for telecommunications signaling transport (e.g., SS7 over IP) and modern data applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** SCTP is a transport layer protocol that provides acknowledged, error-free, non-duplicated transfer of messages between two endpoints, while supporting multi-streaming and multi-homing capabilities that are absent in TCP and UDP.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **telephone conference call** between two offices, where each office has **two separate phone lines** (multi-homing). The conversation is split into **two independent parallel dialogues** (multi-streaming): one line for project updates, another for billing queries. If one phone line drops, the conversation seamlessly continues on the backup line, and messages on the billing line don't get mixed up with project updates. SCTP behaves exactly like this — it manages multiple logical pathways and parallel message lanes between two communicating hosts, ensuring robust and orderly delivery.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics of SCTP

- **Connection-oriented:** Requires association setup before data transfer (similar to TCP).
- **Reliable:** Guarantees in-sequence, non-duplicated delivery through acknowledgments and retransmissions.
- **Message-oriented:** Preserves message boundaries (unlike TCP's byte-stream model).
- **Full-duplex:** Allows simultaneous bidirectional data flow.
- **Unicast communication:** Supports one-to-one communication only.
- **Flow and congestion control:** Similar to TCP mechanisms.
- **32-bit checksum:** Uses **CRC-32c (Castagnoli polynomial 0x1EDC6F41)** for stronger error detection than TCP's 16-bit checksum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics & Constants:**
> - **Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU)** of an SCTP packet: **1500 bytes** (typical Ethernet default)
> - **Common Header size:** **12 bytes**
> - **Verification Tag:** 32-bit secret (anti-spoofing)
> - **Maximum Streams per association:** **65535** (16-bit Stream ID)
> - **State cookie size:** Limited to MTU to prevent memory exhaustion attacks

### 1.4 Why SCTP Was Developed

SCTP was originally designed to transport **SS7 (Signaling System No. 7)** telephony signaling messages over IP networks. Traditional protocols were inadequate:
- **TCP** is reliable but head-of-line blocking occurs in byte-stream.
- **UDP** is fast but unreliable.
- SCTP solves both problems with multi-streaming and reliability.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 SCTP Architecture: The Four Pillars

#### Pillar 1: **Multi-Streaming**
Multiple independent logical channels (streams) exist within a single association. Head-of-line blocking is contained within a single stream — others remain unaffected.

- Each stream is identified by a **16-bit Stream Identifier (SI)**
- **Maximum streams per association:** 65535
- Sequence numbers are per-stream (16-bit SSN — Stream Sequence Number)

#### Pillar 2: **Multi-Homing**
Each endpoint can have **multiple IP addresses**. If one path fails, traffic seamlessly fails over to an alternate path.

- Supports primary + alternate path selection
- Path MTU discovery is performed per destination address
- Heartbeat chunks monitor path liveness

#### Pillar 3: **Message-Oriented Framing**
SCTP preserves **message boundaries** — each `send()` corresponds to one DATA chunk.

#### Pillar 4: **Cookie-Based Association (4-way handshake)**
Prevents **SYN flood DoS attacks** by deferring state allocation until the third packet arrives with a verified cookie.

### 2.2 SCTP Common Header (Packet Format)

Every SCTP packet begins with a fixed **12-byte common header**:

| Field | Size (bits) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Source Port Number | 16 | Sender's port |
| Destination Port Number | 16 | Receiver's port |
| Verification Tag | 32 | Secret tag for association validation |
| Checksum | 32 | CRC-32c of the entire packet |

### 2.3 SCTP Chunk Format

After the common header, one or more **chunks** follow. Each chunk has:

| Field | Size (bits) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Chunk Type | 8 | Identifies chunk (DATA, INIT, SACK, etc.) |
| Chunk Flags | 8 | Type-specific flags |
| Chunk Length | 16 | Length of chunk in bytes (including header) |
| Chunk Data | Variable | Type-specific payload |

### 2.4 SCTP Association Establishment — 4-Way Handshake

| Step | Direction | Chunk Exchanged | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Client → Server | **INIT** | Initiate association; propose parameters |
| 2 | Server → Client | **INIT-ACK** | Accept parameters; send cookie |
| 3 | Client → Server | **COOKIE-ECHO** | Echo back the cookie |
| 4 | Server → Client | **COOKIE-ACK** | Confirm cookie received; association established |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 4-way instead of TCP's 3-way?**
> State is allocated on the **server only after COOKIE-ECHO** arrives, so attackers cannot exhaust server memory by sending spoofed INIT packets (mitigates SYN flood).

### 2.5 SCTP Association Termination — 3-Way Handshake

| Step | Direction | Chunk Exchanged |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initiator → Peer | **SHUTDOWN** |
| 2 | Peer → Initiator | **SHUTDOWN-ACK** |
| 3 | Initiator → Peer | **SHUTDOWN-COMPLETE** |

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas and constants below are essential for KTU 2024 ESE numerical and short-answer questions.

| Concept | Formula / Constant | Description |
|---|---|---|
| SCTP Common Header Size | $H_{common} = 12 \text{ bytes}$ | Fixed header size |
| DATA Chunk Header Size | $H_{data} = 16 \text{ bytes}$ | 4-byte TLV header $\mid$ TSN (4) $\mid$ SI (2) $\mid$ SSN (2) $\mid$ PPID (4) |
| Total Packet Size | $P_{total} = H_{common} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} (H_{chunk,i} + D_i)$ | Sum of all chunk sizes |
| Effective Throughput | $\eta = \dfrac{D_{payload}}{P_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Header overhead ratio |
| Checksum Polynomial | $G(x) = x^{32} + x^{28} + x^{27} + x^{26} + x^{25} + x^{23} + x^{22} + x^{20} + x^{19} + x^{18} + x^{14} + x^{13} + x^{11} + x^{10} + x^{9} + x^{8} + x^{6} + 1$ | CRC-32c Castagnoli |
| Verification Tag | $V \in [0, 2^{32} - 1]$ | Random per-association secret |
| Maximum Streams | $N_{streams} = 2^{16} = 65535$ | Per association |
| Congestion Window | $cwnd$ | Sender-side, similar to TCP |
| Receiver Window | $rwnd$ | Advertised in SACK chunk |
| Path Selection | Use primary $\rightarrow$ failover to alternate | Multi-homing logic |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** 4G/5G core networks, VoLTE signaling
- **Financial Trading:** Reliable, low-latency market data feeds
- **Web Servers:** HTTP/3 (QUIC) uses SCTP-inspired concepts
- **Defense & Aerospace:** Mission-critical reliable messaging
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: SCTP Packet Size Calculation

**Problem Statement:** An SCTP packet contains one DATA chunk with 800 bytes of user payload. Calculate the total packet size and overhead percentage.

**Step 1: Identify fixed components**

$$H_{common} = 12 \text{ bytes (SCTP common header)}$$

**Step 2: Calculate DATA chunk size**

$$H_{data\_chunk} = 16 \text{ bytes (DATA chunk header fields)}$$

$$D_{payload} = 800 \text{ bytes (user message)}$$

**Step 3: Sum the packet size**

$$P_{total} = H_{common} + H_{data\_chunk} + D_{payload}$$

$$P_{total} = 12 + 16 + 800$$

$$P_{total} = 828 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 4: Compute header overhead ratio**

$$\eta = \dfrac{H_{common} + H_{data\_chunk}}{P_{total}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{12 + 16}{828} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{28}{828} \times 100\% \approx 3.38\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** SCTP's overhead is comparable to TCP (when message boundaries are added) but with added benefits of multi-streaming and multi-homing.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum DATA Payload Calculation

**Given:** MTU = 1500 bytes (typical Ethernet).  
**Find:** Maximum user payload per DATA chunk.

**Step 1: Reserve space for common header**

$$P_{available} = MTU - H_{common}$$

$$P_{available} = 1500 - 12 = 1488 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2: Reserve space for DATA chunk header**

$$D_{max} = P_{available} - H_{data\_chunk}$$

$$D_{max} = 1488 - 16 = 1472 \text{ bytes}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Comparison Matrix — TCP vs UDP vs SCTP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most frequently asked analytical question in KTU exams.** Memorize this matrix completely.

| Feature | TCP | UDP | SCTP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Connection-oriented | Yes | No | Yes |
| Reliable | Yes | No | Yes |
| Message boundaries preserved | No (byte stream) | Yes | Yes |
| Ordered delivery | Yes | No | Optional (per stream) |
| Flow control | Yes | No | Yes |
| Congestion control | Yes | No | Yes |
| Multi-streaming | No | No | **Yes** |
| Multi-homing | No | No | **Yes** |
| Header size | 20 bytes | 8 bytes | 12 bytes (common) |
| Checksum bits | 16 | 16 | **32 (CRC-32c)** |
| Handshake type | 3-way | None | **4-way** |
| Primary use case | Web, email | DNS, video streaming | Telephony, signaling |

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Verification Tag Anti-Spoofing Logic

**Mathematical Basis of Verification Tag:**

$$V_{tag} = \text{Random value chosen at INIT}$$

**Validation Rule at Receiver:**

$$\text{Accept packet} \iff V_{received} = V_{expected}$$

- If $V_{received} \neq V_{expected}$, the packet is silently discarded.
- Protects against **blind injection attacks** by off-path attackers.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: SCTP Chunk Types

| Chunk Type (Hex) | Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 0x00 | DATA | Carries user payload |
| 0x01 | INIT | Initiate association |
| 0x02 | INIT-ACK | Acknowledge INIT + cookie |
| 0x03 | SACK | Selective acknowledgment |
| 0x04 | HEARTBEAT | Path liveness probe |
| 0x05 | HEARTBEAT-ACK | Acknowledge heartbeat |
| 0x06 | ABORT | Abnormal termination |
| 0x07 | SHUTDOWN | Graceful close request |
| 0x08 | SHUTDOWN-ACK | Acknowledge shutdown |
| 0x09 | SHUTDOWN-COMPLETE | Final close |
| 0x0A | COOKIE-ECHO | Echo the cookie |
| 0x0B | COOKIE-ACK | Confirm cookie |
| 0x0E | FORWARD-TSN | Stream reset / TSN advance |
| 0x0F | AUTH | Authentication extension |

### 3.6 SCTP State Transition Diagram Analysis

SCTP has **8 states** (per endpoint):

$$\text{CLOSED} \rightarrow \text{COOKIE\_WAIT} \rightarrow \text{COOKIE\_ECHOED} \rightarrow \text{ESTABLISHED}$$

$$\text{ESTABLISHED} \rightarrow \text{SHUTDOWN\_PENDING} \rightarrow \text{SHUTDOWN\_SENT} \rightarrow \text{SHUTDOWN\_RECEIVED} \rightarrow \text{SHUTDOWN\_ACK\_SENT} \rightarrow \text{CLOSED}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The state COOKIE_WAIT exists on the client only after sending INIT and before receiving INIT-ACK. The state COOKIE_ECHOED is reached after the client sends COOKIE-ECHO. The server transitions directly from CLOSED to ESTABLISHED upon validating the cookie.

### 3.7 Step-by-Step: 4-Way Handshake with Cookie

1. **Client sends INIT:** Contains Initiate Tag, A-RWND, Number of Outbound Streams (OS), Number of Inbound Streams (MIS), Initial TSN.
2. **Server responds with INIT-ACK:** Contains server's Initiate Tag, A-RWND, OS, MIS, Initial TSN, and a **state cookie** (encrypted with server's secret key).
3. **Client sends COOKIE-ECHO:** Wraps the received cookie inside this chunk.
4. **Server validates cookie:** Allocates TCB (Transmission Control Block) → Sends COOKIE-ACK.

**Mathematical Expression for State Allocation:**

$$\text{Memory Allocation} = \begin{cases} 0, & \text{on INIT reception} \\ \text{Full TCB}, & \text{on COOKIE-ECHO with valid cookie} \end{cases}$$

This deferred state allocation is the **core defense** against TCP SYN flood attacks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SCTP Architecture: Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Application Layer] --> B1[SCTP Layer]
    B1 --> C1[Multi-Stream Engine]
    B1 --> C2[Multi-Homing Engine]
    B1 --> C3[Reliability and Retransmission]
    B1 --> C4[Congestion Control]
    B1 --> C5[Flow Control]
    B1 --> D1[SCTP Common Header 12 bytes]
    D1 --> E1[IP Network]
    E1 --> F1[Receiver SCTP Layer]
    F1 --> G1[Verification Tag Check]
    G1 --> G2[CRC 32c Checksum Verification]
    G2 --> G3[Chunk Demultiplexer]
    G3 --> G4[DATA Chunk Handler]
    G3 --> G5[SACK Chunk Handler]
    G3 --> G6[Control Chunk Handler]
    G4 --> H1[Application Layer Receiver]
    G5 --> H1
    G6 --> H1
```

### 4.2 SCTP 4-Way Handshake Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant S as Server

    Note over C: CLOSED State
    Note over S: CLOSED State

    C->>S: INIT chunk Initiate Tag
    Note over C: COOKIE_WAIT State
    Note over S: COOKIE_WAIT (no TCB allocated)

    S->>C: INIT-ACK chunk with State Cookie
    Note over C: COOKIE_ECHOED (after sending cookie-echo)
    Note over S: ESTABLISHED (after validating cookie)

    C->>S: COOKIE-ECHO chunk echoes the cookie
    S->>C: COOKIE-ACK chunk
    Note over C: ESTABLISHED
    Note over S: ESTABLISHED
```

### 4.3 SCTP Packet Structure (Common Header + Chunks)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Source Port 16 bits] --> P2[Destination Port 16 bits]
    P2 --> P3[Verification Tag 32 bits]
    P3 --> P4[Checksum 32 bits CRC 32c]
    P4 --> P5[Chunk 1 Type 8 bits]
    P5 --> P6[Chunk 1 Flags 8 bits]
    P6 --> P7[Chunk 1 Length 16 bits]
    P7 --> P8[Chunk 1 Data Variable]
    P8 --> P9[Chunk 2 ... ]
    P9 --> P10[Chunk N ... ]
```

### 4.4 SCTP DATA Chunk Detailed Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Type 8 bits value 0x00] --> D2[Reserved Flags 8 bits U B E bits]
    D2 --> D3[Length 16 bits]
    D3 --> D4[TSN Transmission Sequence Number 32 bits]
    D4 --> D5[Stream Identifier SI 16 bits]
    D5 --> D6[Stream Sequence Number SSN 16 bits]
    D6 --> D7[Payload Protocol Identifier PPID 32 bits]
    D7 --> D8[User Data Variable]
```

### 4.5 SCTP Multi-Homing Failover Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Endpoint has two IP addresses IP1 and IP2] --> Check{Is IP1 reachable Heartbeat OK}
    Check -- Yes --> Path1[Send traffic via IP1 Primary Path]
    Check -- No --> Switch[Failover to IP2 Alternate Path]
    Switch --> Update[Update primary path = IP2]
    Update --> Monitor[Continue heartbeat monitoring on IP1]
    Monitor --> Check
    Path1 --> Monitor
```

### 4.6 SCTP Multi-Streaming Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Application Layer] --> B[SCTP Association]
    B --> S1[Stream 0 Orderly Data]
    B --> S2[Stream 1 Bulk Data]
    B --> S3[Stream 2 Control Messages]
    S1 --> N1[Independent in order delivery]
    S2 --> N2[Independent delivery loss isolated]
    S3 --> N3[Independent delivery]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three features of SCTP that are not present in TCP.**

**Model Answer:**

Three features of SCTP that are not present in TCP are:

1. **Multi-Streaming:** SCTP allows multiple independent streams of data within a single association. Head-of-line blocking is restricted to one stream; other streams continue delivering data even if one is blocked. TCP has only a single byte stream.

2. **Multi-Homing:** Each SCTP endpoint can be associated with multiple IP addresses. This provides path redundancy and automatic failover in case of network failure. TCP supports only a single IP address per endpoint.

3. **Message-Oriented Framing:** SCTP preserves message boundaries — each `send()` call corresponds to one DATA chunk at the receiver. TCP is byte-stream oriented and does not preserve message boundaries.

4. (Bonus point) **32-bit CRC-32c Checksum:** Stronger error detection than TCP's 16-bit checksum.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the role of the Verification Tag in an SCTP packet?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Verification Tag (VT)** is a 32-bit secret value present in the common header of every SCTP packet. Its roles are:

1. **Association Validation:** It validates that the received packet belongs to the correct association.
2. **Anti-Spoofing Protection:** An off-path attacker cannot inject valid packets into an existing SCTP association because they do not know the randomly chosen VT.
3. **Reception Rule:** The receiver silently discards any packet where the received VT does not match the expected VT for that association.

The VT is randomly chosen at association establishment and is exchanged during the INIT/INIT-ACK handshake.

---

### 📕 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **SCTP 4-way handshake** used for association establishment. Compare it with TCP's 3-way handshake. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **SCTP common header format** and the **DATA chunk format** in detail. List the major chunk types used in SCTP. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION (Question A)

### Part (a) — SCTP 4-Way Handshake

The SCTP 4-way handshake is used to establish an association between a **client** and **server**. The process involves four messages:

1. **INIT (Client → Server):** The client initiates the association by sending an INIT chunk. It contains:
   - Initiate Tag
   - Advertised Receiver Window Credit (a-rwnd)
   - Number of Outbound Streams (OS)
   - Number of Inbound Streams (MIS)
   - Initial TSN

2. **INIT-ACK (Server → Client):** The server accepts the proposed parameters and sends an INIT-ACK chunk, which also includes a **State Cookie** (a secure, opaque value containing all the state needed to establish the association, encrypted with a server secret).

3. **COOKIE-ECHO (Client → Server):** The client echoes back the cookie received from the server in a COOKIE-ECHO chunk.

4. **COOKIE-ACK (Server → Client):** The server validates the cookie, allocates the Transmission Control Block (TCB), and acknowledges with a COOKIE-ACK chunk. The association is now **ESTABLISHED**.

**Diagram (also shown as Mermaid in SECTION 4):**

```
Client                   Server
  |                        |
  |------- INIT ---------->|
  |                        |
  |<------ INIT-ACK -------|
  |       (with cookie)    |
  |                        |
  |---- COOKIE-ECHO ------>|
  |                        |
  |<---- COOKIE-ACK -------|
  |                        |
  ESTABLISHED         ESTABLISHED
```

**Comparison with TCP 3-Way Handshake:**

| Feature | TCP 3-Way | SCTP 4-Way |
|---|---|---|
| Number of messages | 3 (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) | 4 (INIT, INIT-ACK, COOKIE-ECHO, COOKIE-ACK) |
| State allocation | At SYN reception | At COOKIE-ECHO (deferred) |
| SYN flood resistance | Vulnerable | Resistant (cookie-based) |
| Carries parameters | Limited | Rich (streams, OS, MIS, TSN) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 4-way?** Deferred state allocation — the server does not allocate resources until the COOKIE-ECHO is received, defeating SYN flood attacks.

### Part (b) — SCTP Common Header and DATA Chunk

**SCTP Common Header (12 bytes):**

| Field | Size | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Source Port | 16 bits | Sender's port |
| Destination Port | 16 bits | Receiver's port |
| Verification Tag | 32 bits | Anti-spoofing secret |
| Checksum | 32 bits | CRC-32c error detection |

**SCTP DATA Chunk Format (16 bytes header + variable payload):**

| Field | Size | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Type | 8 bits | 0x00 for DATA |
| Reserved/Flags (U, B, E) | 8 bits | U=Unordered, B=Beginning, E=Ending |
| Length | 16 bits | Chunk length |
| TSN | 32 bits | Transmission Sequence Number |
| Stream ID (SI) | 16 bits | Stream identifier |
| Stream Seq Num (SSN) | 16 bits | Sequence within stream |
| Payload Protocol ID (PPID) | 32 bits | Application identifier |
| User Data | Variable | Application payload |

**Major SCTP Chunk Types:**

- **0x00 DATA** – Carries user payload
- **0x01 INIT** – Initiation
- **0x02 INIT-ACK** – Initiation acknowledgement
- **0x03 SACK** – Selective acknowledgement
- **0x04 HEARTBEAT** – Path liveness
- **0x05 HEARTBEAT-ACK** – Heartbeat response
- **0x06 ABORT** – Abnormal termination
- **0x07 SHUTDOWN** – Graceful close
- **0x08 SHUTDOWN-ACK** – Close acknowledgement
- **0x0A COOKIE-ECHO** – Cookie returned
- **0x0B COOKIE-ACK** – Cookie confirmed

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **multi-streaming** and **multi-homing** features of SCTP. How do they improve upon TCP? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **SCTP association termination** process. Compare SCTP with TCP and UDP in tabular form. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION (Question B)

### Part (a) — Multi-Streaming and Multi-Homing

**Multi-Streaming:**
SCTP allows multiple independent logical streams within a single association. Each stream has its own 16-bit Stream Sequence Number (SSN), allowing independent in-sequence delivery per stream. Loss in one stream does not block delivery in others (no head-of-line blocking across streams). Maximum 65535 streams per association. SCTP can deliver messages either **ordered** or **unordered** (U-flag in DATA chunk).

**Multi-Homing:**
Each SCTP endpoint can be bound to **multiple IP addresses**. This provides:
- **Path Redundancy:** Backup path available if primary fails
- **Automatic Failover:** Heartbeat chunks monitor path liveness; failure of primary triggers switch to alternate
- **Resilience:** Critical for telecom and mission-critical applications

**Improvement over TCP:**
TCP supports only **one IP address per endpoint** and **one byte stream**. SCTP's multi-homing and multi-streaming provide:
1. **Higher availability** through redundant paths
2. **Lower latency** by avoiding head-of-line blocking
3. **Better throughput** in lossy networks
4. **Enhanced reliability** for real-time signaling

### Part (b) — Association Termination and Comparison

**SCTP Association Termination (3-Way Graceful Shutdown):**

```
Initiator                  Peer
  |                         |
  |------- SHUTDOWN ------->|
  |                         |
  |<----- SHUTDOWN-ACK -----|
  |                         |
  |--- SHUTDOWN-COMPLETE -->|
  |                         |
  CLOSED                CLOSED
```

1. **SHUTDOWN:** Initiator sends SHUTDOWN chunk indicating the last cumulative TSN it will send.
2. **SHUTDOWN-ACK:** Peer acknowledges after delivering all pending data.
3. **SHUTDOWN-COMPLETE:** Initiator confirms and the association closes.

**SCTP vs TCP vs UDP Comparison:**

| Feature | TCP | UDP | SCTP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Connection type | Connection-oriented | Connectionless | Connection-oriented |
| Reliability | Reliable | Unreliable | Reliable |
| Message boundary | No (byte stream) | Yes | Yes |
| Multi-streaming | No | No | **Yes** |
| Multi-homing | No | No | **Yes** |
| Flow control | Yes | No | Yes |
| Congestion control | Yes | No | Yes |
| Header size | 20 bytes | 8 bytes | 12 bytes (common) |
| Checksum bits | 16 | 16 | **32** |
| Handshake | 3-way | None | **4-way** |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing SCTP common header with chunk header** — they are different. Common header is fixed 12 bytes; chunk header is 4 bytes plus variable data.
> 2. **Forgetting the verification tag's anti-spoofing role** — not just an identifier, but a security feature.
> 3. **Mistaking 4-way handshake as redundant** — emphasize cookie-based deferred state allocation.
> 4. **Confusing "stream" in SCTP with "byte stream" in TCP** — SCTP streams are independent message channels, not a single byte stream.
> 5. **Not mentioning CRC-32c** — examiners specifically expect the Castagnoli polynomial reference.
> 6. **Missing the role of heartbeat chunks in multi-homing** — these are what trigger failover.
> 7. **Not drawing the state diagram** — many students skip it and lose 2-3 marks.

---

### 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **SCTP = Stream Control Transmission Protocol (RFC 4960)**
- **Layer:** Transport layer (same as TCP, UDP)
- **Common Header Size:** **12 bytes** (Src Port + Dst Port + VT + Checksum)
- **Verification Tag:** 32-bit anti-spoofing value
- **Checksum:** **CRC-32c** (32-bit, stronger than TCP's 16-bit)
- **4-Way Handshake:** INIT → INIT-ACK → COOKIE-ECHO → COOKIE-ACK
- **3-Way Termination:** SHUTDOWN → SHUTDOWN-ACK → SHUTDOWN-COMPLETE
- **Multi-Streaming:** Multiple independent streams; up to **65535**
- **Multi-Homing:** Multiple IP addresses; failover via HEARTBEAT monitoring
- **Message-Oriented:** Preserves message boundaries (unlike TCP)
- **Deferred State Allocation:** Defeats SYN flood attacks (SYN = COOKIE-ECHO with valid cookie)
- **DATA Chunk Header:** 16 bytes; includes TSN, SI, SSN, PPID, U/B/E flags
- **Major Chunks:** DATA (0x00), INIT (0x01), INIT-ACK (0x02), SACK (0x03), HEARTBEAT (0x04), COOKIE-ECHO (0x0A), COOKIE-ACK (0x0B)
- **Unordered delivery** possible via U-flag in DATA chunk
- **Flow control** uses a-rwnd (advertised receiver window) in SACK
- **Congestion control** uses cwnd similar to TCP
- **Max payload per SCTP packet** on Ethernet = **1472 bytes** (1500 MTU − 28 overhead)
- **Path MTU Discovery** is supported and recommended
- **Primary use cases:** Telecom signaling (SS7 over IP), VoLTE, financial trading, mission-critical reliable messaging
- **Related RFCs:** RFC 4960 (core), RFC 4895 (AUTH chunk), RFC 6525 (I-DATA), RFC 5061 (dynamic address reconfiguration)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Congestion Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET521 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Congestion Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Congestion** is formally defined in the KTU 2024 syllabus as the situation that arises when the **demand for network resources (such as bandwidth, buffer space, and processing capacity)** exceeds the **available capacity** of the network, resulting in a degradation of overall network performance.

In data communication and computer networking, **Congestion Control** refers to the set of mechanisms, algorithms, and protocols employed by networks and transport layers to **prevent, avoid, and recover from congestion** — thereby maintaining acceptable Quality of Service (QoS) levels, minimizing packet loss, and ensuring fairness among competing data flows.

Mathematically, congestion occurs when the **total arrival rate** of packets at a node or link exceeds its **service rate (departure rate)**, causing the queue length to grow unboundedly over time.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Highway Analogy** 🚗
> Imagine a **single-lane bridge** that can comfortably allow **100 cars per minute** to pass through. During morning rush hour, however, **300 cars per minute** attempt to cross. The result? A massive traffic jam, increased waiting time, frustrated drivers, and occasional minor collisions (analogous to packet drops).
> 
> **Why does this happen?**
> - The bridge's *bandwidth* (1 lane) is fixed.
> - Cars arrive faster than they can leave.
> - Once the buffer (waiting area) fills, new cars cannot even enter.
> - This is **CONGESTION**.
> 
> **Congestion Control** is the system of traffic lights, speed limits, alternative route suggestions, and police deployment that keeps traffic flowing smoothly. In networks, these are algorithms like **Slow Start, AIMD (Additive Increase Multiplicative Decrease), RED, Leaky Bucket, and Token Bucket**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

> [!TIP]
> **Key Performance Metrics in Congestion Control (Bold Constants for Memory):**
> - **Throughput (T)** — measured in **bits per second (bps)** or **packets per second (pps)**.
> - **Delay (D)** — measured in **seconds (s)** or **milliseconds (ms)**.
> - **Jitter** — variation in delay, also in **ms**.
> - **Packet Loss Ratio (PLR)** — a **dimensionless ratio** between 0 and 1.
> - **Goodput** — useful data delivered per unit time, **bps**.
> - **Buffer Occupancy (B)** — measured in **bytes** or **packets**.
> - **RTT (Round Trip Time)** — measured in **ms**.
> - **CWND (Congestion Window Size)** — measured in **segments** or **bytes**.

### 1.4 Congestion vs. Flow Control

| Aspect | Congestion Control | Flow Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | End-to-End (Network-wide) | Point-to-Point (Sender ↔ Receiver) |
| **Goal** | Prevents network overload | Prevents receiver buffer overflow |
| **Mechanism** | Adjusts CWND, uses RED, etc. | Uses Receive Window (rwnd) in TCP |
| **Trigger** | Network congestion signals (loss, delay) | Receiver buffer full (rwnd = 0) |
| **KTU Emphasis** | High — core module topic | Tangential — covered in TCP fundamentals |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Favourite Question:** "Differentiate between Congestion Control and Flow Control" — appears almost every year as a 3-mark Part A question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Causes of Congestion (Structured Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Congestion does NOT occur from a single cause. KTU examiners expect students to identify **multi-layered root causes**.

- **Bandwidth Mismatch:** When output link capacity $\lt$ sum of input link capacities. Example: Four T1 links (1.544 Mbps each) feeding into one T1 link.
- **Heterogeneous Devices:** Routers/switches with different processing speeds cause queueing at the slower node.
- **Bursty Traffic:** Statistical multiplexing means that even if average load is acceptable, instantaneous peaks cause congestion.
- **Lack of Buffer Memory:** Finite queue lengths lead to packet drops when bursts occur.
- **Slow Processing at Routers:** CPU-bound operations (routing table lookups, encryption) create bottlenecks.
- **Retransmission Storm:** Lost packets trigger retransmissions, which *worsen* the existing congestion — a **positive feedback loop**.

### 2.2 Effects of Congestion

The classic performance curve shows that beyond a certain **Knee** point, throughput degrades sharply and delay skyrockets.

- **Increased Packet Delay** (queueing delay dominates).
- **Increased Packet Loss** (buffer overflows).
- **Decreased Throughput** (despite more packets being sent).
- **Wasted Retransmissions** (bandwidth consumed by duplicates).
- **Possible Congestion Collapse** (the network delivers almost no useful data despite high load).

### 2.3 The "Why" and "How" — Closed-Loop Congestion Control

> [!NOTE]
> Closed-loop congestion control is the most tested concept in KTU Module 3. It operates in **4 cyclic steps**.

1. **Monitor:** Network metrics (delay, loss, queue occupancy) are continuously observed.
2. **Detect:** Congestion is identified when monitored metrics cross thresholds (e.g., average queue length $\gt Q_{max}$).
3. **Signal:** Feedback is sent to affected nodes (choke packets, ECN bits, ICMP Source Quench).
4. **Adjust:** Source rate is reduced using techniques like **AIMD**.

### 2.4 Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop Comparison

| Aspect | Open-Loop (Preventive) | Closed-Loop (Reactive) |
|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Prevents congestion proactively | Detects and corrects reactively |
| **Decisions** | Made at connection setup or by policy | Made during transmission based on feedback |
| **Examples** | Leaky Bucket, Token Bucket, Traffic Policing | TCP Reno, RED, Choke Packets |
| **Responsiveness** | Fixed, deterministic | Adaptive, dynamic |
| **KTU Tag** | "Prevention better than cure" | "Monitor, Detect, Signal, Adjust" |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> This table contains all high-yield formulas tested in KTU ESE Module 3. Memorize with units.

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{max} = \dfrac{Window\,Size}{RTT}$ | Maximum throughput with window-based control | bits/sec |
| $CWND_{new} = CWND_{old} + MSS$ | Slow Start (per ACK) | segments |
| $CWND_{new} = CWND_{old} + \dfrac{MSS^2}{CWND_{old}}$ | Congestion Avoidance (AIMD) | segments |
| $CWND_{new} = \dfrac{CWND_{old}}{2}$ | Multiplicative Decrease on loss | segments |
| $ssthresh = \dfrac{CWND_{old}}{2}$ | New threshold after timeout | segments |
| $B_{token} = \rho \cdot T$ | Token bucket accumulation | tokens |
| $Token\,Count = \min(C + \rho \Delta t,\, B)$ | Token bucket update rule | tokens |
| $PLR = \dfrac{P_{lost}}{P_{sent}}$ | Packet loss ratio | dimensionless |
| $\bar{q} = (1 - w_q) \cdot \bar{q}_{old} + w_q \cdot q$ | RED average queue length | packets |
| $P_{drop} = \dfrac{max_p \cdot (q - min_{th})}{max_{th} - min_{th}}$ | RED drop probability | dimensionless |
| $Throughput_{util} = \dfrac{Used\,BW}{Total\,BW} \times 100$ | Bandwidth utilization | percent |

> **Note:** In the table above, mathematical notation like $\dfrac{x}{y}$ has been used instead of the vertical pipe symbol to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Internet Routers:** Implement RED/Adaptive RED (ARED) to prevent global synchronization.
- **Data Centers:** Use Explicit Congestion Notification (ECN) in TCP DCTCP for low-latency cloud computing.
- **Telecom Networks:** Apply Token Bucket and Leaky Bucket at ingress edges to enforce SLAs.
- **5G/6G Networks:** AI-based predictive congestion control replaces classical AIMD in some implementations.
- **Satellite Networks:** Use open-loop FEC (Forward Error Correction) as a congestion prevention technique.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Throughput Bound from Window Size and RTT

> **Objective:** Derive the maximum achievable throughput for a window-based flow control protocol.

**Step 1 — Definition of Throughput.**

Throughput is defined as the amount of useful data successfully delivered per unit time. If a window of $W$ bits is acknowledged in one round trip, then:

$$
T = \dfrac{W}{RTT}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitution of Units.**

$W$ is measured in bits, and $RTT$ in seconds. The resulting unit is bits per second, which is the SI unit of throughput.

**Step 3 — Bound for Maximum Throughput.**

Since the window size $W$ cannot exceed the receiver's buffer $R$ nor the congestion window $C$, the true bound is:

$$
T_{max} = \dfrac{\min(R,\, C)}{RTT}
$$

**Step 4 — Pipelining Effect.**

If multiple windows are in transit simultaneously (pipelining), effective throughput scales with the number of in-flight windows $N$:

$$
T_{effective} = \dfrac{N \cdot W}{RTT}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: AIMD Convergence to Fairness

> **Objective:** Prove that Additive Increase Multiplicative Decrease (AIMD) leads to **convergence** and **fairness** among competing TCP flows.

**Step 1 — Initial Setup.**

Let two flows share a bottleneck of capacity $C$. Let $w_1$ and $w_2$ be their congestion windows. The sum equals capacity at equilibrium:

$$
w_1 + w_2 = C
$$

**Step 2 — Additive Increase Phase.**

Each RTT, both flows increase their window by $a$ units (additive):

$$
w_1^{new} = w_1 + a
$$

$$
w_2^{new} = w_2 + a
$$

This phase continues until the sum exceeds capacity, triggering loss.

**Step 3 — Multiplicative Decrease Phase.**

Upon detecting loss, each flow reduces its window by a factor of $b$ (multiplicative), where $0 \lt b \lt 1$:

$$
w_1^{new} = b \cdot w_1
$$

$$
w_2^{new} = b \cdot w_2
$$

**Step 4 — Convergence Proof.**

After one cycle of increase and decrease, the new sum is:

$$
S^{new} = b \cdot (w_1 + w_2) = b \cdot C
$$

For convergence, $S^{new} \lt C$, which is satisfied when $b \lt 1$. This proves AIMD is **stable**.

**Step 5 — Fairness Proof.**

The difference between windows evolves as:

$$
\Delta^{new} = b \cdot \Delta
$$

Since $\vert b \vert \lt 1$, the difference shrinks each cycle, proving **convergence to fairness**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Token Bucket Burst Size

> **Objective:** Calculate the maximum burst a token bucket can absorb.

**Step 1 — Token Bucket Parameters.**

- $C$ = bucket capacity (in tokens)
- $\rho$ = token generation rate (tokens/sec)
- $\Delta t$ = idle duration (sec)

**Step 2 — Token Accumulation.**

After $\Delta t$ seconds of silence, the bucket contains:

$$
Tokens = \min(C + \rho \cdot \Delta t,\, C)
$$

Since bucket capacity is bounded by $C$, accumulated tokens are capped at $C$.

**Step 3 — Maximum Burst Output.**

The sender can immediately transmit at full link rate $L$ for duration $T_{burst}$:

$$
T_{burst} = \dfrac{C}{L}
$$

**Step 4 — Data Equivalent.**

The maximum data burst in bits is:

$$
B_{max} = T_{burst} \cdot L = C \, \text{tokens}
$$

This proves that **bucket capacity $C$ directly determines burst tolerance**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: TCP Congestion Control Variants

| Variant | Slow Start | Congestion Avoidance | Fast Retransmit | Fast Recovery | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TCP Tahoe** | Yes | Yes | No (resets to 1 MSS) | No | High (3 marks) |
| **TCP Reno** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Very High (14 marks) |
| **TCP NewReno** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Modified (partial ACK) | Medium |
| **TCP BIC** | Optimized | Binary search | Yes | Yes | Low |
| **TCP CUBIC** | Yes | Cubic function | Yes | Yes | Low |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Slow Start Phases

> **Problem:** MSS = 1 KB, ssthresh = 8 KB. Plot CWND vs RTT for the first 6 RTTs.

**Step 1 — RTT 1 (Slow Start Phase, since 1 KB $\lt$ 8 KB):**

$$
CWND_1 = 1 + 1 = 2 \, \text{KB}
$$

**Step 2 — RTT 2:**

$$
CWND_2 = 2 + 2 = 4 \, \text{KB}
$$

**Step 3 — RTT 3:**

$$
CWND_3 = 4 + 4 = 8 \, \text{KB}
$$

Now $CWND_3 = ssthresh$. The next RTT switches to **Congestion Avoidance**.

**Step 4 — RTT 4 (Congestion Avoidance, AIMD):**

$$
CWND_4 = 8 + \dfrac{1^2}{8} \approx 8.125 \, \text{KB}
$$

**Step 5 — RTT 5:**

$$
CWND_5 = 8.125 + \dfrac{1}{8.125} \approx 8.248 \, \text{KB}
$$

**Step 6 — RTT 6:**

$$
CWND_6 = 8.248 + \dfrac{1}{8.248} \approx 8.369 \, \text{KB}
$$

> **Exponential growth in Slow Start, Linear growth in Congestion Avoidance — this is the classic KTU graph question worth 7 marks.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Closed-Loop Congestion Control

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Source] --> node2[Sender Transport Layer]
    node2 --> node3[Network Bottleneck Router]
    node3 --> node4[Receiver]
    node4 --> node5[ACK Feedback Path]
    node5 --> node6[Congestion Monitor Module]
    node6 --> node7{Is Congestion Detected?}
    node7 -- No --> node8[Continue Normal Transmission]
    node8 --> node2
    node7 -- Yes --> node9[Congestion Signal Generator]
    node9 --> node10[Choke Packet or ECN]
    node10 --> node11[Window Adjustment Engine]
    node11 --> node12[Apply AIMD Policy]
    node12 --> node2
    node2 -.-> nodeA[Output: Reduced CWND]

    classDef process fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1
    classDef decision fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#BF360C
    classDef signal fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#311B92

    class node1,node2,node3,node4,node5,node6,node8,node11,node12 process
    class node7 decision
    class node9,node10 signal
```

**Explanation:** This flowchart captures the closed-loop congestion control cycle. The **Sender** transmits data through a **Bottleneck Router** to the **Receiver**. The **ACK feedback path** feeds the **Congestion Monitor**, which decides whether to trigger the **Choke Packet or ECN Signal** and the **Window Adjustment Engine** using **AIMD**. The adjusted CWND is fed back to the sender for rate reduction.

### 4.2 TCP Reno State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SlowStart
    SlowStart --> CongAvoid: CWND reaches ssthresh
    SlowStart --> SlowStart: Duplicate ACKs not 3 / Timeout resets to 1 MSS
    CongAvoid --> FastRecovery: 3 Duplicate ACKs received
    CongAvoid --> SlowStart: Timeout occurs
    FastRecovery --> CongAvoid: ACK received for new segment
    FastRecovery --> SlowStart: Timeout occurs
    CongAvoid --> CongAvoid: ACK received increment by MSS x MSS / CWND
    SlowStart --> SlowStart: ACK received increment by MSS

    note right of SlowStart: Exponential growth phase
    note right of CongAvoid: Linear additive increase phase
    note right of FastRecovery: Multiplicative decrease then cwnd equals ssthresh
```

**Explanation:** This state diagram illustrates TCP Reno's transitions among **Slow Start (SS), Congestion Avoidance (CA), and Fast Recovery (FR)**. The transitions are governed by three signals: **Timeout, 3 Duplicate ACKs, and New ACKs**. This is a KTU favourite for **"Explain TCP Reno Congestion Control"** — worth a full 14 marks.

### 4.3 Token Bucket vs Leaky Bucket — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LB[Leaky Bucket System]
        nodeLB1[Input Packet Stream] --> nodeLB2[Bucket of Capacity C]
        nodeLB2 --> nodeLB3[Constant Output Rate R]
        nodeLB3 --> nodeLB4[Network]
    end

    subgraph TB[Token Bucket System]
        nodeTB1[Token Generator at Rate rho] --> nodeTB2[Bucket Holds up to C Tokens]
        nodeTB3[Input Packet Stream] --> nodeTB4{Packet consumes one token}
        nodeTB2 --> nodeTB4
        nodeTB4 -- Tokens available --> nodeTB5[Transmit at Variable Rate up to L]
        nodeTB4 -- No tokens --> nodeTB6[Buffer or Drop]
        nodeTB5 --> nodeTB4net[Network]
    end

    classDef lbStyle fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tbStyle fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF6F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class nodeLB1,nodeLB2,nodeLB3,nodeLB4 lbStyle
    class nodeTB1,nodeTB2,nodeTB3,nodeTB4,nodeTB5,nodeTB6,nodeTB4net tbStyle
```

**Explanation:** The **Leaky Bucket** enforces a constant output rate regardless of input burstiness, ideal for **traffic shaping** at network ingress. The **Token Bucket** allows **controlled bursts** up to bucket capacity $C$, with tokens generated at rate $\rho$. Token Bucket is **more flexible** and is the industry standard for QoS in modern routers.

### 4.4 RED (Random Early Detection) Drop Function

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeR1[Packet Arrives] --> nodeR2[Compute Average Queue Length qbar]
    nodeR2 --> nodeR3{qbar less than min th}
    nodeR3 -- Yes --> nodeR4[Accept and Enqueue]
    nodeR3 -- No --> nodeR5{qbar between min th and max th}
    nodeR5 -- Yes --> nodeR6[Compute Drop Probability Pdrop]
    nodeR6 --> nodeR7{Random less than Pdrop?}
    nodeR7 -- Yes --> nodeR8[Mark or Drop Packet]
    nodeR7 -- No --> nodeR4
    nodeR5 -- No --> nodeR9[Force Drop qbar greater than max th]
    nodeR9 --> nodeR8
    nodeR4 --> nodeR10[Next Packet]
    nodeR8 --> nodeR10

    classDef checkStyle fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px
    classDef actionStyle fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px
    classDef dropStyle fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px

    class nodeR3,nodeR5,nodeR7 checkStyle
    class nodeR1,nodeR2,nodeR4,nodeR6,nodeR10 actionStyle
    class nodeR8,nodeR9 dropStyle
```

**Explanation:** RED uses an **Exponentially Weighted Moving Average (EWMA)** of queue length, $\bar{q}$, and compares it to **min_th** and **max_th** thresholds. Between thresholds, drops occur probabilistically to provide **early congestion notification** before queues overflow. This prevents **global synchronization** — a KTU 7-mark favourite.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define congestion in computer networks. List any four causes of congestion.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Definition (1 Mark):** Congestion in a network is the state that occurs when the demand for network resources (bandwidth, buffer, processing) exceeds the available capacity, leading to packet delay, loss, and throughput degradation.

**Four Causes (½ Mark each, total 2 Marks):**
1. **Bandwidth mismatch** — when the sum of input link capacities exceeds output link capacity.
2. **Bursty traffic patterns** causing instantaneous overloads.
3. **Insufficient router buffer memory** leading to packet drops.
4. **Slow router processing speed** creating a bottleneck node.
5. (Optional fifth) **Retransmission storm** — a positive feedback effect.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Open-Loop and Closed-Loop congestion control.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|
| **Approach** (1 Mark) | Preventive / proactive | Reactive / feedback-based |
| **Decisions** (1 Mark) | At connection setup; no feedback during transmission | During transmission, based on monitored metrics |
| **Examples** (1 Mark) | Leaky Bucket, Token Bucket, Traffic Policing | TCP Reno/Tahoe, RED, Choke Packets |

Open-loop uses **policies** to prevent congestion before it occurs, while closed-loop uses **monitoring and feedback** to detect and recover from congestion dynamically.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **TCP Reno Congestion Control Algorithm** with the help of a state diagram. Discuss Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance, and Fast Recovery phases in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A TCP connection uses a window size of 64 KB and the RTT is 100 ms. The bottleneck link has a bandwidth of 10 Mbps. Calculate the link utilization and the **maximum possible throughput**. If the window size is doubled, what is the new throughput? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
TCP Reno is the most widely deployed variant of TCP congestion control. It uses a **congestion window (CWND)** that dynamically adjusts based on network feedback. It has three main phases: **Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance, and Fast Recovery**.

**Phase 1 — Slow Start (2 Marks):**
- CWND begins at **1 MSS (Maximum Segment Size)**.
- For every ACK received, CWND increases by **1 MSS**.
- The growth is **exponential**: after $n$ RTTs, CWND = $2^n$ MSS.
- Continues until CWND reaches the **slow start threshold (ssthresh)**.

**Phase 2 — Congestion Avoidance (2 Marks):**
- Triggered when CWND $\geq$ ssthresh.
- For every ACK, CWND increases by **MSS² / CWND** (per RTT, this is +1 MSS).
- Growth becomes **linear / additive**.
- This is the **Additive Increase** part of AIMD.

**Phase 3 — Fast Recovery (2 Marks):**
- Triggered by **3 duplicate ACKs** (indicating a single packet loss without timeout).
- ssthresh is set to **CWND / 2**.
- CWND is also reduced to **ssthresh + 3 × MSS** initially.
- On receiving the new ACK, CWND is set to **ssthresh**.
- This is the **Multiplicative Decrease** part of AIMD.

**State Diagram (Bonus — drawn in exam):**
Draw the three-state FSM: Slow Start → Congestion Avoidance → Fast Recovery → back to Congestion Avoidance. Use arrows labeled "timeout," "3 dup ACKs," and "new ACK."

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- Window size $W$ = 64 KB = 65,536 bytes = 524,288 bits
- RTT = 100 ms = 0.1 s
- Bandwidth $B$ = 10 Mbps = $10 \times 10^6$ bps

**Step 1 — Compute Maximum Throughput (3 Marks):**

$$
T = \dfrac{W}{RTT} = \dfrac{524288}{0.1} = 5{,}242{,}880 \, \text{bps} \approx 5.24 \, \text{Mbps}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Link Utilization (2 Marks):**

$$
Utilization = \dfrac{T}{B} \times 100 = \dfrac{5.24 \times 10^6}{10 \times 10^6} \times 100 = 52.4\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Throughput After Doubling Window (2 Marks):**

New window $W_{new} = 2 \times 64 = 128 \, \text{KB} = 1{,}048{,}576 \, \text{bits}$.

$$
T_{new} = \dfrac{W_{new}}{RTT} = \dfrac{1{,}048{,}576}{0.1} = 10{,}485{,}760 \, \text{bps} \approx 10.49 \, \text{Mbps}
$$

This now **exceeds the link capacity** of 10 Mbps, meaning the network becomes the bottleneck. Hence the actual throughput remains capped at **~10 Mbps** (with some loss).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q. (a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **Leaky Bucket** and **Token Bucket** algorithms for traffic shaping. Compare them with respect to burst handling. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Random Early Detection (RED)** in detail. How does it prevent **global synchronization**? Also, state the formula for average queue length calculation. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Leaky Bucket (3 Marks):**
- Modeled as a bucket with a small hole at the bottom.
- Water (packets) is poured in at any rate.
- Water leaks out at a **constant rate R** (the service rate).
- If the bucket is full, **new packets are discarded**.
- The output is a **smooth, constant-rate stream** regardless of input burstiness.
- **Draw:** A bucket diagram showing input stream, bucket with finite capacity, and constant-rate output.

**Token Bucket (3 Marks):**
- A bucket holds **tokens**, generated at rate $\rho$ per second.
- Bucket capacity is $C$ tokens.
- To send a packet of size $L$ bits, the sender must have at least $L$ tokens.
- If tokens are available, the packet is sent **immediately** (allows bursts up to $C$).
- If no tokens, the packet is **buffered or dropped**.
- **Draw:** A token generator, token bucket, and the variable-rate output.

**Comparison (1 Mark):**
- Leaky Bucket enforces a **strict constant rate** — no bursts allowed.
- Token Bucket allows **controlled bursts** up to bucket capacity.
- Token Bucket is **more flexible** and widely used in real networks (e.g., Cisco QoS).

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 Marks**

**RED Overview (2 Marks):**
RED is an **Active Queue Management (AQM)** mechanism deployed in routers. Unlike Tail Drop (which drops only when buffer is full), RED drops packets **early and probabilistically** to signal congestion **before** the buffer overflows.

**Average Queue Length Formula (2 Marks):**
The router maintains an exponentially weighted moving average:

$$
\bar{q} = (1 - w_q) \cdot \bar{q}_{old} + w_q \cdot q
$$

where $q$ is the current queue length and $w_q$ is the weight (typically 0.002).

**Three Threshold Zones (2 Marks):**
- If $\bar{q} \lt min_{th}$: **No drop** — accept all packets.
- If $min_{th} \leq \bar{q} \lt max_{th}$: **Probabilistic drop** — drop probability $P_{drop}$ increases linearly from 0 to $max_p$.
- If $\bar{q} \geq max_{th}$: **Force drop** — drop all incoming packets (acts like Tail Drop).

**Drop Probability Formula (1 Mark):**

$$
P_{drop} = \dfrac{max_p \cdot (\bar{q} - min_{th})}{max_{th} - min_{th}}
$$

**Prevention of Global Synchronization (Bonus):**
In Tail Drop, all flows lose packets simultaneously, leading to synchronized window reduction. RED's **random, gradual** drops affect flows stochastically, desynchronizing their window adjustments and maintaining continuous throughput.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE Module 3:**
> 1. **Confusing CWND growth in Slow Start vs Congestion Avoidance** — Many students write that CWND grows linearly in Slow Start. **WRONG.** Slow Start is exponential; Congestion Avoidance is linear. This mistake alone costs 2–3 marks per question.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** — KTU examiners deduct ½ mark for missing units (bps, ms, KB).
> 3. **Drawing TCP Reno as a 2-state machine** — It has **3 distinct states**. Drawing only Slow Start + Congestion Avoidance omits Fast Recovery and loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up Leaky Bucket and Token Bucket diagrams** — Token Bucket has a **token generator**; Leaky Bucket does NOT. Examiners look for this distinction.
> 5. **Not mentioning ECN in RED** — Modern KTU questions expect students to know that RED can also **mark** packets (ECN) instead of dropping them. Forgetting this loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Writing AIMD without the word "Additive Increase Multiplicative Decrease"** — Examiners want the full phrase expanded at least once.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLETS — Memorize Before Exam Day:**

- **Congestion** = demand for resources exceeds supply. Different from **Flow Control** (which is end-to-end buffer management).
- **5 Causes of Congestion:** Bandwidth mismatch, bursty traffic, limited buffers, slow processors, retransmission storms.
- **5 Effects of Congestion:** Increased delay, increased loss, decreased throughput, wasted bandwidth, possible congestion collapse.
- **Open-Loop** = Prevention; **Closed-Loop** = Detection + Correction. The 4 steps of Closed-Loop are **Monitor → Detect → Signal → Adjust**.
- **TCP Slow Start:** CWND = 1 MSS → doubles every RTT → exponential. **Stops** at ssthresh.
- **TCP Congestion Avoidance:** CWND grows by +1 MSS per RTT → linear (AIMD: Additive Increase).
- **TCP Fast Recovery:** Triggered by 3 duplicate ACKs; CWND = CWND/2; ssthresh = CWND/2.
- **TCP Tahoe** resets to 1 MSS on any loss; **TCP Reno** uses Fast Recovery for 3 dup ACKs.
- **RED** = Random Early Detection. Uses EWMA: $\bar{q} = (1 - w_q)\bar{q}_{old} + w_q \cdot q$. Three zones: below min_th (accept), between thresholds (probabilistic drop), above max_th (force drop).
- **RED prevents global synchronization** via random, staggered drops across flows.
- **Leaky Bucket:** Constant output rate, no bursts. Modeled as bucket with hole.
- **Token Bucket:** Allows controlled bursts up to capacity $C$, tokens generated at rate $\rho$.
- **Choke Packet:** Explicit congestion signal sent by congested router to source. Source must reduce rate.
- **AIMD Convergence:** $b \lt 1$ ensures stability; $\Delta^{new} = b \cdot \Delta$ ensures fairness.
- **Maximum Throughput Formula:** $T_{max} = W / RTT$ where $W$ = min(rwnd, cwnd).
- **Real-world deployments:** RED in Cisco IOS, Token Bucket in Linux TC, DCTCP in data centers, ECN in modern TCP.
- **Common KTU graph question:** Plot CWND vs RTT showing transition from exponential to linear at ssthresh — practice drawing this!
- **ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification):** Marks packets instead of dropping them — more efficient than RED drop.
- **Fair Queueing / WFQ (Weighted Fair Queuing):** Scheduling algorithm that provides bandwidth fairness among flows.
- **Admission Control:** Used in ATM/Integrated Services — refuses new connections if QoS cannot be guaranteed.
- **Load Shedding:** Dropping lower-priority packets when congestion is severe (e.g., video frames, email before voice).

---

**End of Module 3 — Congestion Control Notes**
**Total Marks Coverage in this Module:** 3 + 14 = 17 marks (typical KTU 2024 paper weightage for this topic)
**Recommended Study Time:** 6–8 hours for first-time preparation, 2 hours for revision.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application Layer - Name Space
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET521 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application Layer - Name Space

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **OSI Application Layer (Layer 7)**, a **Name Space** is defined as a structured, formally specified set of unique identifiers (names) that are used to unambiguously refer to resources, services, hosts, or entities in a distributed network environment. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the Application Layer Name Space is the framework that governs how human-readable names (e.g., `www.ktu.edu.in`) are mapped to machine-readable addresses (e.g., **IPv4: 32-bit** or **IPv6: 128-bit**), enabling seamless identification and location-independent communication across the global Internet.

The two principal name spaces in Data Communication are:

- **Domain Name System (DNS) Name Space** – a hierarchical, decentralized naming architecture.
- **Uniform Resource Identifier (URI) Name Space** – a unified identifier system for web resources.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4):** A Name Space is *not* a database; it is a *naming convention* (a set of rules) that determines the syntax, structure, and delegation of authority for unique names.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the world's telephone system before mobile phones existed. You could not just dial a person's name; you had to know their unique **country code → area code → city code → phone number** combination. The telephone system is *hierarchical* — each segment narrows down the location.

A **Name Space in the Application Layer works exactly the same way**:

- Instead of memorizing an IP address like `142.250.190.78` (Google's server), humans use a name like `www.google.com`.
- The hierarchy flows: **Root (.) → Top-Level Domain (.com) → Second-Level Domain (google) → Sub-domain (www)**.
- A **Distributed Database** (the DNS) stores these mappings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** A Name Space is the "postal addressing system" of the Internet — it gives every resource a unique, memorable, and structured identity that can be resolved by machines into routable addresses.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **FQDN** | Fully Qualified Domain Name (e.g., `mail.example.com.`) |
| **TLD** | Top-Level Domain (e.g., `.com`, `.org`, `.in`) |
| **ccTLD** | Country-Code TLD (e.g., `.in`, `.uk`, `.jp`) |
| **gTLD** | Generic TLD (e.g., `.com`, `.edu`, `.gov`) |
| **Root** | The apex of DNS hierarchy, denoted by the trailing dot `.` |
| **Zone** | A contiguous portion of the DNS name space managed by one administrator |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown: How an Application Layer Name Space Operates

The Application Layer Name Space operates through **five logical layers of authority**:

1. **Root Name Space (.)**
   - Managed by **ICANN (Internet Corporation for Assigned Names and Numbers)**.
   - Represented by a single dot `.` at the end of every FQDN (often implied, not written).
   - Delegates authority to TLD registries.

2. **Top-Level Domain (TLD) Name Space**
   - Two categories: **gTLDs** (generic) and **ccTLDs** (country-code).
   - Examples: `.com`, `.org`, `.net`, `.edu`, `.in`, `.uk`.
   - Newer gTLDs: `.app`, `.blog`, `.io` (introduced after **2014**, total > 1200).

3. **Second-Level Domain (SLD) Name Space**
   - Registered by individuals/organizations (e.g., `google` in `google.com`).
   - Unique within its parent TLD.

4. **Sub-domain Name Space**
   - Created at the discretion of the SLD owner (e.g., `www`, `mail`, `ftp`).
   - **Maximum depth supported by DNS: 127 levels** (RFC 1035).

5. **Host Name Space**
   - The leftmost label (e.g., `www`) identifies a specific machine.

### 2.2 Two Structural Models of Name Space

| Model | Description | Example | Scalability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flat Name Space** | Single global directory; no hierarchy | MAC address (`AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF`) | Poor |
| **Hierarchical Name Space** | Multi-level tree with delegated authority | DNS (`www.dept.univ.edu.in.`) | Excellent (used by DNS) |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Why:** DNS adopted a *hierarchical* model because a flat name space would require one server to hold the entire Internet's name-to-address mapping — impossible at scale.

### 2.3 Domain Name System (DNS) Name Space Architecture

The DNS Name Space is a **distributed, hierarchical database** structured as an **inverted tree** (root at top, leaves at bottom). It has the following properties:

- **Distributed:** No single server holds the entire database. As of 2024, there are **13 logical root servers** (A through M), physically replicated at over **1800+ locations** worldwide.
- **Hierarchical:** Authority is delegated downward; the root delegates to TLDs, TLDs delegate to SLDs, and so on.
- **Redundant:** Each zone typically has multiple name servers (primary + secondary) for fault tolerance.

### 2.4 Name Resolution Process (Two Methods)

| Method | Description | Latency | Server Load |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Recursive Resolution** | Server performs full lookup on behalf of client; returns final answer | Lower client load | Higher server load |
| **Iterative Resolution** | Server returns referral (closest known server); client queries next server | Higher client load | Lower server load |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Max DNS Label Length | $L_{label} \leq 63$ characters | RFC 1035 |
| Max FQDN Length | $L_{FQDN} \leq 253$ characters | RFC 1035 |
| Max DNS Label Count (depth) | $D_{max} = 127$ | RFC 1035 |
| IPv4 Address Size | $N_{IPv4} = 2^{32} = 4.29 \times 10^9$ | 32-bit |
| IPv6 Address Size | $N_{IPv6} = 2^{128} = 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | 128-bit |
| DNS Query UDP Port | $P_{DNS} = 53$ | RFC 1035 |
| DNS Name Compression Pointer | `0xC0` prefix (top 2 bits `11`) | RFC 1035 |
| TTL Range in Resource Record | $0 \leq TTL \leq 2^{31} - 1$ seconds | RFC 1035 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** This name space system powers every web access, email delivery (MX records), VoIP calls (SRV records), and load-balanced cloud services. Without it, the modern Internet as we know it cannot function.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Maximum DNS Name Capacity

**Problem:** Compute the maximum number of unique fully qualified domain names theoretically possible under RFC 1035 constraints.

**Given:**
- Each label: $L_{label} \leq 63$ characters.
- Each FQDN: $L_{FQDN} \leq 253$ characters (including dots).
- Maximum depth: $D_{max} = 127$ labels.

**Step 1: Determine label character set.**
The DNS character set (per RFC 1035, extended by RFC 5890 IDN) is:

$$
\Sigma_{DNS} = \{ a\text{-}z,\ A\text{-}Z,\ 0\text{-}9,\ -\ \} \cup \text{IDN Unicode}
$$

For ASCII-only names:

$$
\vert \Sigma_{DNS}^{ASCII} \vert = 26 + 26 + 10 + 1 = 63 \text{ characters}
$$

**Step 2: Compute combinations per label position.**

Each label can be $1$ to $63$ characters long. The number of possible unique label strings is:

$$
N_{label} = \sum_{k=1}^{63} 63^{k}
$$

**Step 3: Geometric series evaluation.**

Using the formula for a finite geometric series:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{n} r^{k} = r \cdot \frac{r^{n} - 1}{r - 1}
$$

We obtain:

$$
N_{label} = 63 \cdot \frac{63^{63} - 1}{63 - 1}
$$

$$
N_{label} = \frac{63^{64} - 63}{62}
$$

**Step 4: Address length constraints.**

Since each FQDN is $\leq 253$ bytes and each label can be up to 63 bytes, with the period `.` taking 1 byte between labels, the **effective number of labels** in the longest possible FQDN is:

$$
D_{eff} = \frac{253 - 1}{63 + 1} = \frac{252}{64} \approx 3.94
$$

So the practical depth is $\approx 4$ labels, but RFC allows up to **127** logical labels separated by dots.

**Step 5: Total unique FQDN capacity (asymptotic bound).**

$$
N_{FQDN}^{max} \leq N_{label}^{D_{max}} = \left( \frac{63^{64} - 63}{62} \right)^{127}
$$

This bound is astronomically large, confirming the **scalability** of the hierarchical DNS name space.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Name Space Resolution

| Parameter | Recursive Resolution | Iterative Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| Number of round trips | **1** (client → server → answer) | **Up to depth $D$** (client hops through referrals) |
| Server CPU load | **High** (server does all work) | **Low** (server only refers) |
| Client CPU load | **Low** | **High** (client must chase referrals) |
| Caching efficiency | Excellent (server caches) | Moderate (client may or may not cache) |
| Failure mode | Single point of failure at resolver | Distributed (failure of one referral not fatal) |
| KTU Exam Keyword | "Server takes full responsibility" | "Server returns best-known pointer" |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Resolving `www.example.com.`

Suppose a client in Kerala (IP `10.0.0.5`) wants to access `www.example.com.` (a registered under `.com`).

**Step 1: Local Resolver Check.**
The OS first checks its local cache. If not present, query goes to the **ISP's recursive resolver** (e.g., `8.8.8.8`).

**Step 2: Root Server Query.**
Resolver queries a root server (e.g., `a.root-servers.net`). The root server **does not know** the IP of `www.example.com`, but it knows the address of the `.com` TLD server. It returns a **referral** (NS record).

**Step 3: TLD Server Query.**
Resolver queries the `.com` TLD server. The TLD server knows the authoritative server for `example.com`. Returns NS referral.

**Step 4: Authoritative Server Query.**
Resolver queries the authoritative server for `example.com`. This server returns the **A record**: `www.example.com. → 93.184.216.34`.

**Step 5: Response & Caching.**
The resolver returns `93.184.216.34` to the client and **caches** the result with a TTL (e.g., 86400 seconds = **24 hours**).

**Total time** (cold cache): ~**20–120 ms** globally.
**Subsequent queries** (warm cache): <**1 ms**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical DNS Name Space (Inverted Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["Root Name Space ."]
    tldA["TLD .com"]
    tldB["TLD .edu"]
    tldC["TLD .in"]
    sldA["example.com"]
    sldB["google.com"]
    sldC["ktu.edu.in"]
    subA1["www.example.com"]
    subA2["mail.example.com"]
    subB1["www.google.com"]
    subB2["maps.google.com"]
    subC1["www.ktu.edu.in"]
    subC2["cse.ktu.edu.in"]

    rootA --- tldA
    rootA --- tldB
    rootA --- tldC
    tldA --- sldA
    tldA --- sldB
    tldC --- sldC
    sldA --- subA1
    sldA --- subA2
    sldB --- subB1
    sldB --- subB2
    sldC --- subC1
    sldC --- subC2

    style rootA fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style tldA fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style tldB fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style tldC fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style sldA fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000
    style sldB fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000
    style sldC fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000
    style subA1 fill:#deebf7,color:#000000
    style subA2 fill:#deebf7,color:#000000
    style subB1 fill:#deebf7,color:#000000
    style subB2 fill:#deebf7,color:#000000
```
