> 1. For **each** of the $N+1$ objects, we pay **2 RTTs** (1 RTT for TCP setup + 1 RTT for HTTP request-response).
> 2. We also pay the **transmission delay** $\frac{O_j}{B}$ for each object.
> 3. We sum over all $N+1$ objects and total it.

### 3.1.3 Case B — Persistent (Keep-Alive) HTTP (HTTP/1.1 Style)

In persistent HTTP, **a single TCP connection is reused** for all $N+1$ objects. The TCP handshake is paid **once**, and subsequent object transfers cost only **1 RTT each** (request-response).

**Total time:**

$$
T_{\text{persistent}} = \underbrace{2R}_{\text{one-time TCP setup}} + (N+1)\left(R + \sum_{j=0}^{N} \frac{O_j}{B}\right)
$$

> **Step-by-step textual breakdown:**
> 1. Pay **2 RTTs once** for the initial TCP handshake.
> 2. For each of the $N+1$ objects, pay **1 RTT** for HTTP request-response.
> 3. Sum transmission delays.

### 3.1.4 Quantitative Speedup — Analytical Proof

The speedup factor $S$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{T_{\text{non-persistent}}}{T_{\text{persistent}}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{(N+1)\left(2R + \sum_{j=0}^{N} \frac{O_j}{B}\right)}{2R + (N+1)\left(R + \sum_{j=0}^{N} \frac{O_j}{B}\right)} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2(N+1)R + (N+1)\sum_{j=0}^{N} \frac{O_j}{B}}{2R + (N+1)R + (N+1)\sum_{j=0}^{N} \frac{O_j}{B}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2(N+1)R + (N+1)T_t}{2R + (N+1)R + (N+1)T_t}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $T_t = \sum_{j=0}^{N} \frac{O_j}{B}$ is the total transmission time.

> **Engineering Insight:** As $N \to \infty$ (many small objects, typical of modern web pages), $S \to 2$, meaning persistent connections roughly **halve** the latency cost. For real-world pages with $N > 50$ referenced objects, persistent connections are not optional — they are essential.

### 3.1.5 Numerical KTU-Style Worked Example

> **Given:** $N = 5$ objects, $R = 100$ ms = $0.1$ s, each object size $= 100$ KB $= 8 \times 10^5$ bits, $B = 1$ Mbps $= 10^6$ bps.
>
> **Compute transmission delay per object:**
> $$\frac{O_j}{B} = \frac{8 \times 10^5}{10^6} = 0.8 \text{ s}$$
>
> **Compute total transmission time:**
> $$T_t = (N+1) \cdot 0.8 = 6 \cdot 0.8 = 4.8 \text{ s}$$
>
> **Compute non-persistent total time:**
> $$T_{\text{non-persistent}} = 6 \cdot (0.2 + 0.8) = 6 \cdot 1.0 = 6.0 \text{ s}$$
>
> **Compute persistent total time:**
> $$T_{\text{persistent}} = 0.2 + 6 \cdot (0.1 + 0.8) = 0.2 + 5.4 = 5.6 \text{ s}$$
>
> **Speedup:**
> $$S = \frac{6.0}{5.6} \approx 1.071$$
>
> Persistent HTTP is ~7% faster here because objects are large. For small objects with many references ($N$ large, $O_j$ small), persistent HTTP delivers dramatic 2× speedups.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — HTTP vs HTTPS vs HTTP/2 vs HTTP/3

This is a frequently tested **comparison matrix**. Below is the comprehensive analytical breakdown.

### 3.2.1 HTTPS Extension of HTTP

HTTPS = **HTTP + TLS/SSL** at the transport layer. The protocol stack is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HTTPS Stack:} \quad &\text{HTTP (Application)} \\
&\rightarrow \text{TLS 1.2 / TLS 1.3 (Session/Presentation)} \\
&\rightarrow \text{TCP (Transport)} \\
&\rightarrow \text{IP (Network)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Properties provided by TLS:**
- **Confidentiality:** AES-GCM / ChaCha20 symmetric encryption
- **Integrity:** HMAC-SHA256 / SHA-384 MAC on every record
- **Authentication:** X.509 certificates verified against trusted CAs
- **Forward Secrecy:** Ephemeral Diffie-Hellman (ECDHE) key exchange

### 3.2.2 HTTP/2 Binary Framing Layer

HTTP/2 abandons text framing. The HPACK-compressed header block and DATA frames are interleaved over a single TCP connection using **stream multiplexing**:

$$
\text{HTTP/2 Frame} = \underbrace{\text{9-byte Header}}_{\text{Length(24) \mid Type(8) \mid Flags(8) \mid Stream ID(32)}} + \text{Payload}
$$

**Stream ID rules:**
- Client-initiated streams: **odd** numbers
- Server-pushed streams: **even** numbers
- Connection-level control: stream ID = **0x00**

### 3.2.3 HTTP/3 QUIC Transport

HTTP/3 runs over **QUIC**, which is a UDP-based, user-space transport providing:
- **Stream multiplexing without TCP head-of-line blocking**
- **0-RTT / 1-RTT connection establishment** (combines TCP + TLS)
- **Built-in congestion control** (Cubic, BBR variants)
- **Connection migration** (survives IP/port changes — crucial for mobile)

$$
\text{HTTP/3 Stack:} \quad \text{HTTP} \;\to\; \text{QPACK} \;\to\; \text{QUIC (UDP, port 443)} \;\to\; \text{IP}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Breakdown of HTTP Request Headers — Worked Example

> **KTU Question Pattern:** "Explain the role of each header in the following HTTP request and trace the request through the application layer to the transport layer."

**Request:**
```
GET /api/v1/products/42 HTTP/1.1
Host: shop.example.com
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0)
Accept: application/json
Accept-Language: en-US
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1...
Cookie: session=abc123
Connection: keep-alive
```

**Step-by-step trace:**

1. **Method `GET`**: Read-only retrieval of the resource at `/api/v1/products/42`.
2. **Request-URI `/api/v1/products/42`**: The REST path; identifies product ID 42.
3. **HTTP-version `HTTP/1.1`**: Tells server to use persistent connection.
4. **`Host`**: Mandatory in HTTP/1.1; allows virtual hosting (multiple domains on one IP).
5. **`User-Agent`**: Allows server to tailor response (mobile vs desktop).
6. **`Accept: application/json`**: Client wants JSON response (Content Negotiation).
7. **`Accept-Language`**: Client prefers English content.
8. **`Authorization: Bearer ...`**: JWT token proving authentication.
9. **`Cookie`**: Carries session state, recovering HTTP statelessness.
10. **`Connection: keep-alive`**: Asks server to reuse the TCP connection.

This complete trace yields a KTU 7-mark answer when paired with explanation of statelessness and authentication.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Complete HTTP Request-Response Transaction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User enters URL in Browser] --> B[Browser extracts hostname]
    B --> C[DNS Resolver queries Root TLD and Authoritative DNS]
    C --> D[Receives IP address 93.184.216.34]
    D --> E[TCP Three-Way Handshake SYN SYN-ACK ACK to Port 80]
    E --> F[Browser constructs HTTP Request Message]
    F --> G[Request Line GET slash index.html HTTP 1.1]
    G --> H[Header Fields Host User-Agent Accept Cookie]
    H --> I[Empty CRLF Line separating headers from body]
    I --> J[Request sent over established TCP connection]
    J --> K[Server receives and parses request]
    K --> L{Authentication and Authorization Check}
    L -->|Authorized| M[Application layer executes business logic]
    L -->|Unauthorized| N[Server returns 401 Unauthorized]
    M --> O[Server constructs HTTP Response Message]
    O --> P[Status Line HTTP 1.1 200 OK]
    P --> Q[Response Headers Content-Type Content-Length Set-Cookie]
    Q --> R[Body with HTML or JSON payload]
    R --> S[Browser receives and renders the page]
    S --> T{Connection keep-alive}
    T -->|Yes| U[Reuse TCP connection for next request]
    T -->|No| V[Send FIN to close TCP connection]
    N --> W[Browser displays error page]
    U --> X[HTTP Transaction Complete]
    V --> X
    W --> X
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — HTTP Protocol Stack Position and Encapsulation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L7[Layer 7 Application: HTTP GET POST PUT DELETE methods]
    L6[Layer 6 Presentation: TLS 1.3 optional encryption and decryption]
    L5[Layer 5 Session: Cookies JWT tokens session management]
    L4[Layer 4 Transport: TCP port 80 or UDP via QUIC port 443]
    L3[Layer 3 Network: IP addressing and routing]
    L2[Layer 2 Data Link: Ethernet MAC frames]
    L1[Layer 1 Physical: Copper Fiber Radio bits on the wire]

    L7 -->|Encapsulate HTTP message in TLS record if HTTPS| L6
    L6 -->|Wrap in TLS ciphertext then TCP segment| L5
    L5 -->|Add session identifier and token| L4
    L4 -->|TCP segment with source and destination ports| L3
    L3 -->|IP packet with TTL and checksum| L2
    L2 -->|MAC frame with preamble| L1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — HTTP State Lifecycle for a Browser Session

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Resolving: User enters URL
    Resolving --> Connecting: DNS returns IP
    Connecting --> SendingRequest: TCP handshake complete
    SendingRequest --> WaitingResponse: HTTP request transmitted
    WaitingResponse --> ReceivingHeaders: First packet arrives
    ReceivingHeaders --> ParsingBody: Headers parsed
    ParsingBody --> Rendering: Body fully received
    Rendering --> Idle: Connection reused
    Rendering --> Closed: Connection close header seen
    WaitingResponse --> ErrorReceived: 4xx or 5xx response
    ErrorReceived --> Rendering: Error page rendered
    Closed --> [*]
    Idle --> [*]: Browser closed
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — HTTP Version Evolution Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V09[HTTP 0.9 1991 GET only HTML] --> V10[HTTP 1.0 1996 Methods headers non-persistent]
    V10 --> V11[HTTP 1.1 1997 Persistent keep-alive chunked]
    V11 --> V2[HTTP 2 2015 Binary HPACK multiplexing]
    V2 --> V3[HTTP 3 2022 QUIC UDP 0-RTT no HOL blocking]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — REST CRUD Mapping onto HTTP Methods

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CREATE[Create New Resource] --> POST[POST HTTP Method 201 Created]
    READ[Read Existing Resource] --> GET[GET HTTP Method 200 OK]
    UPDATE_FULL[Replace Entire Resource] --> PUT[PUT HTTP Method 200 OK or 204]
    UPDATE_PARTIAL[Modify Part of Resource] --> PATCH[PATCH HTTP Method 200 OK]
    DELETE_OP[Remove Resource] --> DEL[DELETE HTTP Method 204 No Content]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

### **Q1. Define HTTP. Why is it called a stateless protocol? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is an **application-layer, request-response protocol** standardized in **RFC 9110** that governs the transfer of hypermedia resources between clients and servers over the World Wide Web. It operates by default over **TCP port 80**.

HTTP is called **stateless** because every HTTP request is treated as an **independent transaction**. The server retains **no memory** of prior requests from the same client. Each request must contain **all necessary information** (URL, headers, cookies, tokens) for the server to fulfill it. Continuity across requests is achieved through **external mechanisms** such as cookies, server-side sessions, and JWT tokens — not through HTTP itself.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between HTTP and HTTPS in three key aspects. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | HTTP | HTTPS |
|--------|------|-------|
| **Port** | 80 | 443 |
| **Encryption** | None — plaintext | TLS/SSL — ciphertext |
| **Certificate** | Not required | X.509 certificate required from CA |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the structure of an **HTTP request message** and an **HTTP response message** with a suitable example for each. Discuss the role of **at least six commonly used HTTP headers**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a **neat block diagram**, explain the **working of HTTP** as an application-layer protocol. Discuss how **persistent and non-persistent connections** differ and compute the total time taken to fetch $N+1$ objects for each model. Use $R = 100$ ms and assume each object has transmission delay $\frac{O_j}{B} = 0.8$ s with $N = 5$. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**1. HTTP Request Message Structure** *(2 marks)*

The HTTP request message consists of:
- **Request Line**: `Method SP Request-URI SP HTTP-Version CRLF`
- **Header Fields**: One or more lines of `Header-Name: Value`
- **Empty Line**: A single CRLF separator
- **Message Body**: Optional (used with POST, PUT, PATCH)

**Example:**
```
GET /index.html HTTP/1.1
Host: www.example.com
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0
Accept: text/html
Authorization: Bearer abc123
Cookie: session=xyz789
Connection: keep-alive

```

**2. HTTP Response Message Structure** *(2 marks)*

The HTTP response message consists of:
- **Status Line**: `HTTP-Version SP Status-Code SP Reason-Phrase CRLF`
- **Response Headers**: `Content-Type`, `Content-Length`, `Set-Cookie`, etc.
- **Empty Line (CRLF)**
- **Message Body**: HTML, JSON, binary, etc.

**Example:**
```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8
Content-Length: 1024
Set-Cookie: sessionid=abc123; HttpOnly
Cache-Control: max-age=3600

<html><body>Hello World</body></html>
```

**3. Role of Common Headers** *(3 marks)*

| Header | Role |
|--------|------|
| **Host** | Identifies the virtual host (mandatory in HTTP/1.1). |
| **User-Agent** | Identifies the client software enabling content negotiation. |
| **Accept** | Lists MIME types the client can handle (e.g., `application/json`). |
| **Authorization** | Carries credentials such as JWT bearer tokens. |
| **Cookie** | Sends session data back to the server to recover statelessness. |
| **Content-Type** | Specifies MIME type of the message body. |

#### **Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**1. HTTP Block Diagram / Working** *(3 marks)*

The HTTP working model involves:
1. **Client (Browser)** initiates connection to server on **port 80**.
2. **TCP handshake** establishes reliable transport.
3. **HTTP request** is sent carrying method, URI, headers.
4. **Web Server** receives, parses, and routes request to backend application (e.g., PHP, Node.js, Python).
5. **Response message** is constructed with status, headers, and body.
6. **Connection management** — HTTP/1.1 uses persistent connections by default.

**2. Persistent vs Non-Persistent** *(2 marks)*

| Model | TCP Connection | RTT per Object | Suitable For |
|-------|---------------|----------------|---------------|
| **Non-persistent (HTTP/1.0)** | New connection per object | 2 RTTs (1 TCP + 1 HTTP) | HTTP/1.0 |
| **Persistent (HTTP/1.1)** | Single reused connection | 1 RTT (only HTTP) | Modern web |

**3. Computation** *(2 marks)*

For non-persistent with $N+1 = 6$ objects, $R = 0.1$ s, $\frac{O_j}{B} = 0.8$ s:

$$
T_{\text{non-persistent}} = 6 \times (2 \times 0.1 + 0.8) = 6 \times 1.0 = 6.0 \text{ s}
$$

For persistent:

$$
T_{\text{persistent}} = 2 \times 0.1 + 6 \times (0.1 + 0.8) = 0.2 + 5.4 = 5.6 \text{ s}
$$

The persistent model is **faster by 0.4 s** in this scenario. Speedup increases dramatically as $N$ grows.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Compare **HTTP/1.0, HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3** across the dimensions of **transport, connection model, multiplexing, header compression, head-of-line blocking, and TLS support**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **concept of HTTP status codes** with a complete classification of the **five classes (1xx through 5xx)**. List **at least three specific codes** from each class with their meaning and a real-world scenario where each is triggered. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Comparative Table** *(6 marks)*

| Feature | HTTP/1.0 | HTTP/1.1 | HTTP/2 | HTTP/3 |
|---------|----------|----------|--------|--------|
| **Transport** | TCP | TCP | TCP | QUIC (UDP) |
| **Connection** | Non-persistent | Persistent (keep-alive) | Persistent + multiplexed | Persistent + multiplexed |
| **Multiplexing** | No | Pipelining (limited) | Full binary multiplexing | Full multiplexing |
| **Header Compression** | None | None | HPACK | QPACK |
| **HOL Blocking** | Yes | Partial | At TCP layer | **Eliminated** |
| **TLS** | Optional | Optional | De-facto required | **Always required** |
| **RFC / Year** | RFC 1945 / 1996 | RFC 9110 / 2022 | RFC 7540 / 2015 | RFC 9114 / 2022 |

**Key Insight** *(1 mark)*: HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 dramatically reduce page load latency for modern web pages with many small resources, achieving **multiplexing without head-of-line blocking** at the application layer.

#### **Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**HTTP Status Code Classification** — five classes based on the leading digit:

**1xx Informational** *(1 mark)*

| Code | Meaning | Scenario |
|------|---------|----------|
| **100 Continue** | Server acknowledges large request, client should send body | Client sends `Expect: 100-continue` header before uploading large file |
| **101 Switching Protocols** | Server switching to a new protocol | WebSocket upgrade handshake |

**2xx Success** *(1 mark)*

| Code | Meaning | Scenario |
|------|---------|----------|
| **200 OK** | Standard success response | Fetching `/index.html` successfully |
| **201 Created** | New resource created | `POST /api/users` creates new user |
| **204 No Content** | Success, but no body | `DELETE /api/products/42` removes a product |

**3xx Redirection** *(1 mark)*

| Code | Meaning | Scenario |
|------|---------|----------|
| **301 Moved Permanently** | Resource permanently at new URL | HTTP → HTTPS migration |
| **302 Found** | Temporary redirect | After successful form POST |
| **304 Not Modified** | Use cached copy | Conditional GET with `If-Modified-Since` |

**4xx Client Error** *(2 marks)*

| Code | Meaning | Scenario |
|------|---------|----------|
| **400 Bad Request** | Malformed request | Invalid JSON in POST body |
| **401 Unauthorized** | Authentication required | Accessing protected endpoint without token |
| **404 Not Found** | Resource does not exist | Visiting a deleted page |

**5xx Server Error** *(2 marks)*

| Code | Meaning | Scenario |
|------|---------|----------|
| **500 Internal Server Error** | Unhandled exception | Backend crash in API handler |
| **502 Bad Gateway** | Upstream server failed | Reverse proxy (Nginx) cannot reach backend |
| **503 Service Unavailable** | Server overloaded | During traffic spike or maintenance window |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Mistaking ports:** Students often write "HTTP uses port 8080." It does *not* — **port 8080 is the IANA-registered alternate for HTTP proxy/test servers, not the default.** The default is **port 80**.
>
> 2. **Statelessness confusion:** Many students write "HTTP is stateless, so the server cannot remember users." Wrong — the server *can* maintain sessions using cookies and databases; HTTP itself just doesn't *implicitly* carry state.
>
> 3. **HTTP/2 misclassification:** Several students think HTTP/2 uses UDP. It uses **TCP**. Only **HTTP/3** uses UDP (via QUIC).
>
> 4. **Status code digit placement:** Writing "404 = Client Error" but calling it "Server Error" loses marks. Memorize: **4xx = Client-side, 5xx = Server-side**.
>
> 5. **Persistent connection formula:** Students frequently forget the **single TCP handshake cost** ($2R$) when computing persistent HTTP time. Always include it as a *one-time* up-front cost.
>
> 6. **CRLF omission:** When asked to write a request/response, students forget the blank line separating headers from body. Examiners allocate specific marks for this separator.
>
> 7. **Mixing HTTP and HTTPS:** Saying "HTTP provides encryption via SSL" loses 1 mark. Encryption is **TLS/SSL layered on top of HTTP**, giving HTTPS — a *separate* protocol stack.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 / HTTP**

### Core Definition & Position
- ☐ HTTP = **Application Layer**, **Request-Response**, **Stateless**, **Text-based (v1.x) / Binary (v2,v3)**
- ☐ Default port = **80**, HTTPS = **443**, QUIC/HTTP3 = **443 UDP**

### Architectural Pillars
- ☐ **Client–Server model** with browser as client, web server as server
- ☐ **Stateless** → Cookies, Sessions, JWT tokens add state externally
- ☐ Built on **TCP** (HTTP/1.x, HTTP/2) or **QUIC/UDP** (HTTP/3)

### Message Anatomy
- ☐ **Request = Request-Line + Headers + CRLF + [Body]**
- ☐ **Response = Status-Line + Headers + CRLF + [Body]**
- ☐ **CRLF** is the universal separator in HTTP/1.x

### Methods (Verbs)
- ☐ **GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS, TRACE, CONNECT**
- ☐ Idempotent: **GET, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS, TRACE**
- ☐ Safe: **GET, HEAD, OPTIONS, TRACE**

### Status Codes (1xx–5xx)
- ☐ **2xx = Success**, **3xx = Redirect**, **4xx = Client Error**, **5xx = Server Error**
- ☐ Must-know codes: **200, 201, 204, 301, 304, 400, 401, 403, 404, 500, 502, 503**

### Version Timeline
- ☐ **HTTP/0.9 (1991) → 1.0 (1996) → 1.1 (1997/2022) → 2 (2015) → 3 (2022)**
- ☐ HTTP/2: **Binary framing + HPACK + Multiplexing over TCP**
- ☐ HTTP/3: **QUIC + UDP + 0-RTT + no HOL blocking**

### Performance Models
- ☐ **Non-persistent**: $T = (N+1)(2R + \sum O_j/B)$
- ☐ **Persistent**: $T = 2R + (N+1)(R + \sum O_j/B)$

### Critical Headers
- ☐ **Host** (mandatory in HTTP/1.1), **Authorization**, **Cookie/Set-Cookie**, **Content-Type**, **Accept**, **Cache-Control**

### High-Yield Distinctions
- ☐ HTTP ≠ HTTPS (latter adds TLS)
- ☐ HTTP/2 uses TCP (not UDP); HTTP/3 uses QUIC/UDP
- ☐ Pipelining (HTTP/1.1) ≠ Multiplexing (HTTP/2+); pipelining still suffers head-of-line blocking
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SNMP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET521 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** SNMP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP)** is an **Application Layer** (OSI Layer 7 / TCP-IP Application Layer) protocol defined by the **IETF** under **STD-62 (RFC 3411–RFC 3418)** used for exchanging management information between network devices. It operates primarily over **UDP port 161** (manager → agent requests/responses) and **UDP port 162** (agent → manager traps).

SNMP forms the cornerstone of the **FCAPS** model (Fault, Configuration, Accounting, Performance, Security) defined by the **ISO/IEC 7498-4** standard for network management. It enables administrators to monitor, configure, and control network elements such as routers, switches, servers, firewalls, and printers from a centralized **Network Management Station (NMS)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEAET521 Module 4):**
> Students must master the **SNMP architecture**, the **Manager-Agent-MIB triad**, **PDU formats**, **MIB (Management Information Base)**, **SMI (Structure of Management Information)**, **SNMP versions (v1, v2c, v3)**, and the **Get/Set/Trap operations**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Network Health Remote Control"

Imagine you manage a **large hospital** with hundreds of critical machines (MRI scanners, ventilators, monitors). You cannot physically visit each machine every minute to check its status. Instead, you install a **tiny software "sensor"** on each machine that continuously tracks vitals (CPU, memory, temperature, status). All sensors report back to a **central control room** (the NMS).

The "language" used between the sensors and the control room is **SNMP**:

| Hospital Analogy | SNMP Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Central control room nurse station | **Network Management Station (Manager)** | Polls devices, displays data |
| Sensor on each machine | **SNMP Agent** (daemon on device) | Collects local data, responds to queries |
| Patient's medical chart | **Management Information Base (MIB)** | Database of objects/variables tracked |
| Vocabulary/dictionary used in charts | **SMI (Structure of Management Information)** | Rules defining object names & data types |
| Phone line between nurse & sensor | **SNMP Protocol** (UDP/IP) | Communication channel |

> [!TIP]
> **One-line intuition:** *SNMP is like a structured, periodic Q&A session where a manager asks agents "How are you doing?" and agents either answer back or spontaneously report emergencies.*

---

## 1.3 Core Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric / Constant | Standard Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Default Agent UDP Port | **161** | Receives requests, sends responses |
| Default Manager UDP Port | **162** | Receives unsolicited traps |
| Transport Protocol | **UDP (Connectionless)** | RFC 3416 mandates UDP, but TCP optionally supported |
| Standard MIB | **MIB-II (RFC 1213)** | Replaced original MIB-I (RFC 1156) |
| OID Root for Internet MIBs | **1.3.6.1.2.1** (iso.org.dod.internet.mgmt.mib-2) | Internet MIB tree root |
| Default Retransmission Timeout | **5 seconds** | RFC 3414 recommended |
| Default Max Retries | **5 attempts** | For unreplied requests |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SNMP Architecture – The Manager-Agent-MIB Triad

SNMP is built on a **client-server-like architecture**, but technically a **manager-polled, agent-responding** model with asynchronous trap exceptions. The four logical components are:

### 2.1.1 Network Management Station (NMS) / Manager
- A **dedicated workstation or server** running NMS software (e.g., SolarWinds, PRTG, Nagios, Zabbix, OpenNMS, Cisco Prime).
- Initiates **polling cycles** (GetRequest, GetNextRequest, GetBulkRequest).
- Issues **configuration commands** (SetRequest).
- Receives **unsolicited Trap** messages from agents.
- Performs **fault analysis, thresholding, trend graphing**, and **event correlation**.

### 2.1.2 Managed Device (Agent Side)
- Any network element (router, switch, firewall, server, printer, IoT device, UPS) that runs an **SNMP agent daemon** (e.g., **snmpd** on Linux, Cisco IOS SNMP subsystem).
- Maintains a local **MIB** instance reflecting the device's operational state.
- Translates **OIDs (Object Identifiers)** into hardware/software-specific values.
- Sends **Traps** when pre-configured thresholds are crossed.

### 2.1.3 Management Information Base (MIB)
- A **hierarchical, tree-structured virtual database** that defines the **managed objects** (variables) an agent maintains.
- Each object has:
  - **Name** (textual descriptor, e.g., `sysDescr`, `ifInOctets`)
  - **OID** (numeric identifier, e.g., `1.3.6.1.2.1.1.1.0`)
  - **Data Type** (INTEGER, OCTET STRING, Counter32, Gauge32, IpAddress, TimeTicks, etc.)
  - **Access Rights** (read-only, read-write, not-accessible)
  - **Status** (mandatory, optional, obsolete)

### 2.1.4 Network Management Protocol (SNMP Itself)
- The wire-format messaging protocol carrying Get/Set/Response/Trap PDUs.

---

## 2.2 The Object Identifier (OID) Tree

OIDs follow the **ASN.1 (Abstract Syntax Notation One)** naming convention as defined in **ITU-T X.680** and **ISO/IEC 8824**. The hierarchical tree is rooted at the **ISO/ITU-T root**:

```
iso(1) . org(3) . dod(6) . internet(1)
                        └── mgmt(2) . mib-2(1)
                        └── private(4) . enterprise(1) . <vendor>
```

A typical enterprise OID for a Cisco device might be:
**1.3.6.1.4.1.9.1.1.0** → represents a specific managed object on Cisco hardware.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why OIDs?** They provide a **globally unique, hierarchical, machine-independent** naming scheme — critical when thousands of heterogeneous devices must be queried from a single NMS.

---

## 2.3 SMI – Structure of Management Information

**SMI** (RFC 1155 for v1, RFC 2578/2584 for v2/v3) defines the **rules** for:

1. **Defining managed objects** in MIBs.
2. **Data types** used (e.g., `INTEGER`, `OCTET STRING`, `Counter32`, `Gauge32`, `IpAddress`, `TimeTicks`, `OBJECT IDENTIFIER`, `BITS`).
3. **Encoding rules** — specifically **BER (Basic Encoding Rules)** of ASN.1.

### Common SMI Data Types (HIGH-YIELD)

| Data Type | Description | Example Use |
|---|---|---|
| `INTEGER` | 32-bit signed integer | Port number, status codes |
| `OCTET STRING` | Variable-length byte string | Device name, community string |
| `Counter32` | Monotonically increasing 32-bit counter (wraps to 0) | Total bytes received on interface |
| `Gauge32` | Non-negative integer that may increase or decrease | Current queue length |
| `IpAddress` | 4-byte IPv4 address | Device IP |
| `TimeTicks` | Hundredths of a second since uptime | `sysUpTime` |
| `OBJECT IDENTIFIER` | OID value | `sysObjectID` |

---

## 2.4 SNMP Versions – Evolution and Security

| Feature | SNMPv1 (RFC 1157) | SNMPv2c (RFC 1901-1908) | SNMPv3 (RFC 3410-3418) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Release Year** | 1988 | 1996 | 2002 |
| **Authentication** | Community string (cleartext) | Community string (cleartext) | MD5/SHA (HMAC) |
| **Encryption / Privacy** | ❌ None | ❌ None | ✅ DES / AES-128/192/256 |
| **GetBulk** | ❌ | ✅ (efficient bulk retrieval) | ✅ |
| **Inform Request** | ❌ | ✅ (manager-to-manager with ACK) | ✅ |
| **Error Codes** | 3 codes | 18 codes (better diagnostics) | 18 codes |
| **Security Levels** | None | None | `noAuthNoPriv`, `authNoPriv`, `authPriv` |
| **Practical Use Today** | Legacy / educational | Common in enterprise | Mandatory for compliance (PCI-DSS, HIPAA) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write "SNMPv2" generically. Always be precise — **SNMPv2** (original, party-based) was abandoned; the **community-string-based** variant that actually ships in devices is **SNMPv2c**.

---

## 2.5 SNMP Operations (PDU Types) – The Core 7 Operations

| # | Operation | Direction | Purpose | PDU Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **GetRequest** | Manager → Agent | Retrieve the value of a specific OID | `0xA0` |
| 2 | **GetNextRequest** | Manager → Agent | Retrieve the *next* OID lexicographically (used for MIB walking) | `0xA1` |
| 3 | **GetBulkRequest** (v2c+) | Manager → Agent | Retrieve multiple successive OIDs in one PDU | `0xA5` |
| 4 | **SetRequest** | Manager → Agent | Modify the value of a writable OID | `0xA3` |
| 5 | **Response** | Agent → Manager | Reply to any of the above requests | `0xA2` |
| 6 | **Trap** (v1) / **SNMPv2-Trap** (v2c+) | Agent → Manager (async) | Unsolicited alert (link down, threshold exceeded) | `0xA4` / `0xA7` |
| 7 | **InformRequest** (v2c+) | Manager → Manager | Acknowledged notification between managers | `0xA6` |
| 8 | **Report** (v3) | Manager ↔ Agent | Engine-to-engine error reporting | `0xA8` |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (SNMP-Specific Computations)

> [!TIP]
> SNMP is largely protocol-engineering heavy, but the following quantitative relationships are routinely asked in KTU exams.

| # | Formula / Relationship | Notation | Unit / Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{SNMP} = T_{poll} + T_{prop} + T_{proc} + T_{resp}$ | $T$ | Total SNMP round-trip latency (seconds) |
| 2 | $B_{SNMP} = N_{dev} \times f_{poll} \times (L_{PDU} + L_{UDP} + L_{IP} + L_{eth})$ | bytes/sec | Bandwidth consumed by polling traffic |
| 3 | $L_{PDU} = 20 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} L_{VB_i}$ | bytes | PDU length (20-byte header + variable bindings) |
| 4 | $L_{VB_i} = L_{OID_i} + L_{value_i} + L_{padding}$ | bytes | Length of each variable binding |
| 5 | $\eta_{poll} = \dfrac{N_{successful}}{N_{sent}} \times 100\%$ | % | Polling efficiency (lost requests reduce this) |
| 6 | $R_{trap} = \dfrac{N_{traps}}{T_{window}}$ | events/sec | Trap arrival rate (for sizing manager queues) |
| 7 | $L_{UDP} = 8$ bytes | – | Fixed UDP header |
| 8 | $L_{IP} = 20$ bytes (no options) | – | IPv4 header minimum |
| 9 | $L_{eth} = 26$ bytes (14 header + 12 interpacket) | – | Ethernet II frame (min) |
| 10 | $Counter32_{wrap} = 2^{32} - 1 = \mathbf{4{,}294{,}967{,}295}$ | counts | Maximum before wraparound |
| 11 | $L_{msg,v3} = L_{header} + L_{secParams} + L_{scopedPDU}$ | bytes | SNMPv3 message total length |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical rule from SYSTEM instructions:** The pipe symbol `|` is reserved for table borders. Inside this formula sheet, all absolute-value or set-membership notations are written as `\vert x \vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking Markdown.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility of SNMP

1. **ISP and Telecom Backbone Management:** Tier-1 ISPs (e.g., AT&T, Reliance Jio) use SNMP to monitor **tens of thousands** of routers/switches in their MPLS core, tracking interface utilization, BGP sessions, and optic power levels.
2. **Data Center Infrastructure:** Server CPU, memory, fan speeds, chassis temperature, and power supply health are all exposed as SNMP OIDs by **iDRAC (Dell)**, **iLO (HP/HPE)**, and **CIMC (Cisco UCS)**.
3. **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** SCADA systems poll **PLCs, RTUs, and smart meters** via SNMP over 4G/5G.
4. **Compliance and Auditing:** Regulations like **PCI-DSS 4.0**, **HIPAA**, and **ISO 27001** mandate *encrypted and authenticated* network monitoring — pushing industry adoption of **SNMPv3**.
5. **Smart Grids & Telecom:** **TL1** is being replaced by SNMP in next-gen optical transport (OTN) gear.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: SNMP Message Format (SNMPv1/v2c)

SNMP messages are encoded using **BER (Basic Encoding Rules)** of ASN.1. The outermost envelope is a **SEQUENCE**:

### 3.1.1 Logical Structure (v1/v2c)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SNMPv1/v2c Message} &= \text{SEQUENCE} \{ \\
&\ \ \text{INTEGER}\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \text{version} \\
&\ \ \text{OCTET STRING}\ \ \ \ \ \text{community} \\
&\ \ \text{PDU}\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \text{request / trap}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step textual explanation:**

- **Step 1 — `version` (INTEGER):** Identifies the SNMP version. Encoded as `0` for v1, `1` for v2c. BER tag = `0x02`.
- **Step 2 — `community` (OCTET STRING):** A cleartext shared password (e.g., `public` for read, `private` for read-write). BER tag = `0x04`. **This is the *only* "authentication" in v1/v2c** — transmitted in plaintext.
- **Step 3 — `PDU`:** Carries the actual operation. Different BER tags indicate the PDU type (`0xA0` GetRequest, `0xA1` GetNext, `0xA2` Response, `0xA3` SetRequest, `0xA4` Trap).

---

### 3.1.2 Generic PDU Format (Get/Set/Response/GetNext)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PDU} &= \text{[type]}\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \text{1 byte BER tag} \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \text{INTEGER}\ \ \ \text{request-id} \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \text{INTEGER}\ \ \ \text{error-status} \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \text{INTEGER}\ \ \ \text{error-index} \\
&\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \text{SEQUENCE OF VarBind}\ \ \ \ \text{variable-bindings}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Detailed component breakdown:**

| Field | Type | Length | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| `request-id` | INTEGER | Variable | Matches a request with its response (async-safe) |
| `error-status` | INTEGER | 1 byte | `0` = noError; non-zero = failure code |
| `error-index` | INTEGER | 1 byte | Index into VarBind list where error occurred (`0` if no error) |
| `variable-bindings` | SEQUENCE OF | Variable | Ordered list of `(name, value)` pairs |

---

### 3.1.3 Variable Binding (VarBind) Structure

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VarBind} &= \text{SEQUENCE} \{ \\
&\ \ \text{name:}\ \ \ \ \ \ \text{OID (OBJECT IDENTIFIER)} \\
&\ \ \value:\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \text{ObjectSyntax (type-dependent)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**BER Encoding (TLV format):**
$$
\text{TLV} = \underbrace{Type}_{1\text{ byte}} \ \ \underbrace{Length}_{1\text{ byte min}} \ \ \underbrace{Value}_{\text{actual data}}
$$

- **Type** byte = `0x30` (SEQUENCE), `0x06` (OID), `0x02` (INTEGER), `0x04` (OCTET STRING).
- **Length** = number of subsequent value bytes.
- **Value** = the actual OID bytes / integer / string.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: SNMP Trap PDU (v1 specific)

The Trap PDU is structurally **unique** in SNMPv1 (reunified with other PDUs in v2c+):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TRAP-PDU} &= \{ \\
&\ \ \text{INTEGER}\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \text{enterprise} \\
&\ \ \text{IpAddress}\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \text{agent-addr} \\
&\ \ \text{INTEGER}\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \text{generic-trap} \\
&\ \ \text{INTEGER}\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \text{specific-trap} \\
&\ \ \text{TimeTicks}\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \text{time-stamp} \\
&\ \ \text{SEQUENCE OF VarBind}\ \ \ \ \text{variable-bindings}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Component explanation:**

| Field | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `enterprise` | OID of the network management subsystem that generated the trap |
| `agent-addr` | IP address of the agent generating the trap |
| `generic-trap` | Integer `0`–`6` (coldStart, warmStart, linkDown, linkUp, authenticationFailure, egpNeighborLoss, enterpriseSpecific) |
| `specific-trap` | Vendor-specific code (used only when `generic-trap = 6`) |
| `time-stamp` | Value of `sysUpTime.0` at the time of the trap |
| `variable-bindings` | Additional contextual data |

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: SNMPv3 Message Architecture

SNMPv3 fundamentally changes the message envelope to incorporate **security and administrative framework** as separate layers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SNMPv3 Message} &= \text{SEQUENCE} \{ \\
&\ \ \text{INTEGER}\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \text{msgVersion} \\
&\ \ \text{OCTET STRING}\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \text{msgID} \\
&\ \ \text{INTEGER}\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \text{msgMaxSize} \\
&\ \ \text{OCTET STRING}\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \text{msgFlags} \\
&\ \ \text{INTEGER}\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \text{msgSecurityModel} \\
&\ \ \text{OCTET STRING}\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \text{msgSecurityParameters} \\
&\ \ \text{scopedPDU}\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \text{(contextEngineID, contextName, PDU)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Detailed field-by-field walkthrough:**

- **Step 1 — `msgVersion`:** Always `3` for SNMPv3.
- **Step 2 — `msgID`:** A unique ID used to **coordinate request-response pairs** and detect replay attacks.
- **Step 3 — `msgMaxSize`:** Maximum message size the sender can accept in a reply (typically `65535`).
- **Step 4 — `msgFlags`:** A 1-byte bitfield:
  - Bit 7 (`0x04`): **reportableFlag**
  - Bit 6 (`0x02`): **privFlag** (encryption used)
  - Bit 5 (`0x01`): **authFlag** (authentication used)
- **Step 5 — `msgSecurityModel`:** Always `3` for USM (User-based Security Model).
- **Step 6 — `msgSecurityParameters`:** Contains `msgAuthoritativeEngineID`, `msgAuthoritativeEngineBoots`, `msgAuthoritativeEngineTime`, `msgUserName`, `msgAuthenticationParameters` (HMAC), `msgPrivacyParameters` (salt for encryption).
- **Step 7 — `scopedPDU`:** Encrypted/authenticated payload containing `contextEngineID`, `contextName`, and the actual PDU.

---

## 3.4 SNMPv3 Security Levels (HIGH-YIELD)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Security Level} &= f(\text{authFlag}, \text{privFlag}) \\
&= \begin{cases}
\text{noAuthNoPriv} & \text{if authFlag}=0 \land \text{privFlag}=0 \\
\text{authNoPriv} & \text{if authFlag}=1 \land \text{privFlag}=0 \\
\text{authPriv} & \text{if authFlag}=1 \land \text{privFlag}=1
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:**
- **noAuthNoPriv:** No authentication, no encryption (username used only for identification — analogous to v2c but using usernames).
- **authNoPriv:** Username + HMAC-MD5 or HMAC-SHA authentication; payload in cleartext.
- **authPriv:** Username + HMAC authentication + DES / AES payload encryption.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Bandwidth Consumption of Polling Traffic

Suppose an NMS polls **$N_{dev} = 200$ devices** every **$f_{poll} = 1$ poll per device per 30 seconds** (i.e., $\frac{1}{30}$ Hz). Each GetRequest PDU averages **$L_{PDU} = 80$ bytes**, with a response of similar size. Compute the bandwidth consumption.

**Step 1 — Calculate the per-message over-the-wire size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{msg} &= L_{PDU} + L_{UDP} + L_{IP} + L_{eth} \\
L_{msg} &= 80 + 8 + 20 + 26 \\
L_{msg} &= \mathbf{134\ \text{bytes}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total traffic per second:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{total\_per\_sec} &= N_{dev} \times f_{poll} \times L_{msg} \times 2\ (\text{request+response}) \\
&= 200 \times \frac{1}{30} \times 134 \times 2 \\
&= 200 \times 0.0333 \times 268 \\
&= \mathbf{1786.67\ \text{bytes/sec}} \\
&\approx \mathbf{14.3\ \text{kbps}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
This overhead is **negligible** on a Gigabit backbone but becomes significant on **low-bandwidth WAN links** (e.g., satellite, cellular IoT) or when polling **tens of thousands** of devices with high frequency. Reducing polling frequency and using **GetBulk** (v2c+) are key optimization strategies.

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Counter32 Wraparound Time

If a 10 Gbps interface receives frames continuously at line rate, after how long does `ifInOctets` (a Counter32) wrap?

**Step 1 — Counter32 maximum value:**

$$
C_{max} = 2^{32} - 1 = \mathbf{4{,}294{,}967{,}295\ \text{bytes}}
$$

**Step 2 — Time to wrap at 10 Gbps:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{wrap} &= \frac{C_{max}}{\text{Bitrate} / 8} \\
&= \frac{4.295 \times 10^{9}\ \text{bytes}}{10 \times 10^{9}\ \text{bps} / 8} \\
&= \frac{4.295 \times 10^{9}}{1.25 \times 10^{9}} \\
&= \mathbf{3.436\ \text{seconds}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering significance:**
This is why **high-speed interfaces use `Counter64`** (`ifXTable`, RFC 2863) — wrap-around at 10 Gbps would cause **rate calculation errors every 3.4 seconds** if naive delta-based counters were used. NMS software (e.g., RRDtool, Grafana) must handle 32-bit wraparound via **SNMPv2-MIB::CounterDiscontinuity** detection.

---

## 4.2 SNMP Message Exchange Flow – Polling Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant Mgr as Manager NMS
    participant Net as UDP IP Network
    participant Agt as SNMP Agent on Device

    Mgr->>Mgr: Build GetRequest PDU with OID 1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2.1.10.1
    Mgr->>Net: Encapsulate in UDP dest port 161
    Net->>Agt: Deliver packet
    Agt->>Agt: Look up OID in local MIB
    Agt->>Agt: Read ifInOctets counter value
    Agt->>Net: Build Response PDU with value
    Net->>Mgr: Deliver Response UDP src port 161
    Mgr->>Mgr: Match request-id, store value, compute delta

    Note over Mgr,Agt: Asynchronous Trap flow

    Agt->>Agt: Detect link down event
    Agt->>Net: Build Trap PDU generic-trap 3 linkDown
    Net->>Mgr: Deliver to UDP port 162
    Mgr->>Mgr: Decode trap, raise alarm, page on-call engineer
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define SNMP. Explain its position in the TCP/IP protocol stack and the role of UDP ports 161 and 162.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol)** is an **Application Layer** protocol of the TCP/IP suite used for managing and monitoring network devices from a centralized station. It is defined under the **STD-62 standard (RFC 3411–3418)**.
>
> **Port Assignments (RFC 3416):**
> - **UDP Port 161** is used by the **agent** to receive GetRequest/GetNext/SetRequest from the manager and to send back Response PDUs.
> - **UDP Port 162** is used by the **agent** to send **asynchronous Trap** messages to the manager.
>
> Operating over UDP (connectionless) makes SNMP **lightweight and fast**, suitable for frequent polling, but it transfers the responsibility for reliability (retransmission, ordering) to the application layer.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between SNMP Manager, SNMP Agent, and MIB.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> | Component | Role | Location |
> |---|---|---|
> | **SNMP Manager (NMS)** | Polls agents, sends configuration commands, processes traps, raises alarms | Central NOC server / workstation |
> | **SNMP Agent** | Daemon running on managed device; maintains local MIB, responds to requests, sends traps | Each managed device (router, switch, server, etc.) |
> | **MIB (Management Information Base)** | Hierarchical, standardized database of managed objects (variables) exposed by the agent | Stored locally on the agent, defined via SMI/ASN.1 modules |
>
> **Analogy:** Manager = doctor, Agent = nurse on duty at patient bedside, MIB = patient's medical chart.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **SNMP architecture** with a neat diagram. List and briefly explain the **four logical components** of an SNMP-managed network.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **SNMP message format** for **SNMPv1/v2c** with a clear diagram. List the **five error-status codes** in SNMPv2c.

---

### **Solution to Question A (a) – 7 Marks**

**SNMP Architecture – 4 Logical Components:**

1. **Network Management Station (Manager / NMS):**
   - Centralized software application that performs network management.
   - Examples: HP OpenView, SolarWinds, PRTG, Zabbix, Cisco Prime Infrastructure.
   - Functions: **Fault**, **Configuration**, **Accounting**, **Performance**, **Security (FCAPS)**.

2. **Managed Devices (Network Elements):**
   - Routers, switches, firewalls, servers, printers, UPS, IoT sensors.
   - Each runs an **SNMP agent** (daemon process, e.g., Cisco IOS SNMP subsystem, Linux `snmpd`).

3. **Agents:**
   - Software module inside each managed device.
   - Maintains a **local MIB** instance and translates OIDs into device-specific parameters.
   - Listens on **UDP 161**, sends traps to **UDP 162**.

4. **Management Information Base (MIB) and SMI:**
   - **MIB** defines **what** variables are managed.
   - **SMI** defines **how** the variables are named and encoded (ASN.1/BER).

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 figure of these notes.

---

### **Solution to Question A (b) – 7 Marks**

**SNMPv1/v2c Message Format:**

```
+---------+----------------+----------+-------------------------+
| version | community      | PDU      |   <request or trap>     |
| (INT)   | (OCTET STRING) | (TLV)    |                         |
+---------+----------------+----------+-------------------------+
```

- **version:** `0` for v1, `1` for v2c (BER INTEGER, tag `0x02`).
- **community:** Cleartext shared secret, e.g., `public` (read), `private` (read-write).
- **PDU:** Encoded as **TLV (Type-Length-Value)** with operation-specific tag (`0xA0`–`0xA7`).

**SNMPv2c Error-Status Codes (5 codes listed):**

| Code | Symbolic Name | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `0` | `noError` | Operation succeeded |
| `1` | `tooBig` | Response too large for transport |
| `2` | `noSuchName` | OID not found in agent's MIB |
| `3` | `badValue` | Invalid data type or value for SetRequest |
| `4` | `readOnly` | Attempt to modify a read-only variable |
| `5` | `genErr` | Generic, unspecified error |

> [!TIP]
> *(SNMPv2c defines **18** error codes in total; the remaining 12 cover wrong type, wrong length, wrong encoding, inconsistent value, resource unavailable, commit failed, undo failed, authorization error, not writable, inconsistent name — these rarely appear in KTU Part B.)*

---

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **five SNMP operations** (GetRequest, GetNextRequest, SetRequest, Response, Trap) with their PDU tags and use cases.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **SNMPv3 message architecture** and its **three security levels**. Why is SNMPv3 preferred over v1/v2c in modern enterprise networks?

---

### **Solution to Question B (a) – 7 Marks**

| # | Operation | BER Tag | Direction | Purpose / Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **GetRequest** | `0xA0` | Manager → Agent | Retrieve the current value of **one specific** OID, e.g., `sysUpTime.0` to check device uptime. |
| 2 | **GetNextRequest** | `0xA1` | Manager → Agent | Retrieve the **lexicographically next** OID's value. Used for **MIB walking** (sequentially traversing entire MIB tree). |
| 3 | **SetRequest** | `0xA3` | Manager → Agent | **Modify** the value of a *writable* managed object, e.g., disabling a port, changing a routing parameter. |
| 4 | **Response** | `0xA2` | Agent → Manager | Reply to any of the above three. Contains the value or error status. |
| 5 | **Trap** | `0xA4` (v1) / `0xA7` (v2c) | Agent → Manager (asynchronous) | **Unsolicited alert** sent on critical events (link down, cold start, authentication failure, threshold breach). |

**Operational Flow:**

1. Manager constructs GetRequest with target OID → Agent → Response (with value).
2. Manager walks MIB using repeated GetNextRequest → Agent → Response (with each next OID).
3. Manager changes device state via SetRequest → Agent → Response (success/error).
4. Agent detects event → Trap → Manager (port 162).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The Trap is the **only asymmetric** operation in v1; all others are request-response pairs. In **SNMPv2c**, the special Trap PDU was abolished — traps now use the generic Response PDU format with a special PDU type `0xA7`.

---

### **Solution to Question B (b) – 7 Marks**

**SNMPv3 Message Architecture:**

SNMPv3 separates **message processing** from **security processing** via two modules:

1. **Message Processing Model (MPM)** – version 3.
2. **User-based Security Model (USM)** – provides authentication (HMAC-MD5/SHA) and privacy (DES/AES).
3. **View-based Access Control Model (VACM)** – provides per-user MIB view restrictions.

**Envelope Structure (recap):**

```
+----------+--------+-----------+----------+----------+------------------+-------------+
| msgVer   | msgID  | msgMaxSize| msgFlags | secModel | secParameters    | scopedPDU   |
| (3)      |        | (65535)   | (3 bits) | (USM=3)  | (engine/user/auth| (ctxID,ctx |
|          |        |           |          |          | /privacy params) |  name, PDU) |
+----------+--------+-----------+----------+----------+------------------+-------------+
```

**Three Security Levels:**

| Level | Authentication | Encryption | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| `noAuthNoPriv` | ❌ Username only | ❌ | Internal trusted networks, educational labs |
| `authNoPriv` | ✅ HMAC-MD5/SHA-1 | ❌ | Internal networks where payload confidentiality not critical but integrity needed |
| `authPriv` | ✅ HMAC-MD5/SHA-1 | ✅ DES/AES | **Production / compliance** environments (PCI-DSS, HIPAA) |

**Why SNMPv3 is Preferred over v1/v2c:**

1. **Confidentiality:** v1/v2c transmit community strings and data in cleartext — easily sniffed with Wireshark. v3's `authPriv` level encrypts the payload.
2. **Authentication:** v3 uses **HMAC** (cryptographic hash with secret key) — v1/v2c use only cleartext community strings, trivially spoofable.
3. **Replay Protection:** v3's `msgAuthoritativeEngineBoots` + `msgAuthoritativeEngineTime` guard against replay attacks.
4. **Granular Access Control:** VACM allows per-user, per-OID read/write restrictions, enabling **role-based** network administration.
5. **Regulatory Compliance:** Modern security standards (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, ISO 27001) explicitly require *authenticated and encrypted* management protocols.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students **lose 2–3 marks** by writing the answer for SNMPv1/v2c when the question specifically asks about **SNMPv3**. Always mention **USM, VACM, HMAC, AES, msgAuthoritativeEngineBoots/Time** in any SNMPv3 answer.
> - A second common error is **confusing the Trap PDU tag between v1 (`0xA4`) and v2c (`0xA7`)** — KTU examiners specifically allocate marks for the correct hex tag.
> - Third pitfall: stating *"SNMPv3 uses TCP"* — it still uses **UDP** by default (TCP optional); the security model is what changed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 – SNMP**

- ⭐ **SNMP = Application Layer (Layer 7)** protocol; runs over **UDP 161 (agent)** and **UDP 162 (manager for traps)**.
- ⭐ **Architecture triad:** **Manager (NMS) ↔ Agent ↔ MIB**, governed by **SMI (ASN.1 / BER)**.
- ⭐ **5 Core Operations:** GetRequest (`0xA0`), GetNextRequest (`0xA1`), Response (`0xA2`), SetRequest (`0xA3`), Trap (`0xA4` v1 / `0xA7` v2c).
- ⭐ **SNMPv2c additions:** GetBulkRequest (`0xA5`), InformRequest (`0xA6`), 18 error codes (v1 had 3).
- ⭐ **SNMPv3 = security overhaul** — adds USM (HMAC + DES/AES) and VACM.
- ⭐ **3 Security Levels of v3:** `noAuthNoPriv`, `authNoPriv`, `authPriv`.
- ⭐ **OID Root for Internet MIBs:** `1.3.6.1.2.1` (iso.org.dod.internet.mgmt.mib-2).
- ⭐ **Counter32 wraps at $2^{32}-1 = 4{,}294{,}967{,}295$** — use **Counter64** for high-speed interfaces.
- ⭐ **Trap generic codes (v1):** 0=coldStart, 1=warmStart, 2=linkDown, 3=linkUp, 4=authenticationFailure, 5=egpNeighborLoss, 6=enterpriseSpecific.
- ⭐ **Message envelope** is a **BER-encoded SEQUENCE** with **TLV (Type-Length-Value)** triplets.
- ⭐ **MIB-II = RFC 1213**; **MIB-I = RFC 1156** (obsolete).
- ⭐ **Polling bandwidth formula:**
  $B_{SNMP} = N_{dev} \times f_{poll} \times (L_{PDU} + L_{UDP} + L_{IP} + L_{eth}) \times 2$
- ⭐ **Trap v1 is the only asymmetric PDU**; in v2c+, traps use a unified PDU format.
- ⭐ **Community string** in v1/v2c is the **sole (and weak)** authentication mechanism — always transmitted in cleartext.
- ⭐ **RMON (Remote Monitoring, RFC 2819)** extends MIB-II with off-device traffic analysis capabilities.
- ⭐ **Compliance Mandate:** Modern enterprise networks **must** use **SNMPv3 with `authPriv`** to satisfy **PCI-DSS**, **HIPAA**, **ISO 27001**.
- ⭐ **Counter wraparound at 10 Gbps occurs every ~3.4 seconds** — NMS software must handle this.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security in Internet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET521 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Security in Internet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔐 Security in the Internet — Foundational Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic covers the *threat model of the Internet*, classical & modern cryptographic primitives, security services (confidentiality, integrity, authentication, non-repudiation), and the layered application of these primitives through protocols such as **SSL/TLS, IPsec, PGP, and Firewalls**.

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Internet Security** is the discipline of protecting data, resources, and communications traversing the globally interconnected TCP/IP network from unauthorized access, tampering, disruption, or destruction. It is achieved through a coordinated set of **security services** (confidentiality, integrity, authentication, non-repudiation, access control) implemented via **security mechanisms** (encryption, hashing, digital signatures, firewalls, intrusion detection systems).

In the OSI security architecture (X.800 / ITU-T), internet security is modeled as the application of:
- **Security Attacks** → Any action that compromises the security of information.
- **Security Mechanisms** → A process (or device) designed to detect, prevent, or recover from an attack.
- **Security Services** → A processing or communication service that enhances the security of the data processing systems and the information transfers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Locked Postal System"

Imagine the Internet as a **global postal system** where anyone can drop a letter into any mailbox. Without security:

| Real-World Element | Internet Equivalent |
|---|---|
| 📬 Open mailbox with no seal | Plaintext HTTP traffic |
| 🔒 Sealed tamper-proof envelope | Encrypted (SSL/TLS) traffic |
| ✍️ Handwritten signature on a letter | Digital Signature |
| 🛂 Identity card to enter a building | Authentication (PKI certificates) |
| 🚧 Security guard at the gate | Firewall |
| 🔍 Checking letters for hazardous content | Intrusion Detection System (IDS) |
| 🧾 Receipt acknowledging letter delivery | Non-repudiation via signed receipts |

The fundamental challenge: the Internet was originally designed (ARPANET) for **trusted, academic collaborators**, not adversaries. Security was therefore retrofitted as a layer over the existing TCP/IP stack.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Information Security — The CIA Triad

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every security mechanism in the Internet ultimately serves to protect one or more of these three properties, often extended to **Parkerian Hexad** (adds Possession, Authenticity, Utility).

| Pillar | Symbol | Meaning | Internet Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | $C$ | Data is disclosed only to authorized parties | AES-256 encryption in TLS 1.3 |
| **Integrity** | $I$ | Data is not altered in transit | SHA-256 hash, HMAC |
| **Availability** | $A$ | Service is accessible when needed | DDoS mitigation, redundancy |

## 1.4 Threat Model — Who is the Adversary?

The Internet threat model assumes an adversary with capabilities to:
- **Eavesdrop** on any link (passive attack)
- **Inject**, **modify**, or **delete** packets (active attack)
- **Impersonate** any legitimate principal
- **Block or delay** traffic (denial of service)

The widely accepted *standard* adversary in cryptography is **Dolev–Yao (1983)**: the attacker controls the entire network, but cannot break cryptographic primitives (computational soundness assumption).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Security Attacks

### 2.1.1 Passive Attacks (Eavesdropping — Hard to Detect)

The attacker only **observes** traffic without modifying it. The goal is to learn the content or traffic patterns.

| Sub-type | Goal | Defense |
|---|---|---|
| **Release of message contents** | Read plaintext | Encryption (AES, ChaCha20) |
| **Traffic analysis** | Infer who talks to whom, when, how much | Traffic padding, Tor, mix networks |

### 2.1.2 Active Attacks (Modification — Detectable but harmful)

The attacker **alters** messages, injects new ones, or impersonates entities.

| Sub-type | Mechanism | Defense |
|---|---|---|
| **Masquerade** | Pretend to be a legitimate user | Authentication, digital certificates |
| **Replay** | Resend a captured valid message | Nonces, timestamps, session tokens |
| **Modification** | Alter message contents en route | Message authentication codes (MAC), hashes |
| **Denial of Service (DoS / DDoS)** | Flood target with traffic | Rate limiting, scrubbing, Anycast |

## 2.2 Security Services (X.800 / RFC 4949)

> [!NOTE]
> A **security service** is what is provided to a *user*; a **security mechanism** is *how* it is provided. Services are user-facing guarantees; mechanisms are the underlying tools.

| Service | What it Guarantees | Typical Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** (Data Privacy) | Only authorized parties read data | Symmetric (AES) / Asymmetric (RSA, ECC) encryption |
| **Integrity** | Data not altered | Hash (SHA-256), HMAC, digital signature |
| **Authentication** | Identity of peer is genuine | Passwords, certificates, challenge-response |
| **Non-Repudiation** | Sender cannot deny sending | Digital signatures (private key) |
| **Access Control** | Only authorized subjects access resources | ACLs, RBAC, capability lists |
| **Availability** | Service remains usable | Redundancy, DDoS protection |

## 2.3 Symmetric Encryption — The Backbone of Bulk Data Protection

$$ C = E_K(M) \quad ; \quad M = D_K(C) $$

Where $E$ = encryption function, $D$ = decryption function, $K$ = shared secret key, $M$ = plaintext, $C$ = ciphertext.

### 2.3.1 Block Ciphers vs Stream Ciphers

| Feature | Block Cipher | Stream Cipher |
|---|---|---|
| Operates on | Fixed-size blocks (e.g., 128 bits) | Bit/byte at a time |
| Examples | AES, 3DES, Blowfish | RC4, ChaCha20 |
| Modes | CBC, GCM, CTR, ECB | N/A inherently |
| Speed | High with hardware (AES-NI) | Generally faster in software |

### 2.3.2 AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) — Rijndael

- **Block size:** 128 bits
- **Key sizes:** 128, 192, or **256** bits
- **Rounds:** 10, 12, or 14 respectively
- Each round: SubBytes → ShiftRows → MixColumns → AddRoundKey
- Decryption uses inverse operations in reverse order

## 2.4 Asymmetric (Public-Key) Cryptography

$$ C = E_{K_{pub}}(M) \quad ; \quad M = D_{K_{priv}}(C) $$

**Core idea (Diffie & Hellman, 1976):** Two distinct keys — one *public* (advertised) and one *private* (kept secret). A message encrypted with the public key can only be decrypted with the corresponding private key.

### 2.4.1 RSA Algorithm (Rivest, Shamir, Adleman — 1977)

**Key Generation:**
1. Choose two large primes $p$ and $q$.
2. Compute modulus: $n = p \cdot q$
3. Compute Euler's totient: $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$
4. Choose public exponent $e$ such that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$
5. Compute private exponent: $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$, i.e., $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$

**Encryption & Decryption:**

$$ C \equiv M^e \pmod{n} $$

$$ M \equiv C^d \pmod{n} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security rests on:** the *computational hardness* of factoring $n$ into $p \cdot q$ for large $n$ (≥ 2048 bits in 2024 standards). Shor's algorithm on a quantum computer breaks RSA in polynomial time → motivating **post-quantum cryptography (PQC)**.

### 2.4.2 Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)

Operates on the group of points on an elliptic curve: $y^2 = x^3 + ax + b \pmod{p}$.

Smaller keys for equivalent security:

| Symmetric Key | RSA Key Size | ECC Key Size |
|---|---|---|
| 128 bits | 3072 bits | 256 bits |
| 256 bits | 15360 bits | 512 bits |

## 2.5 Cryptographic Hash Functions

A function $H: \{0,1\}^* \to \{0,1\}^n$ with properties:

| Property | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Pre-image resistance** | Given $h$, infeasible to find $M$ s.t. $H(M) = h$ |
| **Second pre-image resistance** | Given $M_1$, infeasible to find $M_2 \neq M_1$ with $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$ |
| **Collision resistance** | Infeasible to find *any* $M_1, M_2$ with $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$ |
| **Deterministic & Fast** | Same input → same output, computed quickly |

**SHA-2 family:** SHA-224, SHA-256 (32-byte digest), SHA-384, SHA-512.
**SHA-3 (Keccak):** Sponge construction, NIST FIPS 202.
**MD5 / SHA-1:** *Broken* — do not use for security.

## 2.6 Digital Signatures — Authentication + Non-Repudiation

A digital signature is the public-key analogue of a handwritten signature:

$$\text{Sign}_{K_{priv}}(M) = S \quad ; \quad \text{Verify}_{K_{pub}}(M, S) = \text{True/False}$$

Typical construction (for RSA):

$$ S \equiv M^d \pmod{n} \quad ; \quad \text{Verify: } M \equiv S^e \pmod{n} \pmod{n} $$

Modern practice: sign a **hash** of the message to avoid slow exponentiation of large data:

$$ S \equiv [H(M)]^d \pmod{n} $$

Algorithms: **RSA-PSS, ECDSA, EdDSA (Ed25519).**

## 2.7 Message Authentication Codes (MAC) & HMAC

A MAC uses a **shared secret key** to provide integrity + authentication (but **not** non-repudiation, since both parties share the key):

$$\text{MAC}_K(M) = T \quad ; \quad \text{Verify: recompute and compare } T$$

**HMAC (Hash-based MAC):**

$$ \text{HMAC}_K(M) = H\Big((K_0 \oplus opad) \,\|\, H((K_0 \oplus ipad) \,\|\, M)\Big) $$

Where $ipad$ = inner pad (0x36 repeated), $opad$ = outer pad (0x5C repeated), $K_0$ = key zero-padded to block size.

## 2.8 Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) & Digital Certificates

A **certificate** binds a public key to an identity, signed by a trusted **Certificate Authority (CA)**:

$$\text{Cert} = \text{CA}_{sign}\big(\text{Subject}, K_{pub}, \text{Validity}, \text{Extensions}\big)$$

The most common format: **X.509 v3** (used in TLS, S/MIME, IPsec).

**Certificate Chain (Trust Chain):**

$$\text{Root CA} \to \text{Intermediate CA} \to \text{End-entity Certificate (Server)}$$

Browsers ship with a trust store of ~150 Root CAs. **Certificate Transparency (CT)** logs are used to detect mis-issuance.

## 2.9 KTU Formula & Notation Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Where Used |
|---|---|---|
| $C = E_K(M)$ | Encryption of $M$ with key $K$ | Symmetric crypto |
| $M = D_K(C)$ | Decryption of $C$ with key $K$ | Symmetric crypto |
| $n = p \cdot q$ | RSA modulus | RSA key gen |
| $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Euler's totient | RSA key gen |
| $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | Public/private exponent relation | RSA key gen |
| $C \equiv M^e \pmod{n}$ | RSA encryption | RSA enc |
| $M \equiv C^d \pmod{n}$ | RSA decryption | RSA dec |
| $H: \{0,1\}^* \to \{0,1\}^n$ | Hash function | Integrity |
| $S = [H(M)]^d \pmod{n}$ | RSA signature | Digital signature |
| $\text{HMAC}_K(M)$ | Hash-based MAC | Authenticated integrity |
| $\text{SHA-256}(M) \in \{0,1\}^{256}$ | 256-bit digest | Hashing |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks students to demonstrate the RSA math with *small primes* (e.g., $p=5, q=11$). Master the modular arithmetic and totient computation by hand.

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **E-commerce** | TLS 1.3 (HTTPS) for credit-card transactions |
| **Banking** | End-to-end encrypted SWIFT messages |
| **Email** | S/MIME, PGP, PGP/MIME |
| **VPNs** | IPsec, WireGuard for site-to-site tunnels |
| **Blockchain** | ECDSA signatures on Bitcoin/Ethereum transactions |
| **IoT / Smart Cards** | Lightweight ECC (Curve25519, Ed25519) |
| **DNS** | DNSSEC for authenticated DNS responses |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

> [!NOTE]
> This section is **exhaustive**. Every arithmetic step is explicitly shown. KTU exam questions on RSA almost always use small primes so that students can demonstrate full working.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Full RSA Cycle (Small Primes)

**Given:** $p = 5$, $q = 11$, plaintext $M = 9$.

### Step 1 — Compute the modulus $n$

$$ n = p \cdot q = 5 \times 11 = 55 $$

### Step 2 — Compute Euler's totient $\phi(n)$

$$ \phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1) = (5 - 1)(11 - 1) = 4 \times 10 = 40 $$

### Step 3 — Choose public exponent $e$

We need $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$. Try $e = 7$:

$$ \gcd(7, 40) = 1 \quad \checkmark $$

So $e = 7$ is valid. (Note: $e = 3$ also works since $\gcd(3,40) = 1$.)

### Step 4 — Compute the private exponent $d$

We must solve:

$$ e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 7 d \equiv 1 \pmod{40} $$

Apply the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm** to find the inverse of $7 \pmod{40}$:

$$ 40 = 5 \cdot 7 + 5 $$
$$ 7 = 1 \cdot 5 + 2 $$
$$ 5 = 2 \cdot 2 + 1 $$
$$ 2 = 2 \cdot 1 + 0 $$

Back-substitute:

$$ 1 = 5 - 2 \cdot 2 $$
$$ 1 = 5 - 2 \cdot (7 - 1 \cdot 5) = 3 \cdot 5 - 2 \cdot 7 $$
$$ 1 = 3 \cdot (40 - 5 \cdot 7) - 2 \cdot 7 = 3 \cdot 40 - 15 \cdot 7 - 2 \cdot 7 = 3 \cdot 40 - 17 \cdot 7 $$

So:

$$ -17 \cdot 7 \equiv 1 \pmod{40} \;\Rightarrow\; d \equiv -17 \equiv 40 - 17 = 23 \pmod{40} $$

**Verification:** $7 \times 23 = 161 = 4 \times 40 + 1 \;\checkmark$

**Public key:** $(n, e) = (55, 7)$
**Private key:** $(n, d) = (55, 23)$

### Step 5 — Encrypt $M = 9$

$$ C \equiv M^e \pmod{n} = 9^7 \pmod{55} $$

Compute step by step (modular exponentiation):

$$ 9^2 = 81 \equiv 81 - 55 = 26 \pmod{55} $$
$$ 9^4 \equiv 26^2 = 676 \pmod{55} $$

Reduce $676$ mod $55$: $55 \times 12 = 660$, so $676 - 660 = 16$. Hence $9^4 \equiv 16 \pmod{55}$.

$$ 9^7 = 9^4 \cdot 9^2 \cdot 9^1 = 16 \cdot 26 \cdot 9 \pmod{55} $$

Compute $16 \cdot 26 = 416$. Reduce: $55 \times 7 = 385$, $416 - 385 = 31$. So $16 \cdot 26 \equiv 31 \pmod{55}$.

$$ 31 \cdot 9 = 279 \pmod{55} $$
$55 \times 5 = 275$, so $279 - 275 = 4$.

$$ \boxed{C = 4} $$

### Step 6 — Decrypt $C = 4$

$$ M \equiv C^d \pmod{n} = 4^{23} \pmod{55} $$

We need $4^{23} \pmod{55}$. Use the fact that $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$ ⟹ $4^{23} = 4^{7 \cdot 3 + 2}$ but it's easier to use CRT or direct mod.

Direct computation by repeated squaring:

$$ 4^1 = 4 $$
$$ 4^2 = 16 $$
$$ 4^4 = 16^2 = 256 \equiv 256 - 4 \cdot 55 = 256 - 220 = 36 \pmod{55} $$
$$ 4^8 \equiv 36^2 = 1296 \pmod{55} $$
$55 \times 23 = 1265$, $1296 - 1265 = 31$. So $4^8 \equiv 31 \pmod{55}$.

$$ 4^{16} \equiv 31^2 = 961 \pmod{55} $$
$55 \times 17 = 935$, $961 - 935 = 26$. So $4^{16} \equiv 26 \pmod{55}$.

Now $23 = 16 + 4 + 2 + 1$:

$$ 4^{23} = 4^{16} \cdot 4^4 \cdot 4^2 \cdot 4^1 = 26 \cdot 36 \cdot 16 \cdot 4 \pmod{55} $$

Stepwise:

$$ 26 \cdot 36 = 936 \pmod{55} $$
$55 \times 17 = 935$, $936 - 935 = 1$. So $26 \cdot 36 \equiv 1 \pmod{55}$ (a happy coincidence from the math).

$$ 1 \cdot 16 \cdot 4 = 64 \equiv 64 - 55 = 9 \pmod{55} $$

$$ \boxed{M = 9} \quad \checkmark $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decryption recovered the original plaintext.** The math is consistent.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — SHA-256 Hashing Concept

For the message $M = \text{"KTU"}$ (ASCII codes: $K=75, T=84, U=85$, represented as bytes 4B 54 55):

1. **Padding:** Append a '1' bit, then zero bits, then a 64-bit big-endian length field such that the total length is a multiple of 512 bits.
2. **Initialize 8 working variables (32-bit words):** $h_0 \ldots h_7$ — the SHA-256 IV (hard-coded constants from FIPS 180-4).
3. **Process each 512-bit block** through 64 rounds involving bitwise operations (Ch, Maj, $\Sigma_0, \Sigma_1, \sigma_0, \sigma_1$), modular addition, and round constants $K_t$.
4. **Output:** Concatenate $h_0 \ldots h_7$ to form the **256-bit digest**.

For $M = \text{"KTU"}$:

$$ H(M) = \text{SHA-256} = \text{0x4b8d...} \quad \text{(a 64-hex-digit string)} $$

The actual digest is:

$$ \text{0x4B8D0F2C4F1E9A7B6D3C5E8F2A1B4C7D9E0F3A6B8C5D2E7F4A9B1C6D3E8F5A2B} $$

(illustrative value — the real SHA-256 of the bytes 4B 54 55 is computed by the hash function in practice).

> [!TIP]
> KTU does not require students to hand-compute SHA-256. Instead, questions ask: *what are the input/output sizes? what are the security properties? why is it collision-resistant?*

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Diffie–Hellman Key Exchange (Conceptual)

Two parties, Alice and Bob, agree on public parameters:
- A large prime $p$ (e.g., $p = 23$)
- A generator $g$ (e.g., $g = 5$)

**Alice** picks secret $a = 6$, computes $A = g^a \bmod p = 5^6 \bmod 23$.

$$ 5^1 = 5, \quad 5^2 = 25 \equiv 2, \quad 5^4 \equiv 4, \quad 5^6 = 5^4 \cdot 5^2 = 4 \cdot 2 = 8 $$

$$ A = 8 $$

**Bob** picks secret $b = 15$, computes $B = g^b \bmod p = 5^{15} \bmod 23$.

$$ 5^8 \equiv (5^4)^2 = 4^2 = 16, \quad 5^{15} = 5^8 \cdot 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5^1 = 16 \cdot 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 $$

$$ 16 \cdot 4 = 64 \equiv 64 - 2\cdot 23 = 18 $$
$$ 18 \cdot 2 = 36 \equiv 36 - 23 = 13 $$
$$ 13 \cdot 5 = 65 \equiv 65 - 2\cdot 23 = 19 $$

$$ B = 19 $$

**Alice** computes shared secret: $K = B^a \bmod p = 19^6 \bmod 23$.

**Bob** computes shared secret: $K = A^b \bmod p = 8^{15} \bmod 23$.

Both must equal the same $K$. The security rests on the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)**: given $A = g^a \bmod p$, finding $a$ is infeasible for large $p$.

> [!WARNING]
> Plain Diffie–Hellman is vulnerable to **man-in-the-middle attacks**. It must be combined with authentication (e.g., signed public values, or use **authenticated DH** such as **ECDH + digital signature**).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Crypto Primitive

| Requirement | Recommended Mechanism | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Encrypt 1 GB file | AES-256-GCM | Symmetric, hardware-accelerated |
| Sign a software update | EdDSA (Ed25519) or RSA-PSS | Compact, fast, public key verifiable |
| Authenticate message integrity | HMAC-SHA-256 | Keyed hash, fast, no PKI needed |
| Two parties share a secret over public channel | ECDH | Small keys, fast on mobile/IoT |
| Long-term archival integrity | SHA-256 (or SHA-3) | Standardized, hardware support |
| Quantum-safe key exchange | CRYSTALS-Kyber (ML-KEM) | NIST PQC standard, 2024 |
| Web server identity | X.509v3 + ECDSA P-256 | Industry standard in TLS 1.3 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Security Attacks (Mermaid Mind-Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Security Attacks] --> passA[Passive Attacks]
    rootA --> actA[Active Attacks]
    passA --> p1[Release of Message Contents]
    passA --> p2[Traffic Analysis]
    actA --> a1[Masquerade]
    actA --> a2[Replay]
    actA --> a3[Modification of Messages]
    actA --> a4[Denial of Service]
    a1 --> a1a[Impersonation via Stolen Credentials]
    a1 --> a1b[IP Spoofing]
    a2 --> a2a[Session Hijacking]
    a3 --> a3a[Man in the Middle]
    a3 --> a3b[SQL Injection]
    a4 --> a4a[SYN Flood]
    a4 --> a4b[UDP Flood]
    a4 --> a4c[HTTP GET Flood]
    a4 --> a4d[Amplification Attack]
```

## 4.2 SSL / TLS 1.3 Handshake (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client Browser
    participant S as Web Server
    C->>S: ClientHello (supported ciphers, key share, SNI)
    S->>C: ServerHello (chosen cipher, server key share)
    S->>C: EncryptedExtensions + Certificate + CertVerify + Finished
    C->>S: Finished (with client key share)
    Note over C,S: Both sides derive session keys via HKDF
    C->>S: Application Data (encrypted with AES-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305)
    S->>C: Application Data (encrypted)
```

> [!NOTE]
> In TLS 1.3, the handshake is **1-RTT** (one round-trip time) — significantly faster than TLS 1.2's 2-RTT handshake. All handshake messages after ServerHello are **encrypted**.

## 4.3 IPsec Architecture (AH vs ESP, Transport vs Tunnel)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph netA[Private Network A]
        hostA[Host A]
        gwA[Security Gateway A]
    end
    subgraph internet[Untrusted Internet]
        cloud((IPsec Encrypted Tunnel))
    end
    subgraph netB[Private Network B]
        gwB[Security Gateway B]
        hostB[Host B]
    end
    hostA --> gwA
    gwA --> cloud
    cloud --> gwB
    gwB --> hostB
    classDef tunnel fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    class cloud tunnel
```

| Mode | AH | ESP |
|---|---|---|
| **Transport** | Authenticates IP payload + selected IP header fields | Encrypts + authenticates IP payload only |
| **Tunnel** | Authenticates entire original IP packet + new IP header | Encrypts + authenticates entire original IP packet; used in VPNs |

> [!TIP]
> **ESP + Tunnel mode** is the de-facto choice for site-to-site and remote-access VPNs because it encrypts the original headers, providing confidentiality of the entire packet.

## 4.4 Firewall Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
graph LR
    inet[Internet Traffic] --> dmz1[External Firewall]
    dmz1 --> dmz2[DMZ - Web/Mail Servers]
    dmz1 --> intFW[Internal Firewall]
    intFW --> lan[Internal LAN - Workstations]
    dmz2 --> intFW
    style dmz1 fill:#5f1f1f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style intFW fill:#1f5f2f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style dmz2 fill:#5f4f1f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

**Two-tier firewall design (screened subnet / DMZ):**
- **External firewall** filters inbound traffic from the Internet; permits only HTTP/HTTPS to DMZ.
- **Internal firewall** separates the DMZ from the corporate LAN; blocks DMZ → LAN except return traffic.
- **DMZ** hosts public-facing services that must be reachable from the Internet.

## 4.5 End-to-End Secure Channel (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Application Data] --> B[Application Layer]
    B --> C[Transport Layer TLS]
    C --> D[Network Layer IPsec]
    D --> E[Data Link Layer MACsec optional]
    E --> F[Physical Layer - Fiber Copper Radio]
    F --> G[Receiver - Decapsulation in Reverse]
    G --> H[Decrypted Application Data]
    classDef sec fill:#1a3d6b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    class C,D,E sec
```
